Email & Async Channel Management
QUERY RESOLUTION & SELF-SERVICE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages all asynchronous customer communications — email, web forms, and messaging app threads — with AI triage, intelligent suggested responses, and automated resolution for routine requests.
  Email remains the highest-volume CS channel for complex issues — AI management dramatically improves response quality and speed.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Email Parsing & Intent Extraction: Reads inbound emails — extracts the primary issue, any attachments (photos, invoices, error screenshots), customer details, and urgency signals. Handles multi-issue emails and email thread context.

  Layer 2 — Attachment Analysis: Analyses email attachments — reads invoices, extracts order numbers from photos, reads error screenshots, and processes any supporting evidence the customer has attached.

  Layer 3 — Response Drafting: Drafts a complete, personalised email response for agent review — with the specific answer, relevant policy, and any required next steps. Agent reviews, modifies if needed, and sends in one click.

  Layer 4 — Thread Management: Tracks email thread continuity — ensures replies go to the same agent handling the thread, links new emails in a thread to the open ticket, and detects when a new email has started a new topic requiring re-classification.

  Layer 5 — SLA Compliance: Tracks email response SLAs — first response within 4 hours, resolution within 24 hours. Flags emails approaching breach. Enables auto-acknowledgement when agent response is not yet ready.

12
Formal Complaint Intake & Classification
COMPLAINT MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Distinguishes formal complaints from general queries — applies the formal complaint process, regulator-compliant acknowledgement, and root-cause-focused investigation — protecting the company from regulatory and reputational risk.
  Mishandling a complaint as a general query is a regulatory and legal risk — AI classification ensures every complaint gets the right process.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Complaint vs. Query Detection: Applies a multi-signal classifier to distinguish formal complaints (customer is dissatisfied and seeks remedy) from general service queries. Uses linguistic markers — 'complaint', 'unacceptable', 'never again', 'demand', 'disgusted' — and contextual signals.

  Layer 2 — Complaint Taxonomy: Classifies the complaint into the formal taxonomy — Product Quality, Service Failure, Billing & Payment, Staff Behaviour, Delivery/Logistics, Policy Unfairness, Discrimination, and Safety. Enables systematic root cause tracking.

  Layer 3 — Severity Assessment: Rates complaint severity — Critical (safety risk, regulatory, legal threat, media mention, VIP), High (significant financial impact, repeat customer, social media public post), Medium (standard complaint), Low (minor service miss).

  Layer 4 — Regulatory Identification: Identifies complaints that may trigger regulatory reporting obligations — consumer forum filings, sector regulator complaints (IRDAI, RBI, SEBI, FSSAI), or CCPA matters. Flags for legal review.

  Layer 5 — Complaint Acknowledgement: Sends a formal, regulator-compliant acknowledgement — within 24 hours, with case reference, assigned owner, and committed resolution timeline. Tone and content appropriate to severity.

13
Complaint Investigation & Root Cause Analysis
COMPLAINT MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Investigates every formal complaint — gathering evidence, identifying root cause, determining fault, and computing the appropriate remedy — so that the resolution is fair, defensible, and prevents recurrence.
  Complaints that are resolved without root cause analysis tend to recur — AI-powered investigation ensures systemic fixes, not just case closures.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Evidence Gathering: Collects all relevant evidence for the complaint — transaction records, delivery logs, call recordings or chat transcripts, agent notes, product batch records, and any customer-provided evidence (photos, videos, receipts).

  Layer 2 — Timeline Reconstruction: Reconstructs the full customer journey relevant to the complaint — what happened, in what sequence, and at what point things went wrong. Maps the failure point precisely.

  Layer 3 — Root Cause Classification: Classifies the root cause using the standard taxonomy — Process Failure (the process was not followed), Product Defect (product did not perform as specified), System Error (technology failed), Third-Party Failure (courier, partner), or Policy Failure (policy caused the poor outcome).

  Layer 4 — Fault Determination: Determines fault allocation — Company Fault (full), Shared Fault (contributory), Customer Fault, or Third-Party Fault. This drives the remedy quantum and recovery options.

  Layer 5 — Systemic Pattern Detection: Checks whether the same root cause is appearing across multiple complaints — if so, flags a systemic issue to operations, product, or process improvement team. Prevents the same failure from causing thousands of complaints.

14
Complaint Resolution & Remedy Management
COMPLAINT MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Determines and executes the appropriate remedy for every formal complaint — whether refund, replacement, repair, compensation, or apology — within the company's policy framework and the handler's delegated authority.
  Inconsistent remedy decisions are a major driver of complaint escalation and regulatory action — AI-assisted decisions ensure fairness and policy compliance.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Remedy Policy Engine: Reads the company's remedy policy — what remedies are available for which complaint types, what the standard and maximum remedy values are, and what requires manager approval.

  Layer 2 — Remedy Recommendation: Based on the investigation findings and fault determination, recommends the specific remedy — full refund, partial refund, replacement product, repair, voucher, goodwill gesture, or formal apology.

  Layer 3 — Proportionality Check: Validates that the remedy is proportionate — neither excessively generous (creating precedent risk) nor inadequate (risking escalation). Flags outliers for manager review.

  Layer 4 — Remedy Execution: Once approved, executes the remedy — processes the refund through the payment gateway, raises a replacement order, initiates the repair job, or sends the compensation voucher. Confirms execution to the customer.

  Layer 5 — Closure Communication: Sends the formal complaint resolution communication — what the investigation found, what remedy has been provided, and what steps the company is taking to prevent recurrence. Tone calibrated to severity and fault.

Escalation & Executive Complaint Management
COMPLAINT MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages high-severity complaints that escalate beyond the frontline — including social media viral complaints, consumer forum filings, media enquiries, regulatory complaints, and executive complaints — with dedicated, senior-level handling.
  A complaint that goes viral or reaches the regulator is a crisis — having a dedicated, AI-assisted escalation process contains damage and improves outcomes.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Escalation Trigger Detection: Detects all escalation triggers — customer requests manager, consumer forum filing received, social media post gaining traction (>100 engagements), media enquiry, regulator forwarding a complaint, or repeat contact after inadequate resolution.

  Layer 2 — Social Media Escalation Management: For social media complaints gaining traction, activates the crisis response protocol — response within 30 minutes, move to private channel for resolution, and prevent public back-and-forth.

  Layer 3 — Regulatory Complaint Response: For complaints forwarded by regulators (consumer forum, sector regulator), prepares a structured, legally reviewed response within the regulatory prescribed period.

  Layer 4 — VIP & High-Profile Escalation: For VIP customers, media personalities, or high-profile individuals, assigns a named senior CS manager as dedicated single point of contact. White-glove resolution process.

  Layer 5 — Learning from Escalations: After every escalation is resolved, conducts a structured post-mortem — could it have been resolved earlier? What was the trigger? What systemic fix would prevent future similar escalations?

16
Consumer Forum & Regulatory Complaint Management
COMPLAINT MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages all consumer forum cases (District Forum, State Commission, NCDRC), CCPA complaints, and sector regulator complaints — filing timely responses, managing hearing dates, and pursuing early settlement where appropriate.
  Consumer forum defaults result in ex-parte orders and penalties — systematic management eliminates this risk while improving settlement rates.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Forum Notice Intake: Receives and registers all consumer forum and regulatory notices. Classifies by forum level, claim amount, and subject matter. Sets response deadlines per the Consumer Protection Act and regulatory rules.

  Layer 2 — Case Merit Assessment: Assesses the merits of the complaint — factual basis, policy compliance, prior resolution attempts, and likelihood of adverse order. Determines the optimal strategy: contest, settle, or settle partially.

  Layer 3 — Written Statement Preparation: Prepares the written statement / reply — factual background, company's position, evidence (order records, communications, resolution offered), and legal grounds.

  Layer 4 — Hearing Date Management: Tracks all hearing dates across forums. Ensures company representative or external counsel is available. Manages adjournment requests where needed. Never allows a default.

  Layer 5 — Settlement Analysis: For cases where settlement is preferred, computes the settlement range — claim amount, legal costs to defend, time value, and precedent risk. Authorises settlement within defined parameters.

RR   Returns, Refunds & Warranty
Use Cases 17-21
Return Processing, Refund Management, Warranty Claims, Replacement Orders & Returns Fraud


17
Return Request Processing & Policy Enforcement
RETURNS, REFUNDS & WARRANTY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Processes all return requests — validating eligibility against policy, authorising approved returns, rejecting ineligible ones with clear explanation, and initiating pickup logistics — in real time without agent involvement for standard cases.
  Returns are high-volume, policy-intensive interactions — automation reduces cost while ensuring consistent policy application.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Return Eligibility Check: Validates the return request against all eligibility criteria — return window (e.g., 7/10/30 days from delivery), product condition (new, unopened, or damaged), reason code (defective, not as described, change of mind), and item category (non-returnable items).

  Layer 2 — Evidence Collection: For damage or defect claims, requests photographic evidence. Validates uploaded photos — checks that they show the correct product, that the damage is clearly visible, and that the product matches the order. Uses CV (computer vision) analysis.

  Layer 3 — Fraud Risk Scoring: Scores each return request for fraud risk — customer's return history (frequent returner), account age, product category (high-value electronics), and consistency between claimed reason and evidence.

  Layer 4 — Return Authorisation: For approved returns, generates a Return Merchandise Authorisation (RMA) with barcode, pickup scheduling (for physical products), and refund/replacement commitment with timeline.

  Layer 5 — Rejection Communication: For rejected returns, sends a clear, empathetic explanation — which policy criterion was not met, what options remain (e.g., warranty claim instead of return), and how to appeal.

18
Refund Processing & Payment Reconciliation
RETURNS, REFUNDS & WARRANTY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Processes refunds across all payment methods — UPI, card, netbanking, wallet, BNPL — computing the correct refund amount, initiating the payment reversal, tracking completion, and reconciling against the order and returns ledger.
  Refund delays are one of the top drivers of customer frustration and social media complaints — fast, accurate processing is a competitive differentiator.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Refund Amount Computation: Computes the correct refund amount — original payment less any applicable deductions (restocking fee if policy allows, partial refund for used product, or delivery charge non-refund). Applies payment method-specific rules.

  Layer 2 — Payment Method Routing: Routes the refund to the correct payment channel — UPI refund to original VPA, card refund to original card, wallet credit, BNPL reversal, or bank transfer for cash-on-delivery refunds.

  Layer 3 — Refund Initiation & Confirmation: Initiates the refund through the payment gateway / banking API. Captures the refund reference ID. Sends the customer an immediate notification with the refund amount, method, and expected credit timeline.

  Layer 4 — Refund Status Tracking: Monitors the refund journey — from initiation through processing to credit confirmation. Detects failed or stuck refunds (e.g., expired card, closed bank account) and triggers resolution.

  Layer 5 — Finance Reconciliation: Reconciles all refunds against the refund ledger — total refunds by day, payment method, and product category. Flags unreconciled refunds to Finance for investigation.

19
Warranty Claim Management
RETURNS, REFUNDS & WARRANTY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the complete warranty claim lifecycle — eligibility validation, defect assessment, repair vs. replacement decision, service centre routing, and claim closure — providing a seamless warranty experience for customers.
  Warranty claims are a trust-building moment — handled well, they create loyal customers; handled poorly, they generate viral complaints.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Warranty Eligibility Validation: Validates the warranty claim — product purchase date vs. warranty period, product registration status (if required), and whether the defect is covered (manufacturing defect vs. accidental damage vs. wear and tear).

  Layer 2 — Defect Assessment: Collects evidence of the defect — customer description, photos or videos. Classifies the defect type and severity. Determines whether the defect is consistent with a manufacturing issue (triggering full warranty) or user damage (out-of-warranty).

  Layer 3 — Repair vs. Replacement Decision: Based on defect type, product value, repair cost estimate, and customer preference, determines the optimal resolution — on-site repair, send-in repair, advance replacement, or refund.

  Layer 4 — Service Network Routing: Routes the claim to the appropriate service resource — nearest authorised service centre, in-home service technician, or pick-and-drop repair service. Books the appointment and confirms with the customer.

  Layer 5 — Claim Tracking & Closure: Tracks the repair or replacement through to delivery or service completion. Sends status updates to the customer. Confirms resolution satisfaction and captures post-warranty-claim CSAT.

20
Replacement & Exchange Order Management
RETURNS, REFUNDS & WARRANTY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages all replacement and exchange orders — creating the replacement order, coordinating with warehousing for product availability, arranging simultaneous pickup of the defective item, and tracking to delivery.
  Replacement management is operationally complex — without AI coordination, replacements get stuck between CS, warehouse, and logistics.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Replacement Order Creation: Creates the replacement order in the OMS (Order Management System) — same SKU, size, and variant as original, or upgraded variant if the original is out of stock. Links to the original order for tracking.

  Layer 2 — Inventory Availability Check: Checks replacement product availability — nearest fulfillment centre, expected replenishment date if out of stock. Offers the customer alternatives (different colour, upgraded model) if original is unavailable.

  Layer 3 — Simultaneous Return Coordination: Coordinates the replacement dispatch with defective product pickup — either simultaneous (advance replacement) or sequential (return first, then ship). Selects based on product value and customer history.

  Layer 4 — Priority Fulfilment: Flags replacement orders for expedited fulfilment — next-day or 2-day delivery rather than standard timelines. Ensures the replacement experience is better than the original order experience.

  Layer 5 — Replacement Delivery Confirmation: Tracks the replacement to delivery. Confirms receipt with the customer. Verifies the replacement product is satisfactory before closing the original complaint.

21
Returns Fraud Detection & Policy Abuse Prevention
RETURNS, REFUNDS & WARRANTY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Identifies and prevents returns fraud and policy abuse — serial returners, wardrobing, return of counterfeit items, and organised return fraud rings — while maintaining the experience for legitimate customers.
  Returns fraud costs Indian e-commerce companies an estimated 0.5-1% of GMV — AI detection provides significant financial protection.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Customer Return History Analysis: Builds a return behaviour profile for each customer — return rate vs. cohort baseline, return reason patterns, product categories returned, and time-since-purchase patterns.

  Layer 2 — Return Pattern Detection: Identifies suspicious return patterns — wardrobing (returning after use event), buy-return cycling (same item multiple times), systematic size abuse, and bulk return fraud.

  Layer 3 — Product Authenticity Check: Uses computer vision on return photos to identify return of counterfeit products — different product than ordered, tampered packaging, missing serial numbers, or visible quality differences.

  Layer 4 — Network Analysis: Detects organised fraud rings — multiple accounts with the same delivery address, phone, or payment method; accounts created just before high-value purchases; and coordinated bulk return activity.

  Layer 5 — Policy Enforcement Actions: Based on fraud risk score, applies graduated policy responses — enhanced verification for medium-risk, manual review for high-risk, account suspension for confirmed fraud, and policy exception denial for serial abusers.

Field Service Scheduling & Dispatch
FIELD SERVICE & ESCALATION


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Optimises field service scheduling — matching technician skills to job requirements, minimising travel time, predicting job duration, and dynamically re-routing when situations change — maximising first-visit resolution rate.
  Inefficient field service scheduling costs 30-40% in productivity — AI optimisation dramatically improves technician utilisation and customer satisfaction.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Job Requirement Analysis: Reads the service request — product type, reported fault, warranty or paid service, customer location, and any special requirements (requires senior technician, specific tools). Determines skill and time requirements.

  Layer 2 — Technician Skill Matching: Matches job requirements to available technicians — certified for the product, carries required tools and parts, language match for customer location, and within feasible travel distance.

  Layer 3 — Route & Time Optimisation: Optimises the daily schedule for each technician — sequencing jobs to minimise travel time, accounting for realistic job durations, traffic, and customer availability windows. Uses live traffic data.

  Layer 4 — Parts Pre-Staging: Based on the reported fault, predicts likely required spare parts. Checks technician van stock. Triggers parts collection from depot if van stock is insufficient — ensuring first-visit fix rate.

  Layer 5 — Dynamic Re-routing: Re-routes technicians in real time when situations change — job overrun, emergency priority job, cancelled appointment, or traffic disruption. Updates customer ETAs automatically.

High-Value Customer & VIP Service Management
FIELD SERVICE & ESCALATION


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Provides premium, differentiated service to the company's highest-value customers — identifying VIPs from the customer base, routing them to dedicated senior agents, applying enhanced SLAs, and proactively managing their service experience.
  VIP customers represent a disproportionate share of revenue — losing one due to a poor service experience is far more costly than the investment in premium service.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — VIP Identification: Identifies VIP customers from the customer master — based on lifetime value (top 5%), spend in last 12 months, strategic account designation, referral volume, or manual VIP flag. Segments into Gold, Platinum, and Diamond tiers.

  Layer 2 — Dedicated Routing: Routes all VIP contacts to a dedicated VIP service team — bypassing standard queues, with SLA targets of < 2 minutes first response and < 4 hours resolution for all tiers.

  Layer 3 — Named Relationship Manager: For Diamond tier customers, assigns a named relationship manager — the customer always knows who to contact. The RM receives a daily brief on the customer's recent interactions and account status.

  Layer 4 — Proactive Account Health Monitoring: Proactively monitors VIP customer account health — recent orders, delivery performance, contact history, NPS score trend, and any open issues. Triggers proactive outreach when health indicators decline.

  Layer 5 — VIP Recovery Programme: When a VIP customer has had a poor experience, activates the VIP recovery programme — senior management outreach, enhanced remedy, and a structured win-back plan.

24
Escalation Path Management
FIELD SERVICE & ESCALATION


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the complete escalation hierarchy — from frontline agent to team lead to CS manager to CS Director — ensuring every escalation follows a clear process, is handled at the right level, and is resolved without unnecessary delay.
  Poorly managed escalations damage customer trust and create avoidable costs — structured escalation paths improve outcomes at every level.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Escalation Trigger Monitoring: Monitors live conversations for escalation triggers — customer explicitly requests manager, agent marks case as stuck, issue exceeds agent authority, customer mentions regulator or legal action, or conversation sentiment hits critical threshold.

  Layer 2 — Level Determination: Determines the appropriate escalation level — not every escalation needs the CS Director. Level 1 (team lead): authority issues. Level 2 (CS Manager): policy exceptions, high-value remedies. Level 3 (CS Director): legal threat, media, VIP. Level 4 (C-suite): strategic accounts, regulatory.

  Layer 3 — Context Handoff: Prepares a complete escalation brief for the receiving manager — customer history, issue summary, what was tried and failed, what the customer wants, and recommended resolution.

  Layer 4 — Escalation SLA: Applies a strict SLA to each escalation level — L1: acknowledge within 15 min, resolve within 4 hrs. L2: acknowledge within 30 min, resolve within 24 hrs. L3: acknowledge within 15 min, resolve within 4 hrs (priority).

  Layer 5 — Escalation Prevention Learning: Analyses all escalations to identify whether they could have been resolved at frontline level with better tools, training, or authority. Feeds recommendations to training and policy teams.

25
Third-Party Service Partner Management
FIELD SERVICE & ESCALATION


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the performance of third-party service partners — delivery partners, installation contractors, repair network, and outsourced CS teams — ensuring they deliver the experience the company has promised customers.
  Third parties cause ~40% of customer complaints but companies take 100% of the customer relationship risk — structured performance management is essential.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Partner Performance Monitoring: Tracks key performance metrics for each third-party partner — delivery success rate, damage in transit rate, installation first-visit success, repair turnaround time, and outsourced CS CSAT score.

  Layer 2 — Customer Complaint Attribution: Attributes complaints to the responsible party — company fault vs. third-party fault. For third-party-attributed complaints, automatically triggers a claim against the partner's SLA.

  Layer 3 — SLA Penalty Computation: Computes SLA penalties against underperforming partners — as per the service agreement. Prepares the penalty deduction claim and submits it to Finance and the partner.

  Layer 4 — Partner Performance Reviews: Prepares quarterly performance review packs for each key partner — trends, root cause analysis, and improvement requirements. Escalates chronic underperformers for contract review.

  Layer 5 — Partner Risk Monitoring: Monitors partner financial health, compliance status, and capacity adequacy. Flags partners at risk of service disruption — enabling early contingency planning.

Use Cases 26-30
CSAT / NPS / CES Surveys, Customer Effort Analysis, Social Media Listening, Feedback Intelligence & CS Analytics


26
CSAT, NPS & CES Survey Management
VOICE OF CUSTOMER & ANALYTICS


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Deploys and analyses customer satisfaction, Net Promoter Score, and Customer Effort Score surveys across all touchpoints — providing real-time insight into customer experience quality and trends.
  Real-time CSAT/NPS data allows the CS team to catch declining satisfaction before it becomes churn — it is the early warning system for customer experience.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Survey Trigger & Deployment: Triggers CSAT surveys at the right moments — immediately after contact resolution (CSAT), 7 days after purchase (transactional NPS), and quarterly for relationship NPS. Selects channel (WhatsApp, email, in-app) based on customer preference.

  Layer 2 — Response Collection & Validation: Collects survey responses. Validates for completeness and detects fraudulent or incentivised responses (same IP, batch responses). Applies response rate optimisation — reminder at 48 hours for non-respondents.

  Layer 3 — Score Computation: Computes CSAT (% satisfied), NPS (Promoters% - Detractors%), and CES (average effort score). Segments by channel, product, agent, team, and customer tier. Tracks trend over time.

  Layer 4 — Verbatim Analysis: Applies NLP to open-text survey responses — extracts themes, sentiment, and specific improvement suggestions. Identifies the top 5 drivers of high scores and low scores.

  Layer 5 — Closed-Loop Follow-Up: For Detractors (NPS 0-6) and low CSAT (1-2 stars), triggers an automatic follow-up — acknowledges the negative experience, escalates to a senior agent, and offers to resolve the underlying issue.

Customer Effort Score & Journey Friction Analysis
VOICE OF CUSTOMER & ANALYTICS


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Analyses the customer effort required across every service journey — identifying high-friction points where customers have to work too hard to get resolution — and recommends specific changes to reduce effort.
  Customer Effort is the single best predictor of loyalty and churn — systematically reducing effort is the highest-ROI CS investment.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Journey Mapping: Maps all customer service journeys — from initial contact through resolution and closure. For each journey, identifies the steps, channels, and handoffs involved.

  Layer 2 — Effort Signal Collection: Collects effort signals at every touchpoint — CES survey scores, number of contacts to resolve an issue, number of transfers, time waiting, number of times customer had to re-explain, and self-service abandonment events.

  Layer 3 — High-Effort Journey Identification: Identifies the journeys with highest customer effort — computed from CES score, repeat contact rate, escalation rate, and complaint rate. Ranks by impact (volume × effort score).

  Layer 4 — Root Cause of Friction: For each high-effort journey, identifies the friction root cause — policy too complex, information unavailable, multiple handoffs required, or process not customer-centric.

  Layer 5 — Improvement Recommendation: Generates specific, actionable improvement recommendations for each high-effort journey — process change, policy simplification, KB article addition, or self-service feature.

Social Media Listening & Reputation Management
VOICE OF CUSTOMER & ANALYTICS


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Monitors all social media channels for brand mentions, product discussions, and emerging complaints — detecting issues before they go viral and enabling real-time brand reputation management.
  A social media crisis can damage brand value worth hundreds of crores in days — real-time listening and rapid response is the defence.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Social Listening: Monitors Twitter/X, Instagram, Facebook, YouTube, LinkedIn, Reddit, Google Reviews, and App Store reviews for all brand mentions — direct tags, indirect mentions, hashtags, and competitor comparisons.

  Layer 2 — Sentiment & Virality Scoring: Scores each mention for sentiment (positive/neutral/negative) and virality risk (engagement rate, follower count of poster, trending hashtag association). Prioritises by combined risk score.

  Layer 3 — Issue Theme Clustering: Clusters related mentions by theme — product defect mentions, pricing complaints, service failures, or PR crises. Identifies when a theme is reaching critical mass (potential viral event).

  Layer 4 — Response Routing & Drafting: Routes mentions to the appropriate team — CS team for individual complaints, PR for media mentions, Legal for defamatory content. Drafts platform-appropriate responses for CS team to review and post.

  Layer 5 — Crisis Detection & Escalation: Detects crisis signals — sudden spike in negative mentions, influencer post, news article pickup. Activates the crisis communication protocol and alerts senior management.

Customer Feedback Analytics & Product Intelligence
VOICE OF CUSTOMER & ANALYTICS


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Synthesises all customer feedback signals — surveys, complaint themes, chat transcripts, social media, and app reviews — into structured product and service intelligence that drives improvement decisions.
  Customer feedback is the richest source of product and service improvement data — but only if it is systematically captured, analysed, and acted upon.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Multi-Source Feedback Aggregation: Aggregates customer feedback from all sources — CSAT verbatim, complaint root causes, bot escalation reasons, social media comments, app store reviews, and support chat transcripts.

  Layer 2 — Theme Extraction & Quantification: Applies NLP to extract themes from all text sources. Quantifies each theme by frequency and sentiment severity. Identifies themes that are increasing in frequency (emerging issues).

  Layer 3 — Product vs. Service Attribution: Classifies each feedback theme as a Product issue (design, quality, features), Service issue (CS process, agent behaviour, policy), or Operational issue (delivery, packaging, logistics).

  Layer 4 — Product Intelligence Reporting: Packages product-attributed feedback into structured product intelligence reports — top 10 product complaints, feature gap requests, quality issues by batch or supplier, and competitive comparisons from reviews.

  Layer 5 — Feedback Loop Closure: Tracks which feedback themes have been actioned by Product, Operations, or CS — and whether the action has resulted in a measurable improvement in feedback scores for that theme.

CS Performance Analytics & Reporting
VOICE OF CUSTOMER & ANALYTICS


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Measures, analyses, and reports on all CS function performance metrics — agent productivity, team KPIs, SLA performance, channel metrics, and cost-to-serve — enabling data-driven management of the CS operation.
  CS leaders who manage by intuition manage badly — comprehensive analytics transforms CS into a measurable, improvable operation.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Real-Time Operations Dashboard: Provides a live view of CS operations — contacts in queue, agent availability, SLA compliance rate, AHT by channel, bot resolution rate, and CSAT rolling 7-day. Enables intraday management decisions.

  Layer 2 — Agent Performance Analytics: Measures individual agent performance — AHT, FCR rate, CSAT score, adherence to schedule, and quality score. Identifies top performers and underperformers for coaching.

  Layer 3 — SLA Performance Analysis: Analyses SLA performance — by channel, tier, team, and issue type. Identifies which segments are consistently breaching and root causes (volume spike, understaffing, or routing issue).

  Layer 4 — Cost-to-Serve Analysis: Computes cost per contact by channel and issue type — and cost per resolved ticket. Tracks trend. Identifies the most expensive contact types and the opportunities to reduce cost through automation or prevention.

  Layer 5 — Executive & Board Reporting: Prepares the monthly CS performance report for the CCO and CEO — contact volume trend, CSAT/NPS, SLA performance, cost-to-serve, and key improvements. Includes forward-looking volume forecast.

Use Cases 31-36
Quality Assurance, Agent Training, Workforce Management, Technology Management, Root Cause Improvement & Budget


31
Quality Assurance & Interaction Monitoring
CS OPERATIONS & QUALITY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Evaluates the quality of every agent interaction — using automated AI scoring supplemented by targeted human review — identifying coaching needs, compliance issues, and best practices.
  Quality monitoring at scale is impossible manually — AI QA covers 100% of interactions vs. the 2-5% manual sample, giving a true picture.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — 100% Interaction Coverage: Reads or listens to every agent interaction — chat transcripts, email responses, call recordings (post-speech-to-text). Applies the standard QA scorecard to each interaction automatically.

  Layer 2 — QA Scorecard Evaluation: Scores each interaction against the defined scorecard — greeting, problem identification, knowledge accuracy, empathy, solution quality, resolution confirmation, and compliance disclosures. Scores 0-100.

  Layer 3 — Compliance Monitoring: Checks every interaction for mandatory compliance elements — regulatory disclosures (for financial/insurance), data privacy statements, escalation rights disclosure, and complaint recording obligation.

  Layer 4 — Agent Coaching Identification: Identifies specific coaching needs per agent — not just a score, but the specific skill gap (e.g., Agent X consistently scores low on empathy, Agent Y has knowledge accuracy issues on Product Z).

  Layer 5 — Best Practice Extraction: Identifies interactions with scores above 95 — extracts what made them excellent (specific phrases, resolution approaches, de-escalation techniques) and packages them for team training.

32
Agent Training & Knowledge Development
CS OPERATIONS & QUALITY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Identifies CS team training needs, designs and delivers targeted learning — product knowledge, process skills, and soft skills — and tracks competency development to ensure every agent is equipped to resolve issues correctly.
  Undertrained agents are the root cause of poor FCR, low CSAT, and high escalation rates — systematic training directly improves all CS metrics.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Training Need Identification: Analyses QA scores, escalation drivers, and FCR data by agent to identify specific knowledge and skill gaps. Generates a prioritised training agenda per agent — not generic training, but gap-specific development.

  Layer 2 — Learning Content Curation: Curates training content for each identified gap — product knowledge modules, process walkthroughs, policy guides, and soft skills (empathy, de-escalation, active listening). Links to real interaction examples.

  Layer 3 — Personalised Learning Paths: Creates a personalised learning path for each agent — mixing mandatory content (product updates, compliance) with gap-specific content. Schedules micro-learning sessions during natural workflow gaps.

  Layer 4 — Onboarding Programme: Manages the new agent onboarding programme — structured 4-week curriculum combining product knowledge, process training, shadowing, buddy calls, and progressive live contact handling with QA monitoring.

  Layer 5 — Competency Assessment: Runs regular knowledge checks — product quizzes, scenario-based assessments, and simulation calls. Tracks competency development over time. Identifies agents who are not progressing despite training.

33
Workforce Management & Capacity Planning
CS OPERATIONS & QUALITY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Forecasts contact volumes, plans staffing levels, manages schedules, and optimises agent utilisation — ensuring the CS team is the right size, in the right place, at the right time, at the right cost.
  Workforce planning errors cost both ways — understaffing destroys customer experience, overstaffing wastes budget.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Volume Forecasting: Forecasts contact volume by channel, day, and half-hour interval — using historical patterns, known upcoming events (promotions, product launches, seasonal peaks), and leading indicators (order volume, delivery batches).

  Layer 2 — Staffing Requirement Computation: Converts volume forecast into staffing requirements — accounting for AHT by channel, occupancy targets, shrinkage (breaks, training, absence), and service level targets.

  Layer 3 — Schedule Building: Builds optimal agent schedules — matching staffing to the intraday demand curve, respecting legal requirements (working hours, breaks), agent preferences, and part-time/flexible arrangements.

  Layer 4 — Real-Time Adherence: Monitors agents' adherence to their scheduled activities in real time — are they on time, on the right channel, taking breaks at the right time? Alerts supervisors to adherence issues.

  Layer 5 — Intraday Reforecasting: Continuously updates the volume forecast through the day — if actual volume is higher or lower than forecast, triggers intraday staffing adjustments: overtime, voluntary early departure, or cross-skilling.

CS Technology & Tools Management
CS OPERATIONS & QUALITY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the CS technology stack — CRM, ticketing system, telephony/ACD, chatbot platform, QA tools, and WFM software — ensuring all systems are integrated, performant, and delivering the capabilities the team needs.
  CS technology failures directly impact customer experience and agent productivity — proactive management prevents avoidable incidents.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Systems Health Monitoring: Monitors the health of all CS technology systems in real time — uptime, response time, API error rates, and integration point health. Detects degradation before it becomes a customer-visible failure.

  Layer 2 — Integration Management: Manages all CS system integrations — CRM to OMS, telephony to CRM, chatbot to knowledge base. Monitors data flow, detects sync failures, and ensures customer data is consistent across systems.

  Layer 3 — Agent Experience Optimisation: Identifies friction in the agent desktop — multiple system switches per contact, slow search results, missing data fields. Recommends UX improvements that reduce AHT.

  Layer 4 — Technology Roadmap: Maintains the CS technology roadmap — mapping capability gaps to available technology solutions, prioritising investments by ROI, and managing the procurement and implementation pipeline.

  Layer 5 — Vendor & Licence Management: Manages technology vendor relationships — SLAs, licence costs, renewal dates, and performance reviews. Identifies opportunities for licence optimisation and vendor consolidation.

35
Complaint Root Cause & Process Improvement
CS OPERATIONS & QUALITY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Analyses complaint and contact data to identify systemic process failures, product issues, and policy gaps — and drives cross-functional improvement projects that eliminate the root causes of customer dissatisfaction.
  The best CS team is the one that uses its contact data to eliminate the need for contacts — root cause improvement is the highest-leverage CS activity.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Contact Root Cause Analysis: Analyses all contact and complaint data monthly — what are customers contacting about, what is the root cause, which product/process/policy is responsible, and is the frequency increasing?

  Layer 2 — Systemic Issue Identification: Identifies issues that are systemic rather than one-off — defined as the same root cause appearing in 5+ contacts in a month, or a 20% increase in a contact type over two consecutive months.

  Layer 3 — Cross-Functional Impact Assessment: Quantifies the impact of each systemic issue — contacts generated, customer satisfaction impact, cost-to-serve, and downstream revenue risk (churn likelihood for affected customers).

  Layer 4 — Improvement Project Initiation: Raises formal improvement projects for each high-impact systemic issue — with business case, responsible owner (Operations, Product, Policy), timeline, and success metric.

  Layer 5 — Impact Measurement: Tracks whether each improvement project has delivered the expected reduction in contact volume or improvement in CSAT for the targeted issue. Closes the loop between insight and action.

36
CS Budget Management & ROI Reporting
CS OPERATIONS & QUALITY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Plans and manages the CS department budget — headcount costs, technology, training, outsourcing, and facility costs — while measuring and communicating the ROI of CS investments to the CFO and CEO.
  CS departments that cannot demonstrate ROI are always the first cut — data-driven value measurement protects the function and justifies investment.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Budget Planning: Builds the annual CS budget — headcount (internal + outsourced), technology licences, training, quality tools, and facility costs. Benchmarks unit cost metrics against industry (cost per contact, cost per resolution).

  Layer 2 — Cost-Per-Contact Tracking: Tracks cost per contact by channel in real time — phone (most expensive), email (moderate), chat (lower), and bot (lowest). Identifies channel shift opportunities that reduce cost without sacrificing experience.

  Layer 3 — Technology ROI Measurement: Measures the ROI of each CS technology investment — bot platform (contacts deflected × cost per contact), QA tool (training cost reduction + CSAT improvement), and WFM (staffing efficiency gain).

  Layer 4 — Revenue Impact of CS: Quantifies the revenue impact of CS quality — retention value from customer recovery, upsell during service contacts, and NPS improvement correlated with revenue growth.

  Layer 5 — CFO & CEO Reporting: Prepares the monthly CS financial report — costs vs. budget, cost per contact trend, automation savings, revenue impact, and forward-looking investment case for planned technology.

01
SAP S/4HANA Real-Time Data Extraction & Synchronization
SAP S/4HANA Integration
WHAT THE INTEGRATION DOES
Establishes real-time bidirectional data synchronization with SAP S/4HANA using native SAP technologies, extracting financial data, master data, and transactional data while maintaining data integrity and system performance.
INTEGRATION LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — OData & CDS Views Integration: Leverages SAP Core Data Services (CDS) views to extract financial data in real-time using OData protocols. Creates custom CDS views for specific financial close data requirements. Implements delta extraction for incremental data changes.
Layer 2 — RFC & BAPI Communication: Utilizes Remote Function Calls (RFC) for synchronous data extraction and Business Application Programming Interfaces (BAPI) for standardized SAP transaction processing. Implements error handling and connection pooling for optimal performance.
Layer 3 — SAP Gateway REST API: Connects via SAP Gateway to expose SAP business logic as RESTful services. Implements OAuth 2.0 authentication and secure API calls. Manages API rate limits and implements retry logic for failed calls.
Layer 4 — Change Data Capture: Implements change data capture mechanisms using SAP change documents and modification logs. Identifies delta changes in financial data and triggers incremental sync. Maintains data consistency across systems.
Layer 5 — Performance Optimization: Implements parallel processing for large data volumes, uses background job scheduling for batch extractions, caches frequently accessed data, and implements connection pooling to minimize SAP system load.
INTEGRATION FLOW — STEP BY STEP

Financial data extracted via OData

02
SAP FICO Integration for GL & Subledger Data
SAP S/4HANA Integration
WHAT THE INTEGRATION DOES
Integrates deeply with SAP Financial Accounting (FI) and Controlling (CO) modules to extract general ledger balances, subledger details, cost center data, and profit center information for comprehensive financial close processing.
INTEGRATION LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — GL Account Extraction: Extracts GL account master data (chart of accounts, account descriptions, account types) and transactional data (document headers, line items, posting periods). Handles SAP special GL transactions and noted items.
Layer 2 — Subledger Integration: Extracts AR (customer master, open items, cleared items), AP (vendor master, invoices, payments), Asset Accounting (asset master, depreciation, transactions), and Bank Accounting (bank master, statements, reconciliation).
Layer 3 — Controlling Data Access: Accesses CO data including cost centers, profit centers, internal orders, cost allocations, and activity types. Extracts actual costs, plan costs, and variance data for management reporting.
Layer 4 — Document Flow Tracking: Traces complete document flow in SAP from originating document through clearing document. Maintains referential integrity across document chains. Links FI documents to MM, SD, and other module documents.
Layer 5 — Period Lock & Posting Period Management: Respects SAP posting period locks and period-end closing status. Validates transaction posting dates against open periods. Manages period-end adjustments and carryforward logic.

03
SAP Central Finance Hub Integration
SAP S/4HANA Integration
WHAT THE INTEGRATION DOES
Integrates with SAP Central Finance to consolidate financial data from multiple SAP and non-SAP source systems, enabling group-wide financial close and reporting from a single SAP S/4HANA Central Finance instance.
INTEGRATION LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Multi-Source System Connectivity: Connects to multiple source systems (SAP ECC, S/4HANA, non-SAP) feeding into Central Finance. Maintains source system mappings and handles source-specific data formats and structures.
Layer 2 — Universal Journal Data Access: Leverages SAP Universal Journal in Central Finance for real-time consolidated financial data. Accesses single source of financial truth with embedded analytics and flexible reporting dimensions.
Layer 3 — Mapping & Harmonization: Manages master data harmonization across source systems including chart of accounts mapping, cost center consolidation, profit center alignment, and GL account standardization.
Layer 4 — Real-Time Replication Monitoring: Monitors SLT (SAP Landscape Transformation) or ALE/IDoc replication status from source systems to Central Finance. Identifies replication delays, errors, or data quality issues impacting close.
Layer 5 — Parallel Accounting Support: Handles multiple accounting principles (local GAAP, IFRS, tax basis) in parallel through Central Finance leading ledger concept. Extracts data by accounting principle for specific reporting requirements.

04
SAP Group Reporting & BPC Integration
SAP S/4HANA Integration
WHAT THE INTEGRATION DOES
Integrates with SAP Group Reporting (for legal consolidation) and SAP BPC (Business Planning and Consolidation) for management consolidation, enabling automated consolidation, intercompany elimination, and statutory reporting.
INTEGRATION LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Group Reporting Extraction: Extracts legal consolidation data from SAP Group Reporting including trial balances by entity, intercompany transactions, elimination entries, and consolidation packages. Accesses standardized data upload templates.
Layer 2 — BPC Consolidation Model Access: Connects to SAP BPC consolidation models for management reporting hierarchies, custom dimensions, currency translation logic, and ownership structures. Extracts both input and calculated consolidation data.
Layer 3 — Intercompany Matching Support: Provides enhanced intercompany matching capabilities supplementing SAP native IC matching. Applies fuzzy logic and AI matching to resolve IC differences before consolidation processing.
Layer 4 — Consolidation Rule Extraction: Extracts consolidation business rules including elimination methods, minority interest calculations, equity method application, and purchase price allocation. Validates rule consistency across periods.
Layer 5 — Financial Statement Integration: Integrates with SAP financial statement versions and reporting structures. Extracts formatted financial statements, notes, and KPIs. Supports multiple reporting frameworks (IFRS, US GAAP, local GAAP).

SAP Posting Interface & Workflow Integration
SAP S/4HANA Integration
WHAT THE INTEGRATION DOES
Enables bidirectional integration allowing SequelString AI to post journal entries, adjustments, and other transactions back to SAP S/4HANA while integrating with SAP workflow for approvals and process orchestration.
INTEGRATION LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — BAPI Posting Framework: Uses standard SAP BAPIs (BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST, BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_REV_POST) for posting journal entries to SAP FI. Implements pre-posting validation, error handling, and posting rollback capabilities.
Layer 2 — Document Type & Configuration: Manages SAP document types, posting keys, and field status variants. Ensures posted documents comply with SAP configuration and validation rules. Handles customer-specific posting requirements.
Layer 3 — Batch Input Session Method: Implements batch input sessions for complex postings requiring custom screens or fields. Provides fallback method when BAPIs don't support specific scenarios. Manages session processing and error logs.
Layer 4 — SAP Workflow Integration: Integrates with SAP Business Workflow for approval routing of journal entries created in SequelString AI. Triggers SAP workflow tasks, monitors approval status, and updates based on SAP approval decisions.
Layer 5 — Posting Validation & Reconciliation: Validates successful posting by reading back posted documents from SAP. Reconciles posted amounts to intended amounts. Maintains complete audit trail linking external entries to SAP document numbers.

Oracle Cloud Financials API Integration
Oracle Cloud ERP Integration
WHAT THE INTEGRATION DOES
Integrates with Oracle Cloud Financials using REST and SOAP APIs to extract GL balances, subledger data, and master information while posting transactions back to Oracle Cloud for seamless financial close operations.
INTEGRATION LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — REST API Framework: Leverages Oracle Cloud REST APIs for financial data extraction including GL journal entries, balances, account hierarchies, and ledger metadata. Implements OAuth 2.0 authentication and token management.
Layer 2 — SOAP Web Services: Uses Oracle SOAP web services for complex operations and batch processing. Accesses financial statement data, period close status, and consolidation information through SOAP endpoints.
Layer 3 — GL Cloud Service Integration: Extracts data from GL Cloud Service including chart of accounts, accounting calendars, ledgers, balancing segment values, and journal entry details. Handles multi-ledger and multiple currency scenarios.
Layer 4 — Subledger Accounting (SLA): Integrates with Subledger Accounting to extract AR, AP, and FA accounting entries. Traces subledger entries to GL through accounting event linkage. Validates SLA rules and journal line details.
Layer 5 — Oracle Integration Cloud (OIC): Leverages Oracle Integration Cloud for complex integration scenarios, orchestration, and transformation. Implements OIC integration patterns for real-time and batch processing.

Oracle ARCS (Account Reconciliation) Integration
Oracle Cloud ERP Integration
WHAT THE INTEGRATION DOES
Integrates with Oracle Account Reconciliation Cloud Service (ARCS) to enhance reconciliation capabilities, import/export reconciliation data, and provide AI-powered matching beyond ARCS native features.
INTEGRATION LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — ARCS Data Import/Export: Imports GL balances, subledger data, and bank statements into ARCS format. Exports ARCS reconciliations, match results, and certification status for central reporting and analytics.
Layer 2 — Reconciliation Template Mapping: Maps Oracle ARCS reconciliation templates to SequelString AI matching algorithms. Enhances ARCS auto-match rules with AI-powered matching logic. Supplements ARCS with advanced fuzzy matching.
Layer 3 — Certification Workflow Integration: Integrates with ARCS certification workflow to track preparer completion, reviewer approval, and management certification. Synchronizes certification status across platforms.
Layer 4 — ARCS Analytics Enhancement: Supplements ARCS analytics with predictive insights on reconciliation cycle time, aging of unmatched items, and forecasted reconciliation completion dates.
Layer 5 — Transaction Matching Augmentation: Provides enhanced transaction matching capabilities including many-to-many matching, pattern-based matching, and historical learning that complement ARCS native matching.

Oracle Cloud ERP Integration
WHAT THE INTEGRATION DOES
Integrates with Oracle Financial Consolidation and Close Cloud Service (FCCS) for legal and management consolidation, enabling enhanced intercompany matching, elimination automation, and multi-GAAP reporting.
INTEGRATION LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — FCCS Application Integration: Connects to FCCS consolidation applications to extract entity hierarchies, consolidation rules, intercompany dimensions, and ownership structures. Validates FCCS metadata consistency.
Layer 2 — Data Load & Extract: Loads financial data into FCCS using data load rules and extracts consolidated results. Handles both manual data entry scenarios and automated data integration from source systems.
Layer 3 — Intercompany Elimination Enhancement: Supplements FCCS intercompany elimination with enhanced matching algorithms that resolve IC differences before consolidation. Provides root cause analysis for persistent IC variances.
Layer 4 — Multi-GAAP Scenario Management: Manages multiple GAAP scenarios in FCCS (IFRS, US GAAP, local GAAP, tax basis) with scenario-specific adjustments and eliminations. Provides comparative GAAP reporting.
Layer 5 — Consolidation Business Rules: Integrates with FCCS business rules for elimination, currency translation, minority interest, and equity calculations. Validates rule execution and provides detailed calculation drill-down.
INTEGRATION FLOW — STEP BY STEP
01
FCCS Connection Agent
FCCS application accessed
Consolidation metadata extracted
02
Data Load Agent
Financial data loaded to FCCS
Entity trial balances imported
03
IC Enhancement Agent
Enhanced IC matching performed
Variance resolution before consolidation
04
Multi-GAAP Agent
Multiple scenarios managed
GAAP-specific adjustments applied
05
Consolidation Agent
Business rules executed
Eliminations and translations processed
06
Extract Agent
Consolidated data extracted
Results and drill-down available
07
Reporting Agent
Consolidation package complete
Multi-GAAP statements generated

09
Oracle EPM Cloud Integration
Oracle Cloud ERP Integration
WHAT THE INTEGRATION DOES
Integrates with Oracle Enterprise Performance Management (EPM) Cloud including Planning, PBCS, and Analytics for comprehensive financial planning, budgeting, and performance management integration.
INTEGRATION LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — EPM Planning Integration: Connects to Oracle Planning Cloud for budget and forecast data extraction. Provides actual-vs-budget variance analysis integrating EPM Planning with financial close actuals.
Layer 2 — PBCS (Planning & Budgeting) Data: Extracts planning and budgeting data from PBCS applications including budgets by version, forecasts, driver-based plans, and what-if scenarios for comparative analysis.
Layer 3 — Actual vs. Plan Reporting: Combines actual financial results with EPM plans and budgets to provide integrated variance reporting, KPI tracking, and performance dashboards.
Layer 4 — Data Integration Management (DIM): Leverages Oracle Data Integration Management for ETL processes between source systems, EPM, and financial close platform. Orchestrates complex data flows.
Layer 5 — Smart View Integration: Provides Smart View-like Excel integration enabling users to access close data alongside EPM data in familiar Excel interface with drill-down and refresh capabilities.
INTEGRATION FLOW — STEP BY STEP
01
EPM Connection Agent
EPM Cloud applications accessed
Planning and PBCS connected
02
Budget Extraction Agent
Budget and forecast data extracted
Multiple versions and scenarios
03
Actual Integration Agent
Actuals combined with plans
Variance calculations performed
04
DIM Orchestration Agent
Data flows orchestrated
ETL processes managed
05
KPI Agent
Performance metrics calculated
Actual vs. plan KPIs generated
06
Smart View Agent
Excel integration provided
User-friendly data access
07
Reporting Agent
Integrated performance reports created
EPM and close data combined

10
Oracle FBDI & BI Publisher Integration
Oracle Cloud ERP Integration
WHAT THE INTEGRATION DOES
Leverages Oracle File-Based Data Import (FBDI) for bulk data loading and BI Publisher for advanced reporting, enabling efficient mass data operations and sophisticated financial report generation.
INTEGRATION LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — FBDI Template Management: Uses Oracle FBDI templates for bulk journal entry uploads, GL balances, budgets, and master data. Validates data against FBDI specifications before upload. Handles FBDI error processing.
Layer 2 — Bulk Data Import Process: Implements bulk data import using FBDI for scenarios requiring mass data loads (e.g., year-end adjustments, budget uploads, historical data migration). Monitors import status and validation errors.
Layer 3 — BI Publisher Report Integration: Integrates with Oracle BI Publisher to generate financial statements, management reports, consolidation packages, and variance analyses using BI Publisher report definitions.
Layer 4 — Data Model & Layout Access: Accesses BI Publisher data models to understand report structure and data sources. Generates reports programmatically using BI Publisher web services or scheduler.
Layer 5 — Custom Report Generation: Creates custom BI Publisher reports for financial close specific requirements including reconciliation reports, close status dashboards, and exception reports with drill-down capabilities.
INTEGRATION FLOW — STEP BY STEP
01
FBDI Template Agent
FBDI templates prepared
Data formatted per specifications
02
Data Validation Agent
Pre-upload validation performed
FBDI compliance checked
03
Bulk Import Agent
Mass data uploaded via FBDI
Large volumes processed efficiently
04
Error Handling Agent
FBDI errors processed
Validation failures resolved
05
BI Publisher Agent
Reports generated via BI Publisher
Financial statements created
06
Custom Report Agent
Close-specific reports generated
Drill-down reports created
07
Distribution Agent
Reports distributed to stakeholders
Multi-format output provided

AP   Accounts Payable
Use Cases 01 — 06
Intelligent Invoice Ingestion, Deduplication, Tax, Reconciliation & Working Capital Optimisation


01
Invoice Classification & Routing
ACCOUNTS PAYABLE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Reads incoming invoice from any channel (email, portal, EDI, scan) and classifies it by type, assigns the right approval workflow, and routes without human involvement.
  Handles PO-Based, Non-PO, Utility, SES, Credit Notes, Debit Notes, Proforma, and Advance invoices.
  Determines urgency (due date proximity), routes to the correct approver hierarchy, and tracks SLA.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Document Reading: OCR extracts sender, subject, attachment type, invoice number, date, and amount. NLP classifies document as invoice vs. statement vs. remittance vs. other.

  Layer 2 — PO Reference Detection: Searches for PO number in document body. If found and valid in ERP — classified PO-Based. If absent — triggers Non-PO routing.

  Layer 3 — Vendor Category Lookup: Queries vendor master for spend category (utilities, services, goods, labour). Maps to invoice type (Utility, SES, Goods, Professional Services).

  Layer 4 — Urgency Scoring: Reads due date, payment terms, and any discount windows. Scores urgency (High / Medium / Low) to prioritize queue position.

  Layer 5 — Workflow Assignment: Matches invoice type + amount + cost center to the approval matrix. Assigns approver hierarchy, SLA timer, and escalation path.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

Intake Agent
Email / portal / EDI / scan received
Attachment detected, document isolated, metadata extracted
02
Classification Agent
OCR + NLP document analysis
Invoice vs. non-invoice detected; document fields extracted
03
PO Lookup Agent
ERP API call for PO reference
PO validated or absence confirmed; invoice type assigned
04
Vendor Intel Agent
Vendor master query
Spend category, entity, cost centre retrieved
05
Urgency Agent
Due date and discount window analysis
Priority score computed; queue position assigned
06
Workflow Agent

Approver hierarchy assigned; SLA timer started; notification sent
07
ERP Registration Agent

Unique voucher number assigned; status tracking initiated

Auto-route to assigned approver workflow

< 1 min

5-10 min

Outcome:  Invoice classified and routed in under 60 seconds from receipt. Zero manual triage. SLA breach rate reduced by 85%.


02
Duplicate Invoice Detection
ACCOUNTS PAYABLE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Detects duplicate invoices before payment — including near-duplicates that exact-match rules miss (same amount, slightly different date or invoice number).
  Operates across all invoice types and channels. Catches intentional vendor fraud as well as inadvertent re-submissions.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Fingerprint Generation: Creates a multi-dimensional fingerprint per invoice: Vendor ID + Invoice Number + Amount + Invoice Date + PO Number. Stores fingerprint hash in deduplication index.

  Layer 2 — Exact Match Scan: Queries dedup index for identical fingerprint. Instant flag if found — highest confidence duplicate.

  Layer 3 — Fuzzy Match Analysis: For near-duplicates, computes similarity scores across fields. Invoice numbers differing by one character, amounts within 1% tolerance, dates within 7-day window — all flagged as suspicious.

  Layer 4 — Historical Pattern Analysis: Checks if this vendor has a history of re-submissions. Assigns a vendor fraud risk score. High-risk vendors require manual verification even for low-similarity matches.

  Layer 5 — Context Validation: Validates against GRN and delivery records. If goods/services were received only once, a second invoice for the same PO is definitively duplicate regardless of superficial differences.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

01
Intake Agent
New invoice received
Fingerprint generated; dedup index queried
02
Exact Match Agent
Identical fingerprint search
Match found = immediate hold + alert
03
Fuzzy Match Agent
Similarity scoring across 5 dimensions
Score computed; threshold applied
04
Vendor Risk Agent
Vendor re-submission history analysed
Fraud risk score assigned
05
GRN Validation Agent
Delivery records cross-checked
Goods receipt count vs invoice count compared
06
Decision Agent
Confidence score aggregated
Duplicate confirmed or cleared; action triggered
07
AP Controller Alert
Hold placed or clearance logged
Full reasoning chain sent to AP controller for duplicates

CONFIDENCE-BASED ROUTING

Confidence
Action
Human Effort
> 95% duplicate confidence
Invoice placed on hold; vendor notified; AP controller alerted
Zero
70-95%

3-5 min
< 70%
Flag for awareness; processing continues with monitor
1 min
Confirmed legitimate re-invoice
Previous invoice cancelled in ERP; new invoice processed
5 min

Outcome:  100% of invoices screened before payment. Duplicate payments eliminated. Estimated savings: 0.2-0.5% of total AP spend annually.


03
TDS Category & Rate Determination
ACCOUNTS PAYABLE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Autonomously assigns the correct TDS section (194C, 194J, 194I, 194H, 194D, etc.), rate, and threshold status for every vendor payment — eliminating manual lookup and computation.
  Handles PAN-linked rate variations, higher deduction for non-PAN vendors, and lower deduction certificates.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Payment Nature Classification: Reads invoice description and vendor category to classify the nature of payment — Works Contract, Professional Service, Rent, Commission, Insurance, Interest, etc.

  Layer 2 — Section Mapping: Maps payment nature to the correct TDS section using the Income Tax Act schedule. Handles overlapping categories (e.g., technical services can be 194C or 194J depending on context).

  Layer 3 — Vendor PAN Validation: Checks vendor PAN status in master data. Valid PAN = standard rate. Invalid/missing PAN = 20% higher deduction rate per Section 206AA.

  Layer 4 — Threshold Accumulation Check: Aggregates all payments to this vendor in the current FY. Checks against annual threshold (e.g., 194C: Rs.30,000 per transaction or Rs.1,00,000 cumulative). TDS triggered only when threshold crossed.

  Layer 5 — Lower Deduction Certificate: Checks if vendor has submitted Form 15G/15H or a lower deduction certificate under Section 197. Applies reduced rate or nil deduction accordingly.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

Invoice Agent
Invoice nature and vendor category extracted
Payment classification initiated
02
Section Mapping Agent
TDS section lookup against payment nature
Correct section identified (194C/194J/194I etc.)
03
PAN Validation Agent
Vendor PAN queried from master
Rate adjusted for missing PAN (20% if absent)
04
Threshold Agent
YTD payment accumulation computed for vendor
Threshold breach determined; TDS applicability confirmed
05
Certificate Agent
Form 15G / 15H / Section 197 certificate checked
Reduced rate applied if valid certificate exists
06
Computation Agent
Net TDS amount calculated
TDS deduction computed on base amount
07
ERP Posting Agent
TDS entry created in ledger
Journal posted; TDS payable account credited; challan data prepared

CONFIDENCE-BASED ROUTING

Confidence

Human Effort
> 92% confidence on section
Auto-deduct and post TDS journal entry

2 min

10 min

1 min

Outcome:  TDS applied correctly on 100% of eligible payments. Zero short-deduction penalties. TDS return data auto-populated.


04
Vendor Statement Reconciliation
ACCOUNTS PAYABLE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Matches vendor ledger balance in company books against the vendor's own statement — line by line — identifies timing differences vs. genuine discrepancies, and resolves or escalates.
  Runs automatically at period end or on demand.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Statement Ingestion: Reads vendor statement (email attachment, PDF, Excel) using OCR and structured extraction. Parses each line: invoice number, date, amount, running balance.

  Layer 2 — Ledger Pull: Fetches all vendor transactions from company AP ledger for the reconciliation period. Structures data in matching format.

  Layer 3 — Matching Logic: Performs waterfall matching — exact match first (invoice number + amount), then fuzzy match (amount within tolerance, date within window), then unmatched residuals.

  Layer 4 — Discrepancy Classification: Classifies each unmatched item — timing difference (invoice in transit), credit note not applied, payment not acknowledged, duplicate charge, or genuine dispute.

  Layer 5 — Resolution Routing: Timing differences auto-resolved. Genuine discrepancies trigger vendor communication draft. Disputes above threshold escalated to AP Manager.

Statement Agent

Line items extracted; running balance validated

Matched items cleared; reconciled balance updated

Classification assigned per unmatched line

Communication Agent

Email drafted with supporting evidence; routed for approval

Outcome:  Month-end vendor reconciliation completed in hours vs. days. Dispute rate reduced 60%. Working capital improved through faster resolution.


05
Early Payment Discount Identification
ACCOUNTS PAYABLE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Proactively identifies invoices eligible for early payment discounts, validates positive NPV, and triggers accelerated payment — capturing value that is routinely missed in manual AP.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Payment Terms Extraction: Reads discount terms from invoice (e.g., 2/10 net 30 = 2% discount if paid within 10 days). Parses structured terms and free-text descriptions.

  Layer 2 — NPV Computation: Calculates annualised return of taking the discount. Formula: (Discount% / (1 - Discount%)) x (365 / (Net Days - Discount Days)). Compares against company WACC or benchmark rate.

  Layer 3 — Cash Position Check: Queries treasury system for available cash balance and 7-day cash forecast. Confirms payment will not create a liquidity shortfall.

  Layer 4 — Vendor Relationship Factor: Checks if vendor is strategic (preferred supplier list). Strategic vendors may warrant accelerated payment even for marginal NPV.

  Layer 5 — Approval & Payment Trigger: For positive-NPV discounts with available cash, raises payment proposal to CFO/Treasurer. On approval, triggers accelerated payment in ERP.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

Vendor Priority Agent

Priority score assigned

06
GSTR-2B Reconciliation
ACCOUNTS PAYABLE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Matches the company purchase register against GSTR-2B data from the GST portal, identifies mismatches, quantifies ITC at risk, and communicates with vendors for missing/incorrect filings.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — GSTR-2B Download: Fetches GSTR-2B JSON/Excel from GSTN portal via API for the relevant period. Parses all supplier-wise credit entries.

  Layer 2 — Purchase Register Pull: Extracts all inward supply entries from company books for the same period — invoice-wise with GSTIN, amount, and GST components.

  Layer 3 — Multi-Level Matching: Matches GSTR-2B entries to purchase register — exact match (GSTIN + Invoice No + IGST/CGST/SGST amounts), then amount-only match for date/number variations.

  Layer 4 — Mismatch Classification: Classifies each mismatch — missing in GSTR-2B (vendor has not filed), amount difference (vendor filed incorrect amount), reversed in next period, GSTIN mismatch.

  Layer 5 — ITC Risk Quantification: Computes total ITC at risk from mismatches. Prioritises vendors by ITC value. Generates vendor-wise correction request with evidence.

AR   Accounts Receivable
Use Cases 07 — 11
Cash Application, Deduction Management, Collections, Credit & Dispute Resolution


07
Cash Application & Receipt Allocation
ACCOUNTS RECEIVABLE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Maps incoming bank receipts to open customer invoices autonomously — even when remittance advice is partial, missing, or contains short payments and deductions.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Bank Feed Ingestion: Reads bank statement (MT940, CSV, API feed) in real time. Extracts each credit entry: amount, value date, UTR, narration text.

  Layer 2 — Remittance Parsing: Checks for accompanying remittance advice (email, portal, EDI). Extracts invoice references if present. Falls back to amount-matching if remittance is absent.

  Layer 3 — Customer Identification: Maps receipt to customer using bank account number, UTR narration, or historical payment pattern. Handles multiple entities paying on behalf of one customer.

  Layer 4 — Invoice Matching: Matches receipt amount to open invoice combinations — exact match, FIFO allocation, or partial payment. Handles multi-invoice payments and short payments.

  Layer 5 — Deduction Classification: Short payments classified as valid deductions (agreed discount, claim) or unauthorized. Valid deductions auto-processed; unauthorized flagged for dispute.

08
Customer Deduction Classification
ACCOUNTS RECEIVABLE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  When a customer pays less than the invoice, the agent determines whether the short payment is a valid deduction or unauthorized — and routes to the correct resolution path.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Deduction Detection: Identifies short payment by comparing remittance / payment amount against open invoice total. Computes deduction amount per invoice.

  Layer 2 — Remittance Code Classification: Reads customer-provided deduction codes (pricing dispute, quality claim, freight allowance, early payment discount, promotional allowance) from remittance or email.

  Layer 3 — Contract & Policy Lookup: Checks customer contract for agreed deduction types and limits. Validates claimed deduction against contractual entitlements.

  Layer 4 — Historical Deduction Pattern: Reviews customer's past deduction history. Flags customers with unusual frequency or deduction types not previously claimed.

  Layer 5 — Validity Decision & Routing: Valid deductions auto-processed and credit note raised. Invalid deductions routed to sales / key account manager with evidence pack.

09
Dunning & Collections Prioritisation
ACCOUNTS RECEIVABLE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Scores overdue invoices by collection risk, autonomously drafts and sends right-tone communications per customer segment, and tracks response to escalate intelligently.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Aging Analysis: Scans AR ledger for all overdue invoices. Segments by aging bucket (0-30, 31-60, 61-90, 90+ days). Computes total exposure per customer.

  Layer 2 — Risk Scoring: Scores each customer on payment behaviour (historical DSO, broken promises, dispute frequency), amount at risk, credit limit utilisation, and industry sector risk.

  Layer 3 — Relationship Context: Checks customer strategic importance (top 20 customer list), ongoing sales opportunities, and recent complaint history before determining communication tone.

  Layer 4 — Communication Drafting: Generates dunning message calibrated to risk score and relationship context — from gentle reminder (low risk, valued customer) to formal demand (high risk, transactional).

  Layer 5 — Response Tracking & Escalation: Monitors responses. No reply within SLA triggers next-level escalation. Promises to pay logged and tracked against follow-up dates.

10
Credit Limit Assessment
ACCOUNTS RECEIVABLE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Reviews new customer credit applications or periodic reviews by analysing financials, payment behaviour, and market data — recommends credit limits without manual underwriting.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Application Data Extraction: Reads credit application form, company registration documents, audited financials, and bank references. Extracts key financial metrics: turnover, net worth, current ratio, debt levels.

  Layer 2 — Payment History Analysis: For existing customers, retrieves internal payment history — average DPD, dispute frequency, broken promises. For new customers, requests trade references.

  Layer 3 — External Credit Bureau Data: Queries CIBIL Commercial, Dun & Bradstreet, or Experian Business for credit score, payment rating, and any adverse filings.

  Layer 4 — Industry & Sector Risk: Applies sector risk overlay — distressed industries (real estate, telecom in certain periods) get haircut on credit limit recommendation.

  Layer 5 — Credit Limit Computation: Applies credit limit model — typically a multiple of monthly purchase volume constrained by customer net worth and sector risk. Recommends limit band with rationale.

11
Dispute Resolution Agent
ACCOUNTS RECEIVABLE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Receives customer dispute communications, classifies dispute type, retrieves all supporting documents, resolves straightforward cases autonomously, and routes complex disputes with a complete evidence pack.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Dispute Intake: Reads customer email, portal submission, or phone call transcript. Classifies dispute type: pricing error, quantity discrepancy, quality claim, duplicate billing, goods not received.

  Layer 2 — Evidence Retrieval: Pulls all relevant documents — original invoice, purchase order, delivery note, proof of delivery, quality inspection report, rate contract — automatically.

  Layer 3 — Validity Assessment: Compares customer claim against retrieved evidence. Pricing dispute: checks invoice rate vs. rate contract. Quantity dispute: checks delivery note vs. invoice quantity.

  Layer 4 — Resolution Path: Valid dispute — auto-raise credit note or corrected invoice. Invalid dispute — draft rebuttal with evidence. Ambiguous — route to KAM with full pack.

  Layer 5 — Communication & Closure: Drafts customer response — acknowledgement, resolution confirmation, or rebuttal. Tracks case to closure. Updates AR ledger on resolution.

Use Cases 12 — 17
GL Coding, Journal Intelligence, Accruals, Prepaids, Intercompany & Misposting


12
GL Code Allocation
GENERAL LEDGER


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Multi-layer AI reasoning assigns the correct General Ledger account code to every transaction — vendor payments, expense claims, bank receipts, and journal entries — with context-aware decision making.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Document Intelligence: NLP reads invoice/transaction description to classify spend intent — service, goods, capital, subscription, fee, reimbursement.

  Layer 2 — Vendor Master Intelligence: Retrieves vendor spend category, commodity code, and complete GL posting history. High-consistency vendors get high confidence signal.

  Layer 3 — Historical Learning Model: Queries 2-3 years of journal entry history. Computes probability distribution over GL codes for similar transactions.

  Layer 4 — HSN/SAC Cross-Validation: Validates GL assignment against GST taxonomy to ensure fiscal consistency and correct ITC treatment.

  Layer 5 — Policy Enforcement: Applies capex threshold, chart of accounts validation, blocked GL rules, and budget availability check before final assignment.

Vendor, description, amount, HSN/SAC extracted
02

Spend category and historical GL distribution retrieved
03
ML Model Agent
Historical transaction pattern queried
GL probability distribution computed
04
Tax Rule Agent
HSN/SAC taxonomy cross-checked
Hard GL constraints applied; ITC eligibility confirmed
05
Policy Agent
Capex threshold and COA validation
Policy constraints enforced; budget check performed
06
Confidence Agent
All layer signals aggregated
Composite confidence score and top GL candidates ranked
07
ERP/Exception Agent
Post or route based on score
Auto-post or exception workflow triggered

Human Effort

2-3 min
