Historical policy versions maintained

Training Alert Agent

07
Compliance Reporting Agent

Real-time compliance dashboard updated

05
Organizational Structure & Reporting Line Validation
CONTROL ENVIRONMENT & GOVERNANCE
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Validates that the organizational structure supports proper financial controls, ensures reporting lines maintain segregation of duties, and prevents conflicts of interest in approval chains and control oversight.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Org Structure Mapping: Maintains complete organizational hierarchy including reporting lines, dotted-line relationships, matrix structures, and temporary assignments.
Layer 2 — Control Assignment Validation: Ensures control owners and reviewers are not in the same reporting line, approval chains do not have gaps, and no single individual controls end-to-end processes.
Layer 3 — Conflict of Interest Detection: Identifies situations where family members, related parties, or individuals with financial interests are in approval chains or oversight roles.
Layer 4 — Reporting Line Segregation: Validates that finance, operations, and audit functions maintain independent reporting lines and that control testing is performed by individuals outside the process ownership.
Layer 5 — Structural Risk Assessment: Flags organizational changes (mergers, restructuring, downsizing) that may create control gaps or concentration of authority.
AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

Org Mapping Agent

Change Monitor Agent

RA Risk Assessment & Monitoring
Use Cases 06-10
Financial Risk Identification, Fraud Risk Detection, Third-Party Risk Management, IT General Controls & Change Management Risk
06
Financial Misstatement Risk Identification
RISK ASSESSMENT & MONITORING
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Continuously identifies and assesses risks of material misstatement in financial statements arising from errors, fraud, management override, or control failures across all significant account balances and transaction streams.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Risk Universe Mapping: Maintains a complete inventory of financial statement risks across revenue recognition, inventory valuation, accounts receivable, fixed assets, liabilities, accruals, and disclosures.
Layer 2 — Inherent Risk Assessment: Evaluates the likelihood and magnitude of misstatement before considering controls based on transaction volume, complexity, judgment involved, and prior history.
Layer 3 — Control Effectiveness Evaluation: Assesses how existing controls mitigate identified risks, calculates residual risk exposure, and identifies high-risk areas requiring additional controls.
Layer 4 — Fraud Risk Triangulation: Analyzes pressure (financial targets, incentives), opportunity (weak controls, override ability), and rationalization indicators to flag high fraud risk scenarios.
Layer 5 — Risk Heat Mapping: Creates dynamic risk heat maps showing areas of highest financial statement risk, prioritizes testing and monitoring resources, and triggers enhanced controls.
AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP
01
Risk Inventory Agent
Complete financial statement risks mapped
Account-level and assertion-level risks identified
02
Inherent Risk Agent
Risk likelihood and impact assessed
High-risk areas identified before controls
03
Control Coverage Agent
Control effectiveness against risks evaluated
Residual risk calculated
04
Fraud Risk Agent
Fraud triangle indicators analyzed
High fraud risk scenarios flagged
05
Heat Map Agent
Dynamic risk heat map generated
Testing priorities established
06
Monitoring Agent
High-risk areas continuously monitored
Real-time alerts on risk elevation
07
Reporting Agent
Risk assessment dashboard updated
Management and audit visibility maintained

07
Fraud Risk Detection & Prevention
RISK ASSESSMENT & MONITORING
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Detects potential fraud in financial transactions through pattern analysis, anomaly detection, and behavioral monitoring, enabling proactive fraud prevention rather than reactive detection.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Fraud Scheme Library: Maintains patterns of known fraud schemes including invoice manipulation, vendor fraud, payroll fraud, expense fraud, asset misappropriation, and financial statement fraud.
Layer 2 — Behavioral Analytics: Analyzes user behavior patterns to detect unusual activities such as after-hours access, excessive transaction reversals, frequent policy exceptions, or transaction pattern changes.
Layer 3 — Vendor & Customer Analysis: Identifies red flags in vendor/customer master data including duplicate addresses, suspicious bank accounts, shell companies, related parties, and circular transactions.
Layer 4 — Management Override Detection: Monitors for signs of management override including manual journal entries near period-end, unusual account combinations, approvals outside normal authority, and audit adjustment patterns.
Layer 5 — Collusion Pattern Recognition: Identifies potential collusion through analysis of communication patterns, shared IP addresses, sequential transaction timing, and coordinated approval behaviors.

Continuously updated with new fraud types

Override Detection Agent

08
Third-Party & Vendor Risk Assessment
RISK ASSESSMENT & MONITORING
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Assesses and continuously monitors financial risks associated with third-party vendors, service providers, and business partners to ensure they do not introduce control weaknesses or financial reporting risks.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Vendor Risk Profiling: Categorizes vendors based on financial materiality, access to sensitive data, critical service dependency, geographic risk, and industry-specific risks.
Layer 2 — Due Diligence Validation: Validates vendor financial stability, business licenses, tax compliance, beneficial ownership, sanctions screening, and adverse media checks before onboarding.
Layer 3 — Ongoing Risk Monitoring: Continuously monitors vendor performance, financial health indicators, compliance violations, cybersecurity incidents, and adverse news for early warning signals.
Layer 4 — Control Reliance Assessment: Evaluates whether the organization can rely on vendor controls for financial reporting (e.g., service organization SOC reports, data center controls, payroll processing).
Layer 5 — Vendor Concentration Analysis: Identifies over-reliance on single vendors, assesses business continuity risks, and evaluates financial impact of vendor failures.

Reporting Agent

09
IT General Controls (ITGC) Monitoring
RISK ASSESSMENT & MONITORING
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Ensures that IT general controls supporting financial systems are operating effectively, including access controls, change management, backup and recovery, and IT operations controls that form the foundation for application controls.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Access Control Validation: Monitors user access to financial systems, validates segregation of duties in IT environment, tracks privileged access usage, and ensures timely access revocation.
Layer 2 — Change Management Oversight: Tracks all changes to financial systems including patches, upgrades, configuration changes, and custom code modifications. Validates change approval, testing, and emergency change protocols.
Layer 3 — Backup & Recovery Testing: Validates that backup procedures are executed as scheduled, tests restore capabilities periodically, monitors backup success rates, and validates offsite storage.
Layer 4 — IT Operations Monitoring: Tracks system availability, batch job completion, interface processing, database integrity, and infrastructure controls supporting financial applications.
Layer 5 — ITGC Deficiency Impact Analysis: Assesses how ITGC deficiencies impact application controls and financial statement risks, prioritizes remediation based on financial reporting impact.

10
Change Management Risk Assessment
RISK ASSESSMENT & MONITORING
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Assesses financial reporting risks introduced by business changes including system implementations, process changes, organizational restructuring, mergers and acquisitions, and regulatory changes.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Change Catalog Management: Maintains inventory of all planned and ongoing changes affecting financial processes, systems, policies, or organizational structure.
Layer 2 — Pre-Implementation Risk Assessment: Evaluates financial reporting impact of proposed changes including new control requirements, data migration risks, interface disruptions, and reporting gaps.
Layer 3 — Parallel Run Validation: Validates that parallel runs between old and new systems are performed successfully, reconciled completely, and discrepancies resolved before cutover.
Layer 4 — Post-Implementation Control Validation: Ensures that new controls are operating effectively after implementation, data integrity is maintained, and no unintended consequences emerged.
Layer 5 — Transition Period Monitoring: Provides enhanced monitoring during transition periods when new and old processes coexist, identifies control gaps, and prevents period-end reporting failures.

CA Control Activities & Procedures
Use Cases 11-15
Purchase-to-Pay Controls, Order-to-Cash Controls, Record-to-Report Controls, Payroll Controls & Treasury Controls
11
Purchase-to-Pay (P2P) Control Execution
CONTROL ACTIVITIES & PROCEDURES
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Executes and validates all controls in the purchase-to-pay cycle including requisition approval, PO creation, goods receipt, invoice matching, payment approval, and vendor master management.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Requisition Control Validation: Validates budget availability, approval authority, vendor selection compliance, and segregation between requestor and approver before creating purchase requisitions.
Layer 2 — Three-Way Match Automation: Automatically matches purchase order, goods receipt note, and vendor invoice for quantity, price, and terms. Flags discrepancies for resolution before payment processing.
Layer 3 — Payment Authorization Control: Validates payment approval authority, duplicate payment prevention, vendor bank account verification, and segregation between payment approval and execution.
Layer 4 — Vendor Master Control: Ensures vendor creation requires dual approval, bank account changes are validated, duplicate vendors are prevented, and related party vendors are flagged.
Layer 5 — Period-End Accruals: Identifies goods received but not invoiced (GRNI), monitors outstanding purchase orders, validates accrual completeness, and ensures proper expense period allocation.

Requisition Control Agent

12
Order-to-Cash (O2C) Control Execution
CONTROL ACTIVITIES & PROCEDURES
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Executes and validates all controls in the order-to-cash cycle including customer credit approval, sales order processing, shipment validation, invoicing, revenue recognition, and cash collection.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Credit Approval Control: Validates customer credit limits before order acceptance, monitors credit exposure in real-time, flags overdue customers, and enforces credit hold procedures.
Layer 2 — Revenue Recognition Validation: Ensures revenue is recognized in accordance with accounting standards (ASC 606 / IFRS 15), validates performance obligation completion, and prevents premature revenue recognition.
Layer 3 — Pricing & Discount Control: Validates that pricing follows approved price lists, discount approvals are within authority, contract pricing is correctly applied, and bill-and-hold transactions are properly controlled.
Layer 4 — Shipment Matching Control: Matches sales orders to shipment documents and invoices, prevents revenue recognition without shipment, and identifies unbilled shipments.
Layer 5 — Cash Application & Reconciliation: Automatically matches customer payments to outstanding invoices, handles short payments and overpayments, and reconciles cash receipts to bank deposits.

Aging Agent

13
Record-to-Report (R2R) Control Execution
CONTROL ACTIVITIES & PROCEDURES
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Executes and validates all controls in the record-to-report cycle including journal entry approval, account reconciliations, financial close procedures, consolidation controls, and financial statement preparation.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Journal Entry Control: Validates that all journal entries are supported by appropriate documentation, approved by authorized individuals, and comply with accounting policies. Flags high-risk journal entries for additional review.
Layer 2 — Account Reconciliation Control: Ensures all balance sheet accounts are reconciled within defined timelines, reconciling items are aged and resolved, and reconciliation reviews are completed by independent reviewers.
Layer 3 — Financial Close Checklist: Executes financial close procedures in sequence, tracks completion of required tasks, identifies bottlenecks, and ensures close is completed within target timeline.
Layer 4 — Consolidation Control: Validates intercompany eliminations, ensures consistent accounting policies across entities, identifies consolidation adjustments, and validates currency translations.
Layer 5 — Financial Statement Review: Performs automated analytical review procedures, compares current period to prior period and budget, identifies unusual fluctuations, and validates disclosure completeness.

14
Payroll Processing & Controls
CONTROL ACTIVITIES & PROCEDURES
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Executes and validates all payroll controls including new hire approval, timesheet validation, payroll calculation accuracy, payment authorization, payroll tax compliance, and termination processing.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Employee Master Control: Validates that all new hires are properly approved, employee data changes require HR and finance approval, and terminated employees are removed from payroll immediately.
Layer 2 — Time & Attendance Validation: Validates timesheet approvals, identifies unusual overtime patterns, ensures compliance with labor laws, and validates commission and bonus calculations.
Layer 3 — Payroll Calculation Control: Validates payroll calculations against approved pay rates, identifies calculation anomalies, ensures proper tax withholdings, and validates net pay against historical patterns.
Layer 4 — Payment Authorization: Ensures payroll is approved before payment execution, validates bank account changes, prevents duplicate payments, and segregates payroll preparation from approval.
Layer 5 — Payroll Reconciliation: Reconciles payroll register to general ledger, validates tax deposits against returns, monitors payroll-related liabilities, and ensures compliance with statutory reporting.

15
Treasury & Cash Management Controls
CONTROL ACTIVITIES & PROCEDURES
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Executes and validates treasury controls including bank account management, cash forecasting, investment authorization, debt covenant compliance, foreign exchange management, and daily cash reconciliation.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Bank Account Control: Validates that bank account openings require treasury and CFO approval, authorized signatories are current, account closures are processed timely, and dormant accounts are identified.
Layer 2 — Cash Position Monitoring: Tracks cash positions across all entities and currencies in real-time, forecasts cash requirements, identifies potential shortfalls, and optimizes cash utilization.
Layer 3 — Investment & Borrowing Authorization: Ensures investments and borrowings are within board-approved limits, counterparty risk is assessed, investment policy compliance is maintained, and debt covenants are monitored.
Layer 4 — Daily Cash Reconciliation: Reconciles bank statements daily, identifies and investigates reconciling items, monitors uncleared checks, and validates intercompany cash transfers.
Layer 5 — Foreign Exchange Control: Monitors FX exposure, validates hedge effectiveness, ensures timely revaluation of foreign currency balances, and tracks derivative positions.

IC Information & Communication
Use Cases 16-20
Financial Data Quality, System Integration Monitoring, Reporting Controls, External Communication & Internal Control Communication
16
Financial Data Quality & Integrity Validation
INFORMATION & COMMUNICATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Validates the accuracy, completeness, and consistency of financial data across all systems and processes, ensuring that financial reports are based on reliable data that meets quality standards.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Data Validation Rules: Maintains comprehensive data quality rules including format validation, range checks, referential integrity, mandatory field completion, and logical consistency checks.
Layer 2 — Duplicate Detection: Identifies duplicate transactions, customers, vendors, and general ledger entries. Prevents duplicate processing and flags for resolution.
Layer 3 — Completeness Checks: Validates that all required data elements are captured, sequence gaps in transaction numbers are identified, and all source system data is transferred to target systems.
Layer 4 — Consistency Validation: Ensures data consistency across systems (e.g., customer balance in AR matches GL, inventory quantity matches warehouse system), identifies and resolves discrepancies.
Layer 5 — Historical Data Integrity: Monitors for unauthorized changes to historical transactions, validates audit trail completeness, and ensures data cannot be manipulated without detection.

System Integration & Interface Control Monitoring
INFORMATION & COMMUNICATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Monitors the completeness and accuracy of data transfers between financial systems and source systems, ensuring interfaces operate reliably and data integrity is maintained during transmission.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Interface Inventory Management: Maintains complete inventory of all interfaces feeding financial systems including real-time APIs, batch file transfers, database replication, and manual uploads.
Layer 2 — Interface Execution Monitoring: Tracks scheduled interface runs, identifies failed or delayed interfaces, monitors processing times, and validates interface completion before dependent processes start.
Layer 3 — Data Reconciliation Controls: Reconciles record counts and totals between source and target systems, identifies missing or duplicate records, and validates data transformation logic.
Layer 4 — Error Handling & Retry Logic: Monitors interface error logs, validates automatic retry mechanisms, tracks manual interventions, and ensures failed records are processed or resolved.
Layer 5 — Interface Change Management: Ensures interface changes are properly tested, validated, and approved before production deployment, and parallel runs are performed for critical interfaces.

Validates the accuracy and completeness of financial reports distributed to management, board, investors, and regulators, ensuring reports are derived from accurate data and comply with reporting requirements.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Report Source Validation: Validates that reports are generated from approved data sources, formula and calculation logic is correct, and report parameters match reporting requirements.
Layer 2 — Reconciliation to General Ledger: Ensures all external reports (investor presentations, regulatory filings, press releases) reconcile to the general ledger and supporting schedules.
Layer 3 — Disclosure Review Controls: Validates disclosure completeness against checklists, ensures consistency between financial statements and MD&A, and validates regulatory compliance.
Layer 4 — Version Control & Approval: Maintains version control on all distributed reports, tracks review and approval workflows, and ensures only approved versions are distributed.
Layer 5 — Distribution Control: Validates that reports are distributed to the correct recipients, sensitive information is protected, and distribution timing complies with regulatory requirements.

Layer 1 — Communication Inventory: Maintains inventory of all external financial communications, tracks disclosure obligations, and ensures timely communication of material events.
Layer 2 — Content Validation: Validates that financial metrics in external communications match approved sources, non-GAAP measures are properly reconciled, and forward-looking statements include required disclaimers.
Layer 3 — Consistency Review: Ensures consistency across all external communications, identifies conflicting information, and validates alignment with financial statements.
Layer 4 — Approval Workflow: Enforces multi-level approval for external communications (CFO, legal, investor relations, CEO), tracks review completion, and maintains approval evidence.
Layer 5 — Regulatory Compliance Tracking: Validates compliance with disclosure regulations (Reg FD, SOX, stock exchange rules), tracks filing deadlines, and monitors selective disclosure risks.

Internal Control Communication & Training

Ensures that employees understand their control responsibilities, are trained on control procedures, and that control changes and deficiencies are communicated effectively to all relevant stakeholders.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Control Owner Communication: Ensures control owners understand their responsibilities, receive updates on control changes, and are alerted to control failures or testing results.
Layer 2 — Training Needs Analysis: Identifies training requirements based on role, control ownership, deficiency patterns, and regulatory changes. Tracks training completion and effectiveness.
Layer 3 — Control Change Communication: Communicates control design changes, policy updates, and system enhancements to affected users with adequate lead time for adaptation.
Layer 4 — Deficiency Communication: Ensures control deficiencies are communicated to appropriate levels of management, action plans are tracked, and escalation occurs if remediation is delayed.
Layer 5 — Continuous Awareness Programs: Maintains ongoing internal control awareness through newsletters, workshops, refresher training, and control effectiveness scorecards.

TT Transaction Testing & Validation
Use Cases 21-25
Automated Transaction Testing, Sampling & Statistical Analysis, High-Risk Transaction Review, Continuous Audit & Exception Testing
21
Automated Transaction Testing & Validation
TRANSACTION TESTING & VALIDATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Performs continuous automated testing of financial transactions against control criteria, identifying exceptions and control failures in real-time rather than through periodic testing cycles.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Test Script Library: Maintains library of automated test scripts for each control including authorization checks, calculation validations, segregation of duties tests, and policy compliance tests.
Layer 2 — Continuous Testing Execution: Executes test scripts on 100% of transactions in real-time or near-real-time, eliminating the need for sampling and providing complete population coverage.
Layer 3 — Exception Detection & Categorization: Identifies control exceptions, categorizes by severity (critical, high, medium, low), determines root cause, and routes for investigation.
Layer 4 — Test Results Analytics: Analyzes test results to identify control weakness patterns, high-failure controls, process areas requiring remediation, and emerging risks.
Layer 5 — Evidence Compilation: Automatically compiles test evidence, maintains audit trail of testing performed, documents exceptions and resolutions, and provides audit-ready documentation.
AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP
01
Test Library Agent
Control tests maintained as executable scripts
Test coverage mapped to controls
02
Continuous Testing Agent
Tests executed on all transactions
Real-time exception detection
03
Exception Detection Agent
Control failures identified and categorized
Root cause analysis performed
04
Analytics Agent
Test results analyzed for patterns
Control weakness trends identified
05
Investigation Agent
Exceptions routed for resolution
Remediation tracked to closure
06
Evidence Agent
Testing evidence compiled
Audit trail maintained
07
Reporting Agent
Testing dashboard updated
Real-time control effectiveness visibility

22
Statistical Sampling & Extrapolation
TRANSACTION TESTING & VALIDATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Performs statistical sampling for controls that cannot be tested at 100% population, ensures sample sizes are adequate, and extrapolates results to the entire population with appropriate confidence levels.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Sample Size Determination: Calculates appropriate sample sizes based on population size, expected error rate, confidence level, and precision requirements using statistical sampling methodologies.
Layer 2 — Random Sample Selection: Generates random samples or stratified samples ensuring all population segments are represented, eliminates selection bias, and maintains audit trail of selection.
Layer 3 — Sample Testing Execution: Executes control tests on selected samples, documents test results, identifies exceptions, and validates that sample characteristics match population.
Layer 4 — Error Rate Calculation: Calculates sample error rate, determines if error rate is within acceptable tolerance, and flags when error rates exceed thresholds.
Layer 5 — Population Extrapolation: Extrapolates sample results to entire population, calculates upper error limits with specified confidence levels, and determines population control effectiveness.
AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP
01
Sample Size Agent
Statistical sample size calculated
Confidence and precision parameters set
02
Selection Agent
Random/stratified samples selected
Selection bias eliminated
03
Testing Agent
Control tests performed on samples
Test results documented
04
Error Rate Agent
Sample error rate calculated
Threshold exceedance flagged
05
Extrapolation Agent
Results extrapolated to population
Upper error limits calculated
06
Decision Agent
Control effectiveness conclusion reached
Additional testing triggered if needed
07
Reporting Agent
Sampling results documented
Statistical rigor maintained

23
High-Risk Transaction Review
TRANSACTION TESTING & VALIDATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Identifies and subjects high-risk transactions to enhanced scrutiny and additional validation procedures beyond standard controls, preventing material errors and fraud.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Risk Scoring Model: Scores every transaction based on risk factors including amount, transaction type, approver, timing (period-end), account combinations, and historical patterns.
Layer 2 — Threshold-Based Flagging: Flags transactions exceeding materiality thresholds, involving sensitive accounts, processed outside normal hours, or matching known fraud patterns.
Layer 3 — Enhanced Review Procedures: Routes high-risk transactions for additional documentation review, secondary approval, independent verification, or management review before posting.
Layer 4 — Pattern Anomaly Detection: Identifies unusual transaction patterns such as round amounts, sequential entries, off-cycle processing, or transactions just below approval thresholds.
Layer 5 — Investigative Workflow: Initiates investigation workflow for highest risk transactions, compiles evidence, documents resolution, and maintains case management.

Risk Scoring Agent

04
Anomaly Detection Agent

Continuous Audit Monitoring
TRANSACTION TESTING & VALIDATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Provides continuous audit coverage through automated monitoring of key controls, risk indicators, and compliance requirements, enabling real-time assurance and reducing reliance on periodic audits.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Audit Universe Mapping: Maps all auditable processes, controls, and compliance requirements to automated monitoring routines, ensuring comprehensive audit coverage.
Layer 2 — Real-Time Audit Tests: Executes audit tests continuously on transaction streams, master data changes, access logs, and reconciliation processes rather than at period-end.
Layer 3 — Risk Indicator Monitoring: Tracks leading risk indicators such as increasing error rates, rising exception volumes, control override frequency, and unusual activity patterns.
Layer 4 — Compliance Monitoring: Validates ongoing compliance with policies, regulations, and industry standards through continuous rule-based testing rather than periodic audits.
Layer 5 — Audit Issue Tracking: Maintains register of all audit issues identified through continuous monitoring, tracks remediation actions, and validates issue closure.

25
Exception & Variance Testing
TRANSACTION TESTING & VALIDATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Identifies and investigates exceptions and variances from expected patterns, norms, or thresholds across financial transactions and account balances, detecting errors and irregularities.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Baseline Establishment: Establishes baseline patterns for transaction volumes, amounts, account balances, ratios, and trends based on historical data and business drivers.
Layer 2 — Variance Detection: Compares actual results against baselines, identifies statistically significant variances, and distinguishes between expected business variations and control failures.
Layer 3 — Exception Categorization: Categorizes exceptions by type (timing, amount, frequency, authorization), assigns severity based on financial impact, and prioritizes investigation.
Layer 4 — Root Cause Analysis: Investigates exceptions to determine root cause (system error, manual error, control failure, fraud, business change), documents findings, and recommends corrective action.
Layer 5 — Exception Resolution Tracking: Tracks exception investigation and resolution, validates corrective actions, and monitors for recurrence of similar exceptions.
AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP
01
Baseline Agent

Expected ranges calculated
02
Variance Detection Agent

Significant variances flagged
03
Exception Agent
Exceptions categorized by type
Severity assigned
04
Investigation Agent
Root cause analysis performed
Findings documented
05
Resolution Agent
Corrective actions tracked
Exception closure validated
06
Pattern Agent

JE Journal Entry & Reconciliation
Use Cases 26-30
Journal Entry Review, Manual Entry Monitoring, Period-End Adjustment Controls, Account Reconciliation & Intercompany Reconciliation
26
Journal Entry Review & Approval
JOURNAL ENTRY & RECONCILIATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Reviews and approves all journal entries to ensure they are supported by appropriate documentation, comply with accounting policies, are approved by authorized individuals, and are accurately recorded.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Entry Classification: Classifies journal entries by type (standard, recurring, manual, adjusting, reclassification), risk level, and review requirements.
Layer 2 — Supporting Documentation Validation: Ensures all journal entries have adequate supporting documentation attached, validates calculations, and confirms business rationale.
Layer 3 — Accounting Policy Compliance: Validates that journal entries comply with accounting policies, GAAP/IFRS requirements, and company-specific guidelines.
Layer 4 — Approval Authority Validation: Ensures journal entries are approved by individuals with appropriate authority based on entry type, amount, and account impact.
Layer 5 — Posting Period Control: Validates that entries are posted to the correct accounting period, prevents backdating, and ensures period-end adjustments are properly authorized.
AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP
01
Classification Agent
Journal entries classified by type/risk
Review requirements assigned
02
Documentation Agent
Supporting documentation validated
Incomplete entries rejected
03
Policy Compliance Agent
Accounting policy compliance checked
Non-compliant entries flagged
04
Approval Agent
Approval authority validated
Unauthorized entries blocked
05
Period Control Agent
Posting period validated
Backdating prevented
06
Audit Trail Agent
Complete entry trail maintained
All approvals documented
07
Reporting Agent
JE dashboard updated
Entry volume and risk metrics tracked

27
Manual Journal Entry Monitoring
JOURNAL ENTRY & RECONCILIATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Monitors manual journal entries for unusual patterns, high-risk characteristics, and potential fraud indicators, providing enhanced oversight over the highest risk area of financial reporting.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Manual Entry Identification: Identifies all manual journal entries (entries not automatically generated by systems), tracks volume trends, and monitors increase in manual entries.
Layer 2 — High-Risk Pattern Detection: Flags entries with fraud risk indicators including round amounts, unusual account combinations, period-end timing, reversals in subsequent period, and off-hours posting.
Layer 3 — User Behavior Analytics: Monitors user patterns to identify unusual behavior such as first-time entries to sensitive accounts, entries outside normal work hours, or entries by users with excess access.
Layer 4 — Top-Side Entry Review: Subjects corporate-level consolidation entries and top-side adjustments to enhanced review given their materiality and potential for management override.
Layer 5 — Trend Analysis: Analyzes manual entry trends over time, identifies increases in manual interventions, and assesses whether increases indicate control deterioration or process automation opportunities.
AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP
01
Manual Entry Agent
All manual entries identified
Volume trends monitored
02
Risk Pattern Agent
High-risk patterns detected
Fraud indicators flagged
03
Behavior Analytics Agent
User behavior anomalies identified
Unusual activity investigated
04
Top-Side Review Agent
Corporate entries subjected to enhanced review
Management override risk assessed
05
Trend Analysis Agent
Manual entry trends analyzed
Automation opportunities identified
06
Investigation Agent
High-risk entries investigated
Evidence compiled
07
Reporting Agent
Manual entry metrics tracked
Real-time risk visibility

28
Period-End Adjustment Control
JOURNAL ENTRY & RECONCILIATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Controls and monitors period-end adjusting entries to ensure they are appropriate, properly supported, and do not manipulate financial results to meet targets or manage earnings.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Adjustment Type Validation: Validates that period-end adjustments are for legitimate accruals, deferrals, reclassifications, or corrections rather than earnings management.
Layer 2 — Magnitude & Trend Analysis: Compares period-end adjustment amounts to historical patterns, identifies unusual increases, and flags adjustments just sufficient to meet targets.
Layer 3 — Reversal Pattern Monitoring: Tracks adjustments that reverse in subsequent period, identifies patterns of recurring reversals, and assesses whether adjustments were appropriate.
Layer 4 — Account Sensitivity Analysis: Subjects adjustments to sensitive accounts (revenue, COGS, reserves, provisions) to enhanced scrutiny given their income statement impact.
Layer 5 — Management Pressure Indicators: Identifies periods with high pressure to meet targets (quarter-end, year-end, covenant compliance) and applies enhanced review to adjustments made during these periods.
AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

Adjustment Validation Agent

Reversal Agent

Sensitivity Agent

Pressure Agent

Investigation Agent

Reporting Agent

Account Reconciliation Management
JOURNAL ENTRY & RECONCILIATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Manages the account reconciliation process to ensure all balance sheet accounts are reconciled timely, reconciling items are investigated and resolved, and reconciliation reviews are completed independently.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Reconciliation Universe: Maintains inventory of all accounts requiring reconciliation, defines reconciliation frequency (daily, weekly, monthly), and assigns reconciliation owners.
Layer 2 — Reconciliation Completion Monitoring: Tracks reconciliation completion against schedule, identifies late or missing reconciliations, and escalates to management.
Layer 3 — Reconciling Item Aging: Ages all reconciling items, tracks resolution progress, flags long-outstanding items, and ensures timely resolution.
Layer 4 — Review Completion Validation: Ensures reconciliations are reviewed by independent reviewers, validates review sign-off, and tracks reviewer workload to prevent review bottlenecks.
Layer 5 — Reconciliation Quality Assessment: Assesses reconciliation quality through metrics such as first-time accuracy, number of reconciling items, and time to resolution.

30
Intercompany Reconciliation & Elimination
JOURNAL ENTRY & RECONCILIATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Reconciles and eliminates intercompany transactions and balances to ensure consolidated financial statements are free from internal transactions and that intercompany accounts balance to zero.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Intercompany Transaction Matching: Matches intercompany sales to purchases, receivables to payables, and loans to borrowings across all legal entities within the group.
Layer 2 — Timing Difference Identification: Identifies timing differences in intercompany transaction recording (e.g., goods shipped but not received), tracks in-transit items, and ensures resolution before consolidation.
Layer 3 — Foreign Currency Impact: Validates that intercompany transactions in foreign currencies are translated consistently across entities and eliminates translation differences in consolidation.
Layer 4 — Elimination Entry Validation: Validates that intercompany elimination entries are complete, accurate, and properly offset all internal transactions and balances.
Layer 5 — Unreconciled Balance Resolution: Tracks and resolves unreconciled intercompany balances, investigates root causes, and ensures timely resolution to prevent consolidation delays.

SOX Compliance Management
COMPLIANCE & DOCUMENTATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Manages Sarbanes-Oxley compliance requirements including control documentation, testing, deficiency tracking, certifications, and quarterly/annual assessments to ensure continuous SOX compliance.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Control Documentation Management: Maintains complete documentation of all key controls including control objectives, control activities, frequency, responsible parties, and testing procedures.
Layer 2 — Testing Cycle Management: Manages control testing cycles (quarterly, annual), assigns testing responsibilities, tracks testing completion, and validates testing adequacy.
Layer 3 — Deficiency Management: Tracks control deficiencies from identification through remediation, categorizes severity (material weakness, significant deficiency, deficiency), and monitors management action plans.
Layer 4 — Certification Process: Manages CEO/CFO certification process, compiles supporting evidence, tracks sub-certifications from business unit leaders, and maintains certification documentation.
Layer 5 — Scoping & Prioritization: Performs annual SOX scoping to identify significant accounts and processes, determines key controls, and prioritizes testing resources.

Audit Evidence & Workpaper Management
COMPLIANCE & DOCUMENTATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Manages audit evidence and workpapers to ensure all control testing, exception investigations, and audit requests are properly documented, organized, and readily accessible for internal and external auditors.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Evidence Repository: Maintains centralized repository of all audit evidence including test results, exception investigations, management reviews, approvals, and supporting documentation.
Layer 2 — Automated Evidence Collection: Automatically captures audit evidence from systems including approval workflows, system logs, reconciliation files, and transaction records rather than manual collection.
Layer 3 — Workpaper Organization: Organizes evidence by audit area, control, testing period, and auditor requests, enabling rapid retrieval and preventing duplicate requests.
Layer 4 — Version Control & Retention: Maintains version control on all workpapers, ensures retention policies are followed, and provides secure access to historical audit evidence.
Layer 5 — Audit Request Tracking: Tracks all internal and external audit requests, monitors response deadlines, validates completeness of responses, and maintains request history.

Control Documentation Maintenance
COMPLIANCE & DOCUMENTATION
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Maintains current and complete documentation of all internal controls including narratives, process flows, control matrices, and risk-control mappings, ensuring documentation accurately reflects actual control design.

Layer 1 — Documentation Completeness: Ensures all controls have complete documentation including control objective, detailed control description, frequency, responsible party, and evidence of execution.
Layer 2 — Process Flow Accuracy: Maintains process flowcharts showing where controls operate within processes, validates flows match actual operations, and updates flows when processes change.
Layer 3 — Risk-Control Mapping: Maps each control to specific risks it mitigates, ensures all significant risks have controls, and identifies control gaps.
Layer 4 — Documentation Version Control: Tracks changes to control documentation, maintains historical versions, and ensures all stakeholders work from current versions.
Layer 5 — Documentation Review Cycles: Performs periodic reviews of control documentation to ensure accuracy, identify outdated content, and validate consistency across the organization.

WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Monitors compliance with financial reporting regulations, accounting standards, tax regulations, and industry-specific requirements, ensuring the organization remains compliant and avoids penalties.

Layer 2 — Requirement Tracking: Tracks specific compliance requirements from each regulation, maps requirements to responsible parties, and monitors compliance status.
Layer 3 — Regulatory Change Monitoring: Monitors regulatory updates and new pronouncements, assesses impact on the organization, and ensures timely implementation of new requirements.
Layer 4 — Compliance Gap Assessment: Identifies gaps between current state and regulatory requirements, prioritizes gap remediation based on risk, and tracks remediation to completion.
Layer 5 — Filing & Deadline Management: Tracks all regulatory filing deadlines, monitors preparation progress, validates filing completeness, and ensures timely submission.

Layer 3 — Implementation Documentation: Maintains complete record of implementation activities including testing results, parallel runs, data migration validation, and go-live verification.
Layer 4 — Before/After Comparison: Documents pre-change and post-change state for all significant changes, enabling rollback if needed and providing audit trail of evolution.
Layer 5 — Lessons Learned Capture: Captures lessons learned from each change, identifies opportunities for improvement, and incorporates insights into future change management.

36
Financial Performance Analytics
MANAGEMENT REVIEW & ANALYTICS
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Provides comprehensive financial analytics to management including variance analysis, trend analysis, ratio analysis, and predictive insights to support decision-making and identify potential issues.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Variance Analysis Automation: Automatically calculates variances between actual and budget, actual and prior period, and actual and forecast across all financial statement line items.
Layer 2 — Ratio & Trend Analysis: Calculates key financial ratios (liquidity, profitability, efficiency, leverage), tracks trends over time, and flags deteriorating trends.
Layer 3 — Segment & Product Analytics: Provides profitability analytics by business segment, product line, customer, and geography, enabling informed portfolio decisions.
Layer 4 — Driver-Based Analysis: Links financial results to business drivers (volume, price, mix, cost), quantifies driver impacts, and validates financial results against operational metrics.
Layer 5 — Scenario Modeling: Enables what-if scenario analysis for management decisions, quantifies financial impact of strategic options, and supports business case development.

Analyzes control effectiveness metrics to identify control weaknesses, high-failure controls, emerging risks, and opportunities for control optimization and automation.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Control Performance Metrics: Tracks metrics for each control including execution rate, exception rate, timeliness, and override frequency.
Layer 2 — Failure Pattern Analysis: Identifies patterns in control failures including high-failure controls, repeat failures, failure clustering by period or user, and root cause trends.
Layer 3 — Control Coverage Analysis: Assesses whether control coverage is adequate for identified risks, identifies redundant controls, and highlights control gaps.
Layer 4 — Automation Opportunity Identification: Identifies manual controls with high execution burden, frequent failures, or automation potential to improve efficiency and effectiveness.
Layer 5 — Control Maturity Assessment: Assesses overall control maturity using frameworks (COSO, COBIT), benchmarks against industry standards, and identifies maturity improvement opportunities.

Predictive Risk Analytics
MANAGEMENT REVIEW & ANALYTICS
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Uses predictive analytics and machine learning to forecast future control failures, identify emerging risks before they materialize, and enable proactive risk mitigation rather than reactive response.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Leading Indicator Modeling: Identifies leading indicators that predict control failures or financial statement risks before they occur based on historical patterns.
Layer 2 — Risk Score Forecasting: Develops predictive models to forecast risk scores for accounts, processes, and entities based on historical risk factors and current conditions.
Layer 3 — Anomaly Prediction: Uses machine learning to predict unusual patterns, outliers, and potential fraud scenarios before they fully develop.
Layer 4 — Workload & Resource Forecasting: Predicts future workload for reconciliations, reviews, and approvals to enable proactive resource allocation and prevent bottlenecks.
Layer 5 — Scenario-Based Risk Modeling: Forecasts risk exposure under different business scenarios (growth, downturn, M&A, regulatory change) to enable forward-looking risk management.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — KPI Definition & Tracking: Defines key performance indicators for financial reporting, internal controls, and compliance, tracks against targets, and highlights variances.
Layer 2 — Role-Based Dashboards: Provides customized dashboards for different stakeholders (CFO, Controller, CAE, Board) with relevant metrics and drill-down capabilities.
Layer 3 — Real-Time Data Refresh: Updates dashboards in real-time as data changes, ensuring management decisions are based on current information rather than stale data.
Layer 4 — Exception-Based Reporting: Highlights only items requiring management attention, eliminating information overload and enabling focus on critical issues.
Layer 5 — Mobile Accessibility: Provides mobile-optimized dashboards enabling executives to monitor key metrics and respond to alerts from anywhere.

AP   Audit Planning & Risk Assessment
Use Cases 01-05
Audit Universe, Continuous Risk Monitoring, Scoping, Universe Maintenance & Resource Management


01
Annual Audit Universe & Risk Assessment
AUDIT PLANNING & RISK ASSESSMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Builds the complete audit universe — every auditable entity, process, and system across the organisation — and scores each by inherent risk to produce a prioritised, risk-ranked audit plan for the year.
  Replaces the subjective, relationship-driven audit planning of the past with a data-driven, defensible risk-prioritisation framework approved by the Audit Committee.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Universe Construction: Reads the organisation structure, ERP process catalogue, subsidiary list, and prior audit register to build the complete audit universe — entities, business processes, IT systems, and third-party relationships.

  Layer 2 — Inherent Risk Scoring: Scores each universe item across risk dimensions — financial materiality (balance sheet and P&L exposure), transaction volume, regulatory sensitivity, prior audit findings, management complexity, and rate of change.

  Layer 3 — Control Environment Assessment: Overlays the control environment score — maturity of internal controls, frequency of prior findings, management self-assessment scores, and time since last audit.

  Layer 4 — Residual Risk Ranking: Computes residual risk = Inherent Risk minus Control Effectiveness. Ranks all universe items from Critical to Low. Allocates audit days proportionally to residual risk.

  Layer 5 — Plan Optimisation: Optimises the plan against available audit resources and mandatory audits. Ensures coverage of all Critical and High items. Identifies opportunities for continuous auditing on low-risk, high-volume items.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

01
Universe Agent
Audit universe compiled
All entities, processes, and systems catalogued
02
Risk Scoring Agent
Inherent risk scored per universe item
Multi-dimensional risk score computed
03
Control Agent
Control environment score applied
Prior findings and maturity overlaid
04
Residual Risk Agent
Residual risk ranking produced
Critical / High / Medium / Low classification
05
Resource Agent
Audit days allocated
Plan optimised within audit team capacity
06
Mandatory Audit Agent
Regulatory and board-mandated audits confirmed
Fixed audits locked into plan
07
AC Presentation Agent
Audit Committee presentation prepared
Risk-ranked plan with rationale ready for approval

CONFIDENCE-BASED ROUTING

Confidence
Action
Human Effort
All universe items scored, plan within resources
Plan presented to AC for approval
CAE + AC review
New risk emerges mid-year
Ad-hoc audit inserted; plan rebalanced
CAE decision
Prior year critical finding not remediated
Automatic follow-up audit scheduled
CAE priority
Management disputes risk score
Evidence-based discussion; final CAE decision
CAE arbitration

Outcome:  Audit plan coverage of all Critical and High risks: 100%. Planning cycle from 6 weeks to 2 weeks. Audit Committee confidence in plan rigour improved. Emerging risks captured in-year.


02
Continuous Risk Monitoring
AUDIT PLANNING & RISK ASSESSMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Continuously monitors the risk landscape between planned audits — scanning transactions, KPIs, news, and regulatory changes — to detect emerging risks that require an unscheduled audit or control enhancement.
  Shifts internal audit from periodic snapshot to near-real-time risk sentinel.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Financial Risk Signals: Monitors key financial metrics for anomalies — sudden revenue drops, cost spikes, margin deterioration, unusual accruals, and balance sheet inflections that could signal control failures or fraud.

  Layer 2 — Operational Risk Signals: Tracks operational KPIs — supplier concentration, customer concentration, inventory write-offs, unusual returns, and production downtime — for patterns inconsistent with the business plan.

  Layer 3 — External Risk Signals: Monitors external risk intelligence — regulatory changes, industry enforcement actions, supplier adverse media, and macroeconomic shifts that could affect the company's risk profile.

  Layer 4 — Control Failure Indicators: Reads exception reports from all key control systems — ERP segregation of duty violations, access control exceptions, policy bypass logs, and reconciliation failures.

  Layer 5 — Risk Alert Generation: Aggregates signals and generates risk alerts — with severity classification (Critical / High / Medium), supporting evidence, and recommended audit or management action.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

01
Financial Monitor Agent
Financial KPIs monitored daily
Anomalies vs. trend and budget flagged
02
Operational Monitor Agent
Operational metrics tracked
Process health indicators monitored
03
External Intelligence Agent
News, regulatory, and macro signals scanned
Relevant external risk events identified
04
Control Failure Agent
Control exception reports read
Segregation of duty and access violations flagged
05
Correlation Agent
Multiple signals correlated
Compound risk scenarios identified
06
Alert Agent
Risk alert generated with severity
CAE and relevant audit manager notified
07
Plan Update Agent
Audit plan adjustment recommended
Ad-hoc audit or management advisory triggered

CONFIDENCE-BASED ROUTING

Confidence
Action
Human Effort
Single low-severity signal
Logged; trend monitoring continues
Zero
High-severity signal
CAE alert within 2 hours; investigation initiated
CAE decision
Multiple correlated signals
Emergency audit or management advisory
CAE + board action
Regulatory change with compliance impact
Advisory memo to management; plan updated
CAE action

Outcome:  Emerging risks detected within 24 hours. Unscheduled audits based on actual risk signals rather than calendar. Control failures surface in days, not months.


03
Audit Scoping & Fieldwork Planning
AUDIT PLANNING & RISK ASSESSMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Prepares the detailed scope, objectives, and fieldwork plan for each scheduled audit — including risk and control matrix, sampling plan, data analytics approach, and resource allocation.
  Ensures every audit starts with a clear, risk-focused scope and a realistic, resource-efficient fieldwork plan.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Prior Audit Review: Reads prior audit reports, management action plans, and follow-up status for the audit area. Identifies recurring findings and unresolved issues that must be covered.

  Layer 2 — Process Mapping: Reads process documentation (SOPs, flowcharts) for the audit area. Identifies key controls — preventive, detective, and corrective — and maps them to the relevant risks.

  Layer 3 — Risk & Control Matrix: Builds the Risk and Control Matrix (RCM) — risk ID, risk description, inherent risk, key control, control owner, control frequency, and test approach.

  Layer 4 — Sampling Plan: Determines the testing approach for each control — full population testing (for high-risk automated controls), statistical sampling, or judgmental sampling. Computes sample size based on population and confidence level.

  Layer 5 — Fieldwork Schedule: Builds the fieldwork schedule — auditor assignments, testing days per control area, management interviews, and data request deadlines. Shares with audit team and management.

04
Audit Universe Maintenance
AUDIT PLANNING & RISK ASSESSMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Keeps the audit universe current — adding new processes, entities, and systems as the business evolves, retiring obsolete items, and re-scoring risks when business or regulatory conditions change.
  Prevents the audit plan from becoming stale and missing significant risks introduced by business change.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Business Change Detection: Monitors signals of audit universe change — new subsidiaries formed, M&A activity, new product launches, major IT system go-lives, regulatory expansion, and significant headcount additions.

  Layer 2 — Universe Item Addition: For each detected change, creates a new audit universe item — categorises it (entity/process/system/relationship), assigns an initial risk score, and recommends timing for first audit.

  Layer 3 — Retirement of Obsolete Items: Identifies universe items that no longer exist — discontinued businesses, decommissioned systems, and wound-up subsidiaries — and retires them from the active universe.

  Layer 4 — Risk Re-Scoring: Re-scores existing universe items when their risk profile changes — increased transaction volumes, regulatory changes, management turnover, or prior year findings.

  Layer 5 — CAE Approval: Presents universe changes to the CAE quarterly for review and approval. Any item moved from Low to High risk triggers an automatic plan review.

05
Audit Resource Management & Capacity Planning
AUDIT PLANNING & RISK ASSESSMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the internal audit team's capacity — matching auditor skills to audit requirements, tracking utilisation, managing guest auditors and co-sourcing, and ensuring the annual plan is achievable.
  Prevents plan under-delivery from resource constraints and maximises the return on the audit team investment.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Team Skill Profiling: Maintains a skills inventory of all internal auditors — technical skills (IT audit, forensic, financial, operational), industry knowledge, certifications (CIA, CISA, CFE, CA), and language capabilities.

  Layer 2 — Audit-to-Auditor Matching: For each planned audit, matches the required skill profile (technical, domain, seniority) to available auditors. Flags audits where no suitable internal auditor is available — co-sourcing candidate.

  Layer 3 — Utilisation Tracking: Tracks auditor utilisation — audit days vs. available days, overtime, leave, and training. Identifies over-utilised auditors (burnout risk) and under-utilised capacity.

  Layer 4 — Co-Sourcing & Guest Auditor Management: Manages co-sourcing relationships — SOW preparation, quality oversight, knowledge transfer tracking, and cost vs. budget. Manages guest auditor onboarding and contribution.

  Layer 5 — Plan Feasibility Check: After any plan change or team change, recomputes plan feasibility — available audit days vs. plan days required. Flags infeasibility to CAE early for plan adjustment or resource add.

FA   Financial Auditing
Use Cases 06-10
AP Audit, Revenue & Receivables, Payroll, Fixed Assets & Financial Close


06
Accounts Payable Audit
FINANCIAL AUDITING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Performs a comprehensive audit of the accounts payable process — testing for duplicate payments, fictitious vendors, three-way match failures, payment terms violations, and segregation of duty weaknesses.
  Identifies AP leakage and control failures with near-100% transaction coverage through automated analytics.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Full Population AP Data Pull: Extracts the complete AP transaction file for the audit period — all invoices, payment runs, credit notes, advance payments, and vendor master changes. No sampling — full population.

  Layer 2 — Duplicate Payment Testing: Runs multi-dimensional duplicate detection — same vendor + same amount + same invoice number, same vendor + same amount + similar dates (fuzzy), same amount + same date + different vendors (ghost vendor signal).

  Layer 3 — Three-Way Match Testing: Tests every AP invoice for three-way match compliance — invoice vs. PO vs. GRN. Flags invoices processed without a PO, GRN, or within tolerance limit that exceeded the approved threshold.

  Layer 4 — Vendor Master Change Analysis: Analyses all vendor master changes — new vendor additions, bank account changes, address changes — during the audit period. Flags changes made by AP staff who also process payments (SOD violation).

  Layer 5 — Payment Terms & Early Payment Analysis: Tests compliance with contracted payment terms. Identifies early payments (potential preference fraud) and payments outside approved terms without documented exception.

07
Revenue & Receivables Audit
FINANCIAL AUDITING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits the revenue recognition process and accounts receivable balance — testing for premature recognition, unsupported adjustments, credit note abuse, and collection control weaknesses.
  Provides assurance on one of the highest-risk financial statement assertions — completeness and accuracy of revenue.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Revenue Recognition Testing: Tests that revenue is recognised in the correct period per Ind AS 115 — specifically for multi-element arrangements, milestone-based contracts, and subscription revenue. Flags revenue recognised before performance obligation is met.

  Layer 2 — Credit Note & Discount Analysis: Analyses all credit notes — total value, timing relative to year-end, distribution by salesperson, and concentration. Flags unusual credit note patterns near period-end (potential reversal of recognised revenue).

  Layer 3 — Ageing & Provisioning Test: Tests the adequacy of the bad debt provision — applies expected credit loss (ECL) model per Ind AS 109 to the AR ageing. Identifies under-provisioned balances.

  Layer 4 — Collection Testing: Tests that cash collected is applied to the correct invoices. Flags unapplied cash, long-outstanding unapplied balances, and collection agency payments without matching invoice closure.

  Layer 5 — Channel & Customer Concentration: Analyses revenue by channel, customer, and geography. Flags unusual concentration, customers with unusual payment patterns, and customers who accept large year-end volumes but return post-period.

08
Payroll Audit
FINANCIAL AUDITING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits the payroll process end-to-end — testing for ghost employees, duplicate payments, unsupported salary changes, and statutory compliance — using full population analytics rather than sampling.
  Payroll is often the largest single expense line and highest fraud risk area — continuous analytics provide assurance every month rather than once a year.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Ghost Employee Detection: Compares the payroll register against the HRMS active employee list, badge system access logs, and IT system active users. Employees on payroll but absent from all other systems are flagged as potential ghost employees.

  Layer 2 — Salary Change Validation: Tests every salary change in the period — increment, promotion, new joiner, and off-cycle adjustment — for supporting approval. Flags changes without a documented HRMS approval event.

  Layer 3 — Duplicate Payment Testing: Detects duplicate payroll payments — same employee + same period + same amount, same bank account as another employee, and terminated employees still on payroll.

  Layer 4 — Statutory Deduction Compliance: Tests that PF, ESI, TDS, and Professional Tax deductions are correctly computed and deposited. Compares ECR file against payroll register. Tests that deposited amounts match deducted amounts.

  Layer 5 — High-Risk Employee Testing: Analyses the distribution of payroll — employees in the top 1% by earnings, employees with large changes, and employees with compensation above their grade band ceiling.

09
Fixed Assets Audit
FINANCIAL AUDITING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits the fixed asset register — verifying physical existence, proper capitalisation vs. expensing, depreciation accuracy, impairment assessment, and disposal compliance.
  Fixed assets are a major balance sheet item and common source of both overstatement and misappropriation.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Physical Existence Verification: Selects a sample of fixed assets from the register and sends verification requests to asset custodians. Uses GPS-tagged assets and QR code scanning where available for automated verification.

  Layer 2 — Completeness — Physical to Register: Identifies assets physically present but not in the register (unrecorded assets). Tests whether significant capital expenditure from the period is fully reflected in the asset register.

  Layer 3 — Capitalisation vs. Expense Testing: Reviews items expensed in the period above the capitalisation threshold. Flags items that should have been capitalised — common form of expense manipulation to reduce reported profit.

  Layer 4 — Depreciation Accuracy: Tests depreciation computation — correct rate per asset class, no depreciation on fully depreciated assets, and correct depreciation from the month of addition. Reconciles net book value movement.

  Layer 5 — Disposal & Impairment: Tests that all asset disposals are approved and proceeds properly accounted for. Reviews idle or low-utilisation assets for impairment indicators per Ind AS 36.

10
Financial Closing & Reporting Audit
FINANCIAL AUDITING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits the financial closing process — journal entry review, account reconciliation quality, and financial statement accuracy — providing assurance that the numbers presented to management and the board are reliable.
  The financial close is where errors, manipulations, and omissions most commonly occur — AI-powered analytics cover 100% of journal entries.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Journal Entry Analytics: Extracts all journal entries for the period. Applies high-risk filters — manual journals (vs. automated), journals posted by IT or system admins, round-number journals, journals posted on holidays or outside business hours, and top-side adjustments.

  Layer 2 — Unusual JE Pattern Detection: Identifies patterns that suggest potential manipulation — entries that reverse in the next period, entries that bring an account balance exactly to zero, and entries with vague or missing narrations.

  Layer 3 — Account Reconciliation Quality: Reviews the quality of balance sheet account reconciliations — are all material balances reconciled? Are reconciling items old or unsupported? Is the reconciliation reviewed and approved?

  Layer 4 — Cut-off Testing: Tests financial period cut-off — revenue and expenses recorded in the correct period. Specifically tests goods-in-transit, accruals completeness, and deferred revenue appropriateness.

  Layer 5 — Management Accounts Review: Compares the management accounts against prior period, budget, and industry benchmarks. Flags unexplained variances for auditor follow-up.

PA   Process & Operational Audit
Use Cases 11-16
Procurement, Inventory, Sales & Marketing, Capital Projects, HR Process & Operations


11
Procurement & Vendor Management Audit
PROCESS & OPERATIONAL AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits the procurement process — vendor selection, competitive bidding compliance, purchase order controls, contract management, and related party transactions — identifying fraud and control gaps.
  Procurement is the single highest fraud risk process in most organisations. AI-powered analytics cover every transaction.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Vendor Selection Compliance: Tests whether competitive bidding requirements were followed — all purchases above threshold had minimum 3 quotes, no single-source without documented justification, and no split orders to avoid thresholds.

  Layer 2 — Related Party Vendor Analysis: Cross-references the vendor master against employee, director, and key management personnel databases. Flags vendors with ownership connections to company insiders.

  Layer 3 — Purchase Order Compliance: Tests PO compliance — all purchases with a valid PO before receipt, no after-the-fact POs, approval within authority matrix, and PO not raised after invoice.

  Layer 4 — Contract Compliance: Tests that purchases are within contracted prices, volumes, and terms. Identifies off-contract purchases, price exceeding contract, and purchases from non-approved vendors.

  Layer 5 — Receiving & Approval Independence: Tests that the person who approves the purchase is not the same person who receives the goods (segregation of duties) and that all receiving is physically confirmed.

12
Inventory & Supply Chain Audit
PROCESS & OPERATIONAL AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits inventory management — physical count accuracy, valuation, obsolescence provisioning, movement controls, and supply chain resilience — ensuring inventory is accurately stated and theft/loss is minimised.
  Inventory is a major asset with significant exposure to theft, valuation error, and write-off manipulation.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Stock Count Observation: Plans and oversees the physical stock count — defining count teams, sample selection, count procedures, and variance investigation thresholds. Uses RFID / barcode scanning for accuracy.

  Layer 2 — Book vs. Physical Reconciliation: Reconciles the physical count against the book inventory. Classifies variances — count errors, unrecorded movements, theft, and system timing differences.

  Layer 3 — Valuation Testing: Tests inventory valuation — FIFO/WAC consistency, NRV comparison (for write-down requirement), and cut-off testing for goods in transit.

  Layer 4 — Obsolescence & Write-Off Testing: Analyses slow-moving and non-moving inventory. Tests that obsolescence provisions are adequate per policy and that write-offs are properly approved.

  Layer 5 — Movement Control Testing: Tests inventory movement controls — all movements documented, transfers approved, and FIFO picking sequence maintained. Analyses movement patterns for theft indicators.

13
Sales & Marketing Audit
PROCESS & OPERATIONAL AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits sales and marketing processes — channel incentive compliance, marketing spend effectiveness, customer credit and discount controls, and sales commission accuracy.
  Sales and marketing controls are frequently weak, with high fraud exposure in commissions, channel payments, and marketing spend.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Sales Commission Audit: Tests that sales commissions are computed per the approved commission plan — correct tier, correct revenue base, correct approval of exceptions, and no commission on cancelled or returned orders.

  Layer 2 — Channel Incentive Audit: Tests channel partner incentive payments — scheme terms compliance, actual performance vs. claimed performance, and backdating of target achievement.

  Layer 3 — Discount & Credit Note Controls: Tests customer discounts — whether within approved authority, whether supported by commercial justification, and no unusual concentration around period-end.

  Layer 4 — Marketing Spend Audit: Reviews marketing expenditure — vendor selection, event costs, digital media spend, and agency fees. Tests against market rates and flags spend on related parties or unusual beneficiaries.

  Layer 5 — Customer Credit Risk: Tests customer credit limit compliance — whether orders were processed despite exceeding credit limits, and whether credit limit approvals are within authority.

Capital Projects Audit
PROCESS & OPERATIONAL AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits capital investment projects — procurement compliance, cost control, milestone validation, capitalisation timing, and benefit realisation — protecting major capital expenditure from waste and fraud.
  Capital projects have some of the highest fraud risk and weakest controls in many organisations.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Project Approval & Scope Audit: Tests that projects were approved through the correct capital investment process — business case quality, board/management approval within authority, and scope defined with clear success metrics.

  Layer 2 — Procurement Compliance: Tests procurement within the project — competitive tendering, contractor selection basis, contract terms adequacy, and variation order approval.

  Layer 3 — Cost Control & Overrun Analysis: Monitors project cost vs. approved budget. Tests that cost overruns have proper re-approval. Analyses contract variation orders for inflation or scope creep.

  Layer 4 — Milestone & Quality Validation: Tests milestone payments — whether milestone completion was independently validated before payment. Reviews quality certificates and completion certificates for authenticity.

  Layer 5 — Benefit Realisation: For completed projects, compares actual benefits achieved against the business case. Identifies over-optimistic business cases, scope reductions, or delayed benefit realisation.

15
HR & Payroll Process Audit
PROCESS & OPERATIONAL AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits HR processes — recruitment compliance, employee data integrity, performance rating fairness, and separation controls — independently of the HR function to provide objective assurance.
  HR processes affect employee rights, regulatory compliance, and compensation integrity — independent audit is essential.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Recruitment Process Compliance: Tests that hiring followed the approved process — headcount approval obtained, JD posted internally first, selection based on documented criteria, and offer within approved grade band.

  Layer 2 — Performance Rating Distribution: Analyses rating distributions by department, manager, and level for statistical anomalies — systematic leniency, severity bias, and correlation between manager and team ratings.

  Layer 3 — Increment Compliance: Tests that increments are within approved bands, performance-based, and within the budget envelope. Flags above-band increments without documented CHRO approval.

  Layer 4 — Separation Compliance: Tests separation process — notice period served or waived per policy, F&F computation accuracy, and access revocation on last working day.

  Layer 5 — Statutory Compliance Testing: Independently tests statutory deductions and filings — PF, ESI, TDS, and Professional Tax — for a sample of employees. Cross-checks with TRACES and EPFO records.

16
Operations & Manufacturing Audit
PROCESS & OPERATIONAL AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits core operational processes — production controls, quality management, safety compliance, maintenance records, and environmental compliance — ensuring operational integrity and regulatory adherence.
  Operational failures can result in safety incidents, regulatory penalties, product liability, and reputational damage.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Production Control Testing: Reviews production orders — material consumption vs. standard, yield variance, scrap rates, and production downtime. Identifies systematic over-reporting of scrap (potential theft disguise).

  Layer 2 — Quality Management Audit: Tests the QMS process — incoming inspection records, in-process inspection, final inspection, calibration records for equipment, and customer complaint handling.

  Layer 3 — Safety & EHS Compliance: Reviews safety inspection records, incident reports, near-miss logs, and statutory safety audits. Tests compliance with Factories Act, OSHA equivalent, and environmental regulations.

  Layer 4 — Maintenance Compliance: Audits preventive maintenance schedules — whether PMs are performed on time, maintenance work orders properly approved, and spare parts procurement through approved channels.

  Layer 5 — Environmental Compliance: Tests environmental compliance — effluent treatment records, emissions monitoring, hazardous waste disposal, and CPCB/SPCB regulatory filings.

IT   IT & Cybersecurity Audit
Use Cases 17-21
ITGC, Cybersecurity & DPDP, ERP Application Controls, Continuous Auditing & Third-Party Risk


17
IT General Controls (ITGC) Audit
IT & CYBERSECURITY AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits the IT General Controls that underpin the reliability of all application controls — user access management, change management, data backup, and IT operations — providing the foundation for financial audit reliance on automated controls.
  Weak ITGCs mean that no automated application control can be relied upon — making ITGC the highest-leverage IT audit objective.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — User Access Review: Extracts all system user accounts across key applications (ERP, banking, HR). Tests for terminated user accounts still active, users with excessive privileges, dormant accounts, and shared accounts.

  Layer 2 — Privileged Access Review: Reviews privileged accounts (admin, DBA, root) — are they justified by role, are activities logged, is access time-limited, and are privileged activities reviewed by a separate control owner?

  Layer 3 — Change Management Testing: Tests the IT change management process — all changes logged, tested in non-production before deployment, approved before promotion to production, and emergency changes reviewed post-implementation.

  Layer 4 — Backup & Recovery: Tests backup procedures — are backups performed per schedule, are test restores performed quarterly, and is the recovery time objective (RTO) achievable?

  Layer 5 — IT Operations: Reviews IT operations controls — incident management, capacity planning, availability monitoring, and patch management currency.

Excessive or unjustified privileged access flagged

Change Management Agent

Unauthorised or untested changes identified
05
Backup Agent

Missing backups and untested restores identified
06

Systems with critical unpatched vulnerabilities flagged

18
Cybersecurity & Data Privacy Audit
IT & CYBERSECURITY AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits the cybersecurity control environment — network security, endpoint protection, incident response readiness, and DPDP / GDPR data privacy compliance — identifying vulnerabilities before adversaries do.
  Cybersecurity incidents represent existential risk to the organisation — independent audit provides assurance the board cannot get from management alone.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Security Policy & Governance: Reviews cybersecurity policies, governance structure, and alignment with ISO 27001 / NIST framework. Assesses whether the CISO has adequate authority, budget, and board access.

  Layer 2 — Network & Perimeter Security: Reviews firewall configurations, network segmentation, remote access controls (VPN, MFA), and intrusion detection coverage. Tests for open ports and misconfigured rules.

  Layer 3 — Endpoint & Email Security: Reviews endpoint protection coverage, email security controls (anti-phishing, DLP), and removable media controls. Tests patching currency for endpoints.

  Layer 4 — Data Privacy Compliance: Reviews DPDP Act compliance — personal data inventory, consent management, data retention schedules, breach notification procedures, and Data Protection Officer appointment.

  Layer 5 — Incident Response Readiness: Reviews the incident response plan — comprehensiveness, last test date (tabletop exercise), notification chain, and regulatory reporting procedures.

19
ERP Application Controls Audit
IT & CYBERSECURITY AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits the controls embedded in the ERP system — automated input validation, segregation of duties in system roles, configuration settings, and interface controls — ensuring the ERP is configured to prevent rather than just detect errors.
  ERP application controls are the backbone of financial process integrity — misconfiguration creates systematic risk.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Segregation of Duties (SOD) in ERP: Extracts the full role-to-user matrix from the ERP. Runs SOD conflict analysis — identifies users with conflicting roles (e.g., can both create vendors and process payments) using a pre-defined SOD rule set.

  Layer 2 — Configuration Review: Reviews critical ERP configuration settings — payment approval thresholds, credit limit enforcement, bank account change controls, and period-close authorisations. Tests whether settings match the approved design.

  Layer 3 — Interface Controls: Tests all ERP interfaces — payroll to GL, HR to payroll, supply chain to finance — for completeness (all records transferred), accuracy (no transformation errors), and timeliness (no significant lag).

  Layer 4 — Programme Change Controls: Reviews ERP programme changes — all changes logged, tested, approved, and traced to a valid change request. No unauthorised changes to production environment.

  Layer 5 — Automated Control Reliance Assessment: Assesses which ERP automated controls are reliable enough for financial statement reliance — and communicates results to the external auditor to optimise the overall audit approach.
