20
Data Analytics & Continuous Auditing
IT & CYBERSECURITY AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Builds and operates a continuous auditing engine — running automated tests on transactions 24/7 and alerting auditors to anomalies in near-real-time rather than waiting for the annual audit.
  Continuous auditing transforms internal audit from a periodic reviewer to a real-time control monitoring function.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Analytics Use Case Library: Maintains a library of audit analytics scripts covering all high-risk areas — AP duplicates, revenue recognition timing, payroll anomalies, inventory shrinkage, and expense policy violations.

  Layer 2 — Data Pipeline Management: Manages secure, automated data feeds from ERP, HRMS, expense systems, and other sources into the audit analytics environment. Monitors data quality and pipeline health.

  Layer 3 — Automated Test Execution: Runs analytics tests on a scheduled basis — some daily (high-risk transaction tests), some weekly (KPI monitoring), some monthly (period-end tests). Logs all results with timestamps.

  Layer 4 — Exception Triage: Applies a triage model to analytics results — separates true anomalies from false positives based on historical patterns. Routes only genuine exceptions to auditors for follow-up.

  Layer 5 — Trend & Pattern Learning: Learns from auditor follow-up decisions — which exceptions were genuine findings vs. false positives — to improve triage accuracy over time. Identifies new risk patterns for new analytics tests.

Execution Agent

21
Third-Party & Vendor Risk Audit
IT & CYBERSECURITY AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits the company's third-party risk management process — vendor onboarding due diligence, ongoing monitoring, contractual compliance, and exit management — particularly for vendors with access to company data and systems.
  Third-party breaches are the most common source of major cyber incidents and regulatory penalties.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Vendor Risk Inventory: Builds a complete inventory of third parties — vendors, service providers, cloud providers, and business partners — classified by risk tier based on data access, criticality, and contract value.

  Layer 2 — Onboarding Due Diligence: Reviews whether new high-risk vendors underwent adequate due diligence — security assessment, financial stability check, reference verification, and legal and compliance screening.

  Layer 3 — Contractual Data Protection: Reviews vendor contracts for adequate data protection provisions — NDA, data processing agreement (DPDP compliant), breach notification obligation, audit rights, and data return/destruction on exit.

  Layer 4 — Ongoing Monitoring: Reviews the ongoing monitoring process for high-risk vendors — annual security reassessment, financial health monitoring, adverse media monitoring, and SLA compliance.

  Layer 5 — Concentration Risk: Analyses vendor concentration — single vendors for critical processes, vendors in high-risk geographies, and substitute availability. Flags unacceptable concentration.

Regulatory Compliance Audit
COMPLIANCE & REGULATORY AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits compliance with all key regulatory requirements applicable to the company — SEBI, RBI, Companies Act, sector-specific regulations — providing independent assurance to the board and management.
  Regulatory non-compliance can result in licence revocation, prosecution, and reputational damage — independent audit provides the earliest warning.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Regulatory Universe: Builds and maintains a complete map of all applicable regulations — based on industry, geography, listing status, and business activities. Tracks compliance obligations per regulation.

  Layer 2 — Key Obligation Testing: For each material regulation, tests the key compliance obligations — SEBI insider trading policy compliance, LODR quarterly filings, Companies Act board meeting requirements, RBI reporting for NBFC.

  Layer 3 — Regulatory Filing Completeness: Tests that all required regulatory filings are made on time — ROC filings, SEBI filings, BSE/NSE disclosures, and sector regulator returns.

  Layer 4 — Regulator Correspondence Review: Reviews all regulator notices, queries, and inspection reports. Assesses management responses and tracks remediation to completion.

  Layer 5 — Emerging Regulatory Risk: Monitors upcoming regulatory changes that will affect the company's compliance obligations. Prepares readiness assessments for new regulations before their effective date.

Anti-Money Laundering (AML) Compliance Audit
COMPLIANCE & REGULATORY AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits the Anti-Money Laundering programme — KYC processes, transaction monitoring, STR filing, and PMLA compliance — for applicable businesses including NBFCs, fintech entities, and high-value goods dealers.
  AML failures attract severe regulatory penalties and reputational damage.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — KYC Programme Audit: Tests the KYC process — completeness of KYC documentation, re-KYC frequency for existing customers, risk-based CDD (Customer Due Diligence) application, and PEP/sanctions screening at onboarding.

  Layer 2 — Transaction Monitoring: Reviews the transaction monitoring system — alert generation logic, threshold calibration, alert disposition quality, and escalation procedures. Tests for false negative rates.

  Layer 3 — STR Filing Compliance: Tests Suspicious Transaction Report filing — whether STRs are filed within the 7-day window after identifying suspicion, quality of STR narratives, and non-tipping-off compliance.

  Layer 4 — AML Programme Governance: Reviews AML governance — PMLO appointment, AML policy currency, staff training completion, and board oversight.

  Layer 5 — High-Risk Customer Review: Reviews the high-risk customer portfolio — are enhanced due diligence procedures applied, are relationships reviewed annually, and are any relationships that should be exited still active?

SEBI & Corporate Governance Audit
COMPLIANCE & REGULATORY AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits SEBI LODR compliance, insider trading code adherence, related party transaction approval processes, and board governance practices — providing assurance on corporate governance quality.
  Corporate governance failures result in investor loss of confidence, regulatory action, and personal liability for directors.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — LODR Compliance Testing: Tests compliance with SEBI LODR — quarterly financial result disclosures within 45/60 days, material event disclosures within 24 hours, corporate governance report accuracy, and XBRL filing timeliness.

  Layer 2 — Insider Trading Code: Reviews the Insider Trading Code implementation — UPSI identification process, trading window management, pre-clearance compliance for designated persons, and SEBI disclosure filings.

  Layer 3 — Related Party Transactions: Tests RPT approval process — all material RPTs approved by Audit Committee and shareholders (if required), disclosure in annual report, and arm's length pricing basis documented.

  Layer 4 — Board & Committee Governance: Reviews board meeting compliance — minimum frequency, quorum, independent director participation, agenda quality, and minutes accuracy.

  Layer 5 — Director Disclosure Testing: Tests completeness of director disclosures — Form DIR-8 (annual disqualification), interest declarations, RPT interest disclosures, and shareholding disclosure under LODR.

Anti-Bribery & Corruption Audit
COMPLIANCE & REGULATORY AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits the anti-bribery and corruption compliance programme — gift and hospitality controls, agent and intermediary payments, facilitation payment risk, and Prevention of Corruption Act compliance.
  Bribery and corruption risk is high in India's regulatory environment — proactive audit reduces legal, financial, and reputational exposure.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Gift & Hospitality Register: Reviews the gift and hospitality register — completeness (all items declared), value compliance (within approved limits), and no patterns suggesting improper influence.

  Layer 2 — Agent & Intermediary Payments: Reviews payments to agents, intermediaries, and consultants — especially in regulated sectors and government-related business. Tests due diligence conducted and proportionality of payments.

  Layer 3 — Government Interaction Controls: Reviews controls around government interactions — who is authorised to interact with government officials, documentation of interactions, and no cash payments to government-related parties.

  Layer 4 — Procurement Red Flag Analysis: Analyses procurement transactions for ABC red flags — single-source contracts with government-connected vendors, unusual price premiums, and contracts won without competitive process.

  Layer 5 — Culture & Training: Reviews ABC programme adequacy — policy communication, training completion rates, speak-up mechanism effectiveness, and management tone at the top.

Contract Compliance Audit
COMPLIANCE & REGULATORY AUDIT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Audits contractual compliance — whether the company is performing its obligations under key contracts and whether counterparties are complying with their obligations — protecting the company from liability and recovering entitlements.
  Contracts represent significant financial and legal commitments — systematic audit recovers value and prevents liability.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Contract Obligation Extraction: Uses NLP to extract all key obligations from material contracts — payment terms, delivery milestones, SLA commitments, exclusivity provisions, and audit rights. Creates an obligation schedule.

  Layer 2 — Company Obligation Compliance: Tests whether the company is meeting its contractual obligations — payment on time, data protection commitments, confidentiality, and delivery milestones.

  Layer 3 — Counterparty Compliance: Tests whether counterparties are meeting their obligations — vendors delivering on time, service providers meeting SLAs, and licensees paying royalties correctly.

  Layer 4 — Financial Entitlement Recovery: Identifies contractual entitlements not being claimed — SLA penalty credits, volume rebates, price reduction triggers, and warranty claims. Quantifies unclaimed value.

  Layer 5 — Contract Renewal Risk: Identifies contracts with unfavourable terms coming up for renewal — above-market pricing, one-sided termination rights, or liability caps insufficient for the risk. Prepares renegotiation brief.

FD   Fraud Detection & Investigation
Use Cases 27-30
Fraud Risk Assessment, Real-Time Detection, Investigation Management & Whistleblower Programme


27
Proactive Fraud Risk Assessment
FRAUD DETECTION & INVESTIGATION


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Conducts a comprehensive fraud risk assessment across the organisation — identifying fraud schemes most likely to occur based on the business model, controls environment, and behavioural signals — to focus detection efforts.
  Fraud risk assessment is the foundation of effective anti-fraud programmes.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Fraud Scheme Identification: Maps the organisation's processes against known fraud schemes — ACFE fraud tree applied to the company's specific activities. Identifies the most likely fraud scenarios by process.

  Layer 2 — Red Flag Indicators: For each fraud scheme, identifies the behavioural and transactional red flags that would indicate it is occurring. Builds a library of red flag indicators with data sources.

  Layer 3 — Control Gap Analysis: Assesses the adequacy of anti-fraud controls for each high-risk fraud scheme. Identifies controls that are absent, ineffective, or easily overridden.

  Layer 4 — Inherent Fraud Risk Scoring: Scores each process area for inherent fraud risk — considering financial exposure, ease of perpetration, and likelihood based on control environment.

  Layer 5 — Detection Programme Prioritisation: Prioritises detection analytics based on fraud risk scores — ensures the highest-risk fraud scenarios have active data analytics running against them.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

01
Scheme Mapping Agent
ACFE fraud tree applied
Likely fraud schemes per process identified
02
Red Flag Agent
Red flag indicators identified
Data sources for each indicator mapped
03

28
Real-Time Fraud Detection Analytics
FRAUD DETECTION & INVESTIGATION


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Runs automated fraud detection analytics continuously across all high-risk transaction streams — AP, payroll, expenses, revenue, and procurement — alerting the audit team to anomalies in near-real time.
  Shifts fraud detection from post-incident forensics to in-process prevention and detection.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Multi-Stream Transaction Monitoring: Simultaneously monitors AP transactions, payroll runs, expense claims, revenue transactions, and procurement activities for fraud indicators — running hundreds of analytical tests continuously.

  Layer 2 — Behavioural Pattern Analysis: Establishes normal behavioural baselines for each process, department, and individual. Flags deviations — unusual approval activity outside business hours, sudden spike in expense claims, or unusual vendor payment patterns.

  Layer 3 — Network Analysis: Maps relationships between employees, vendors, customers, and transactions. Detects collusion patterns — employees connected to vendors via personal relationships, circular payment patterns, and kickback networks.

  Layer 4 — Machine Learning Anomaly Detection: Applies unsupervised ML (isolation forest, autoencoder) to detect novel fraud patterns not covered by rule-based tests. Continuously learns from confirmed fraud cases.

  Layer 5 — Alert Prioritisation & Triage: Scores each alert by fraud probability, financial exposure, and urgency. Delivers a prioritised investigation queue to the audit team — ensuring the highest-risk cases get immediate attention.

29
Fraud Investigation Management
FRAUD DETECTION & INVESTIGATION


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages fraud investigations from initial trigger through evidence collection, interview coordination, finding documentation, and recovery/remediation — ensuring investigations are legally sound, confidential, and conclusive.
  A poorly conducted investigation can be worse than no investigation — legal exposure from improper methods, tipping off suspects, or chain of custody failures.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Investigation Scoping: Upon receiving a fraud allegation or detection alert, defines the investigation scope — the alleged fraud scheme, affected period, potential perpetrators, systems to be reviewed, and evidence preservation plan.

  Layer 2 — Evidence Preservation: Immediately executes the evidence preservation plan — forensic capture of digital evidence, preservation of physical documents, and notification to IT to prevent deletion of relevant data.

  Layer 3 — Data Analytics Investigation: Runs forensic analytics on the preserved data — quantifying the fraud, identifying all transactions involved, mapping the timeline, and identifying all individuals who may have been involved.

  Layer 4 — Interview Planning & Execution: Prepares structured interview guides — starting with witnesses and ending with suspects. Records interview notes accurately. Manages the sequencing to prevent tipping off suspects prematurely.

  Layer 5 — Investigation Report & Recovery: Produces a legally sound investigation report — findings, evidence, timeline, responsible parties, quantum of loss, and recommended remedial actions. Initiates recovery and disciplinary/legal action.

30
Whistleblower Programme Management
FRAUD DETECTION & INVESTIGATION


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the whistleblower/vigil mechanism — secure intake, triage, investigation, and protection for complainants — ensuring the programme is trustworthy, effective, and compliant with Companies Act requirements.
  An effective whistleblower programme is the single most effective fraud detection mechanism — more effective than internal audit, external audit, or management review combined.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Secure Intake Channel: Maintains a multi-channel whistleblower intake — web portal, email, phone hotline, and physical drop box. All channels guarantee anonymity. Acknowledges receipt within 48 hours.

  Layer 2 — Complaint Classification: Classifies each complaint — fraud and financial crime, workplace harassment, regulatory violation, safety concern, or conflict of interest. Assigns priority based on severity and credibility.

  Layer 3 — Investigation Routing: Routes complaints to the appropriate investigator — CAE for financial fraud, CHRO for HR matters, Audit Committee for allegations against senior management, and external investigators for independence.

  Layer 4 — Non-Retaliation Protection: Monitors for potential retaliation against identified complainants — unusual adverse employment actions, performance rating drops, or disciplinary actions that follow closely after a complaint.

  Layer 5 — Programme Effectiveness Measurement: Tracks programme metrics — complaint volume, category distribution, investigation completion rate, outcomes, and resolution time. Reports to Audit Committee on programme health.

31
Audit Finding Documentation & Rating
AUDIT REPORTING & FOLLOW-UP


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Standardises the documentation and rating of audit findings — ensuring every finding is clearly articulated, accurately rated, supported by evidence, and positioned for management action.
  Poorly documented findings are often dismissed by management or fail to drive action — quality documentation is as important as quality testing.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Finding Structuring: Structures every audit finding using the standard format — Condition (what we found), Criteria (what should happen), Cause (why it's happening), Consequence (what could go wrong), and Recommendation (what to do).

  Layer 2 — Risk Rating: Rates each finding by risk using a calibrated framework — Critical (potential financial loss > ₹1 Cr or regulatory licence risk), High (significant control failure), Medium (control gap with compensating control), Low (best practice improvement).

  Layer 3 — Evidence Tagging: Links each finding to the specific evidence that supports it — transaction IDs, screenshots, documents, interview notes, and analytical outputs. Evidence is preserved and accessible for management response.

  Layer 4 — Management Response Collection: Sends findings to management for response — specific action planned, owner, and due date. Reviews management response for adequacy — the action must address the root cause, not just the symptom.

  Layer 5 — Finding Consistency Check: Compares each finding against the historical findings database — is this a repeat finding? Is the risk rating consistent with similar findings? Are the recommendations consistent with prior practice?

32
Audit Report Production
AUDIT REPORTING & FOLLOW-UP


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Produces high-quality, clear, and concise audit reports — converting the fieldwork findings into a board-ready document that communicates risk, assurance, and recommendations effectively.
  The audit report is internal audit's primary product — its quality directly determines whether the function is respected and its recommendations acted upon.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Report Structure: Assembles the report using the approved format — Executive Summary (for non-specialists), Audit Objectives & Scope, Overall Opinion, Findings (by risk rating), and Management Action Plan.

  Layer 2 — Executive Summary Drafting: Drafts an executive summary — key risks identified, overall control opinion, top 3 findings, and management commitment to remediate. Clear enough for a board member to act on without reading the full report.

  Layer 3 — Overall Opinion Assignment: Assigns the overall engagement opinion — Effective (no material findings), Partially Effective (some significant findings), Ineffective (material control failures). Validates that the opinion is consistent with the finding ratings.

  Layer 4 — Language Quality: Reviews report for clarity, tone, and factual accuracy. Flags passive voice, jargon, and ambiguous language. Ensures each finding is actionable and the recommendation is specific.

  Layer 5 — Management Review Cycle: Manages the report review cycle — draft to management, management response incorporation, CAE final review, and issued report distribution. Tracks to completion within the SLA.

33
Management Action Plan Tracking
AUDIT REPORTING & FOLLOW-UP


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Tracks all open Management Action Plans (MAPs) from current and prior audits — monitoring completion, validating evidence, escalating overdue items, and reporting status to the Audit Committee.
  MAP tracking is where audit value is realised — findings without remediation are simply observations.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — MAP Register: Maintains a live register of all open MAPs — audit area, finding, agreed action, responsible owner, agreed due date, and current status. Totals open by risk rating and age.

  Layer 2 — Due Date Monitoring: Monitors every MAP due date. Sends reminders to owners at 30, 14, and 7 days before due date. Automatically marks as Overdue the day after the due date.

  Layer 3 — Evidence Validation: When management marks a MAP as complete, collects and validates the supporting evidence — is the action actually done, or is it just claimed as done? Applies risk-based verification intensity.

  Layer 4 — Escalation Logic: For overdue Critical and High findings, escalates to department head (14 days overdue), CFO/CHRO (30 days overdue), and Audit Committee (60 days overdue without remediation).

  Layer 5 — Trend Analysis: Analyses MAP completion patterns — by department, business unit, and management owner. Identifies systemic low compliance departments for CAE and AC attention.

Audit Committee Reporting
AUDIT REPORTING & FOLLOW-UP


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Prepares and delivers the Audit Committee reporting package — plan vs. actual updates, key findings, fraud risk summary, MAP tracker, and Chief Audit Executive's assessment of the overall control environment.
  The Audit Committee reporting is internal audit's primary accountability moment — it must be insightful, balanced, and actionable.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Plan vs. Actual Update: Summarises audit plan delivery — audits completed vs. planned, explanations for any deviations, and resource utilisation. Includes upcoming planned audits for the next quarter.

  Layer 2 — Key Findings Summary: Compiles all findings issued since the last AC meeting — categorised by risk rating, business area, and finding type. Highlights any Critical or repeat findings requiring AC attention.

  Layer 3 — MAP Status Dashboard: Provides a visual summary of MAP completion status — total open by risk rating and age, overdue items requiring AC awareness, and trend in MAP resolution rate.

  Layer 4 — Fraud & Whistleblower Summary: Reports on fraud detection activity, investigations completed, and whistleblower programme metrics. Highlights any material fraud incidents or emerging fraud risks.

  Layer 5 — CAE Control Opinion: Provides the CAE's overall assessment of the control environment — based on audit results, MAP completion trends, and risk monitoring signals. Rated: Strong / Satisfactory / Needs Improvement / Weak.

35
Quality Assurance & Improvement Programme (QAIP)
AUDIT REPORTING & FOLLOW-UP


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages Internal Audit's Quality Assurance and Improvement Programme — ongoing internal quality monitoring, periodic self-assessment, and periodic external quality assessment — in conformance with IIA Standards.
  QAIP is a mandatory IIA requirement and the mechanism by which internal audit holds itself to the same standards it applies to the rest of the organisation.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Ongoing Monitoring: Continuously monitors audit quality indicators — working paper completeness, finding quality scores, report timeliness, MAP closure rate, and AC satisfaction. Flags quality drops for CAE attention.

  Layer 2 — Engagement-Level QA: Reviews a sample of completed audits — are the work papers complete, is the evidence sufficient, do the findings support the opinion, and is the report clear? Provides feedback to auditors.

  Layer 3 — Annual Self-Assessment: Conducts the annual QAIP self-assessment against IIA Standards — for each standard, assesses conformance (full/partial/non-conformance), evidence, and improvement actions.

  Layer 4 — External Quality Assessment: Facilitates the periodic (at least every 5 years) external quality assessment — prepares materials, manages the assessor relationship, tracks recommendations.

  Layer 5 — Improvement Tracking: Tracks all QAIP improvement actions — from self-assessment findings, external assessment findings, and ongoing monitoring gaps. Reports progress to CAE and AC.

Internal Audit Annual Report & Charter
AUDIT REPORTING & FOLLOW-UP


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Prepares the Internal Audit Annual Report — summarising the year's work, key findings, assurance coverage, and value delivered — and reviews the IA Charter annually for adequacy and board approval.
  The Annual Report and Charter are internal audit's accountability document to the board — demonstrating value and securing the mandate for the coming year.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Annual Work Summary: Compiles the year's audit activity — all audits completed, advisory engagements, investigations, and continuous auditing outputs. Compares against the approved annual plan.

  Layer 2 — Key Findings & Value Report: Summarises the year's key findings — by risk type, business area, and finding category. Quantifies value identified — fraud recovered, overpayments identified, compliance risks prevented, and process efficiencies recommended.

  Layer 3 — Assurance Map: Presents the assurance map — which processes and risks received audit coverage, at what level, and what remains in the audit universe for future coverage.

  Layer 4 — IA Team Performance: Reports on the IA team — headcount, qualifications, CPE completions, certifications acquired, and staff turnover. Assesses team capability against the risk profile.

  Layer 5 — Charter Review: Reviews the IA Charter against current IIA Standards, the company's current risk profile, and board governance requirements. Recommends updates to ensure the charter remains fit for purpose and submits for board approval.

01
Contract Request Intake & Triage
CONTRACT LIFECYCLE MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Receives all contract requests from business units — new agreements, amendments, renewals, and terminations — classifies them by type, risk, and urgency, and routes them to the appropriate legal resource or standard template.
  Eliminates the unstructured email-based contract request process that leads to missed requests, incorrect templates, and legal team overload.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Request Intake: Reads contract requests from the legal intake portal, email, or ticketing system. Extracts key parameters — contract type, counterparty, estimated value, business purpose, and requestor details.

  Layer 2 — Contract Type Classification: Classifies the request — NDA, vendor agreement, customer contract, employment contract, JV agreement, licensing, lease, or loan agreement. Determines applicable template and standard terms.

  Layer 3 — Risk Tier Assignment: Assigns a risk tier to the contract — Tier 1 (high value/strategic, e.g., >₹1 Cr or data processing), Tier 2 (standard commercial, ₹10L–₹1 Cr), Tier 3 (low value, standard terms, <₹10L). Determines SLA and resource requirement.

  Layer 4 — Standard vs. Bespoke Assessment: Determines whether the request can be handled by a standard template (self-service) or requires lawyer involvement. Templates cover 70% of volume — NDA, standard vendor T&C, employment letters.

  Layer 5 — Assignment & SLA: Routes Tier 1 to a senior lawyer, Tier 2 to a junior lawyer or paralegal, and Tier 3 to self-service portal. Sets SLA targets — Tier 1: 5 days, Tier 2: 3 days, Tier 3: same day. Tracks against SLA.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

Intake Agent

Request parameters extracted and validated
02

Template and standard terms determined
03
Risk Agent

Value, sensitivity, and complexity assessed
04

Standard template identified or gap flagged
Self-service vs. lawyer assignment determined
05

Lawyer, paralegal, or self-service activated
06

07

All open requests tracked with status and owner

Outcome:  Legal request turnaround improved 60%. Self-service handles 70% of volume. Legal team focused on high-risk work. SLA breach rate < 5%.


02
Contract Drafting & Template Management
CONTRACT LIFECYCLE MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Generates first-draft contracts using AI-powered drafting — drawing from approved clause libraries, standard templates, and playbooks — reducing drafting time by 70% while maintaining legal quality.
  Transforms lawyers from drafters to reviewers, dramatically increasing throughput without adding headcount.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Template Selection: Based on the contract type, jurisdiction, and counterparty profile, selects the most appropriate template from the approved template library. Applies jurisdiction-specific variations automatically.

  Layer 2 — Parameter Extraction: Reads the contract request parameters — parties, commercial terms (price, payment terms, duration), delivery obligations, SLAs, and special conditions — and populates the template with these specifics.

  Layer 3 — Clause Library Application: For each contract section, selects the appropriate clause variant from the approved clause library — standard, enhanced (more protective), or lightweight — based on the risk tier and counterparty type.

  Layer 4 — Playbook Compliance: Checks the draft against the legal playbook — minimum acceptable positions for each key clause (liability cap, indemnity, governing law, jurisdiction, IP ownership, data protection). Flags any draft clauses below the minimum.

  Layer 5 — Template Currency Check: Validates that the template being used reflects the latest approved version — incorporating recent legal changes (DPDP, Indian Contract Act amendments) and policy updates.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

Template Agent

Jurisdiction and type variations applied

All variable fields populated from request
03
Clause Agent

Risk-appropriate clauses inserted
04
Playbook Agent

Below-minimum positions flagged
05
Currency Agent

Latest approved version confirmed
06
Draft Agent

Complete draft ready for lawyer review
07

All deviations from standard highlighted for lawyer

Outcome:  Contract drafting time from 3 days to 4 hours. Template library always current. Playbook compliance automated. Lawyer time freed for complex negotiation.


03
Contract Review & Redline Analysis
CONTRACT LIFECYCLE MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Reviews counterparty-paper contracts — identifying deviations from the company's standard positions, flagging high-risk clauses, and generating a negotiation redline with recommended positions — in minutes rather than hours.
  Ensures consistent application of the legal playbook across all counterparty-paper contracts, regardless of which lawyer reviews them.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Document Ingestion: Reads the counterparty-provided contract (PDF or Word). Extracts the full text, identifies clause headings, and maps the contract structure to the standard clause taxonomy.

  Layer 2 — Playbook Gap Analysis: Compares each counterparty clause against the company's playbook position. Classifies deviations — Acceptable (within standard range), Negotiation Point (deviate from preferred but acceptable with modification), Deal Breaker (must be changed).

  Layer 3 — High-Risk Clause Detection: Flags specific high-risk clause types — uncapped liability, unilateral termination rights, IP assignment to counterparty, unfavourable data processing terms, and jurisdiction clauses pointing to unfavourable courts.

  Layer 4 — Recommended Redline: For each negotiation point, generates the recommended redline language from the clause library. Provides the justification and negotiation strategy — what to prioritise and what to concede.

  Layer 5 — Risk Summary Report: Produces a one-page risk summary — overall contract risk rating (Red/Amber/Green), top 5 risk issues, deal-breaker clauses, and commercial considerations for the business team.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

04
Contract Negotiation & Execution Management
CONTRACT LIFECYCLE MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the negotiation workflow — tracking redline versions, monitoring negotiation progress, flagging concessions beyond approved limits, and managing the e-signature execution process to contract-signed status.
  Provides complete version control and negotiation audit trail while accelerating time-to-signature.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Version Control: Tracks every iteration of the contract — counterparty redlines, company responses, and agreed positions. Maintains a clean version history with timestamps and authors.

  Layer 2 — Negotiation Progress Monitoring: Tracks open issues across negotiation rounds. Identifies clauses that have been agreed, clauses under negotiation, and new issues raised by counterparty. Flags stalled negotiations.

  Layer 3 — Concession Monitoring: Monitors agreed deviations from the playbook during negotiation. When concessions exceed the approved authority level, triggers escalation to senior lawyer or GC before agreeing.

  Layer 4 — Commercial-Legal Alignment: Maintains visibility of commercial terms agreed between business teams separately from legal drafting. Flags when commercial agreements are not reflected in contract language.

  Layer 5 — Execution Workflow: Once all issues are resolved, manages the execution process — final clean version, execution authority check (within signing authority matrix), e-signature routing (DocuSign / SignDesk), and execution confirmation.

05
Contract Repository & Obligation Management
CONTRACT LIFECYCLE MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Maintains the central contract repository — extracting key terms from all executed contracts, tracking obligations and milestones, and alerting responsible owners before deadlines and renewal dates.
  Turns the contract repository from an archive into a live obligation management system that protects the company from missing commitments.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Contract Ingestion & Metadata Extraction: Reads all executed contracts in the repository. Uses NLP to extract key metadata — parties, effective date, expiry date, auto-renewal clauses, value, governing law, key obligations, and notice period for termination.

  Layer 2 — Obligation Tagging: Identifies and tags every obligation in each contract — payment milestones, delivery timelines, reporting requirements, insurance maintenance, exclusivity obligations, and performance benchmarks.

  Layer 3 — Owner Assignment: Assigns each obligation to the responsible business owner — procurement for vendor delivery, finance for payment terms, IT for technical SLAs, and legal for legal notice requirements.

  Layer 4 — Renewal & Expiry Tracking: Monitors all contract expiry and auto-renewal dates. Sends alerts at 90, 60, and 30 days before expiry. Flags auto-renewal contracts where the cancellation window is approaching.

  Layer 5 — Obligation Status Monitoring: Tracks completion of obligations by responsible owners. Flags overdue obligations with risk impact (breach risk, SLA penalty exposure, or lost entitlement). Escalates to GC for critical misses.

Contract Analytics & Performance Reporting
CONTRACT LIFECYCLE MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Analyses the contract portfolio — cycle times, compliance rates, risk exposure, counterparty performance, and legal team productivity — providing GC and CFO with data-driven insights on contract management performance.
  Makes the legal function measurable and accountable, enabling continuous improvement.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Cycle Time Analytics: Measures contract lifecycle times — intake to first draft, draft to execution, and overall cycle time. Benchmarks against targets and identifies bottlenecks.

  Layer 2 — Risk Exposure Dashboard: Aggregates the liability cap, indemnity exposure, and IP risk across all active contracts. Provides total contract risk exposure by business unit, counterparty, and contract type.

  Layer 3 — Playbook Compliance Analytics: Measures how often the legal playbook positions were maintained vs. conceded in negotiations. Identifies clause types where the company consistently loses negotiation ground.

  Layer 4 — Counterparty Performance: Tracks performance of key counterparties under their contracts — on-time delivery, SLA compliance, invoice accuracy, and dispute frequency.

  Layer 5 — Legal Team Productivity: Measures legal team output — contracts reviewed per lawyer, cycle times by lawyer, SLA compliance, and value of contracts managed. Benchmarks against industry.

07
Companies Act Compliance — Filings & Registers
CORPORATE & SECRETARIAL COMPLIANCE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages all mandatory Companies Act filings with the Registrar of Companies (ROC) — annual returns, financial statement filings, director disclosures, charge registrations, and event-based filings — with zero missed deadlines.
  ROC filing defaults attract automatic penalties and director prosecution — AI-driven deadline management eliminates this risk entirely.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Filing Calendar: Maintains a comprehensive Companies Act filing calendar for every entity — ADT-1 (auditor appointment), MGT-7/7A (annual return), AOC-4 (financial statements), DPT-3 (deposits), MSME Form 1, BEN-2 (beneficial ownership), and all event-based forms.

  Layer 2 — Trigger Event Detection: Monitors for events that trigger MCA filings — board changes (DIR-12), change in registered office (INC-22), charge creation (CHG-1), share allotment (PAS-3), and amalgamation orders.

  Layer 3 — Data Compilation: Compiles required data for each filing from Finance (for financial statement filings), HR (for director and employee data), and legal (for event details). Validates completeness before form preparation.

  Layer 4 — Form Preparation & Validation: Prepares MCA forms in the prescribed format. Validates against MCA portal validation rules before filing. Manages DSC (Digital Signature Certificate) routing to authorised signatories.

  Layer 5 — Statutory Register Maintenance: Maintains all statutory registers — Register of Members, Register of Directors, Register of Charges, Register of Contracts, and Register of Beneficial Owners — up to date and audit-ready.

08
Board & Committee Meeting Management
CORPORATE & SECRETARIAL COMPLIANCE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the full board and committee meeting lifecycle — scheduling, agenda preparation, board pack distribution, minute-taking, resolution management, and action tracking — ensuring statutory compliance and governance quality.
  Board governance is a key regulatory obligation and investor trust signal — AI-powered management ensures zero compliance lapses.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Meeting Scheduling: Plans the annual board and committee meeting calendar — ensuring minimum statutory frequency (board: once per quarter, gap not exceeding 120 days; audit committee: at least 4 times per year). Manages director availability.

  Layer 2 — Agenda & Board Pack: Compiles the agenda from all functions — Finance (accounts), Legal (regulatory matters), HR (people matters), and Strategy (business updates). Assembles the board pack with all supporting papers. Distributes 7 days before meeting.

  Layer 3 — Statutory Compliance Checks: Before each meeting, verifies quorum requirements, checks whether independent director participation meets the threshold, validates that agenda items comply with disclosure and related party transaction requirements.

  Layer 4 — Minutes Drafting: After the meeting, drafts minutes from the structured discussion notes — capturing resolutions, voting, dissents, and action items. Routes draft minutes to Chairman for review within 15 days.

  Layer 5 — Resolution & Action Tracking: Extracts all resolutions and action items from minutes. Routes resolutions for appropriate follow-up (e.g., board resolution for bank mandate sent to Finance). Tracks action item completion.

Director Compliance & KYC Management
CORPORATE & SECRETARIAL COMPLIANCE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages all director compliance requirements — KYC updates, disqualification checks, disclosure of interest, DIR filings, and DIN maintenance — for all directors across all group entities.
  Director non-compliance can result in disqualification, company strike-off, and personal liability — proactive management is essential.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Director KYC (DIR-3 KYC): Monitors annual DIR-3 KYC deadlines (September 30 every year) for all active DIN holders. Sends reminders from July 1. Manages the filing process including DSC validation.

  Layer 2 — Disqualification Monitoring: Checks all directors against MCA's disqualified directors list monthly. Alerts GC and board immediately if any director appears on the disqualification list.

  Layer 3 — Director Disclosure Management: Manages MBP-1 (director's interest declaration at each board meeting) and DIR-8 (annual disqualification declaration). Tracks submissions and follows up on non-submissions.

  Layer 4 — Appointment & Resignation Filings: Manages DIR-12 filings for all director changes — appointments, resignations, and changes in designation — within the 30-day prescribed period.

  Layer 5 — Multi-Entity Director Register: Maintains a consolidated director register across all group entities — tracking each director's positions, term lengths, rotation requirements, and maximum directorship limits (10 public companies).

Shareholder & Securities Compliance
CORPORATE & SECRETARIAL COMPLIANCE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages shareholder-related compliance — share allotments, transfers, transmission, buybacks, dividend payments, investor grievance redressal, and SEBI LODR disclosures — for listed and unlisted companies.
  Shareholder compliance failures attract SEBI enforcement action, investor litigation, and reputational damage.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Share Register Maintenance: Maintains the register of members — processing allotments (PAS-3), transfers (SH-4), transmission, and dematerialisation requests. Reconciles with NSDL/CDSL depository records.

  Layer 2 — SEBI LODR Disclosures (Listed Companies): Manages continuous disclosure obligations — quarterly results (within 45 days), material events (within 24 hours), related party transactions, corporate governance report, and annual report.

  Layer 3 — Dividend Processing: Manages the complete dividend payment cycle — board and shareholder approval, record date and book closure, IEPF compliance for unpaid dividends (7-year rule), and Form 15G/H for eligible shareholders.

  Layer 4 — Investor Grievance Redressal: Manages SEBI SCORES complaints and investor correspondence — acknowledgement within 3 days, resolution within 30 days, and escalation for complex grievances.

  Layer 5 — Insider Trading & Trading Window: Manages the insider trading compliance framework — trading window opening and closing announcements, pre-clearance requests from designated persons, and SEBI filing of transactions.

Subsidiary & Group Entity Management
CORPORATE & SECRETARIAL COMPLIANCE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages compliance across the entire group entity structure — tracking each subsidiary's statutory obligations, board compositions, dormant company filings, and cross-entity reporting requirements.
  Multi-entity compliance failures can cascade into group-level regulatory risk if not managed systematically.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Entity Register: Maintains a complete register of all group entities — CIN, jurisdiction, date of incorporation, authorised and paid-up capital, directors, registered office, activity status, and relationship to holding company.

  Layer 2 — Entity-Level Compliance Matrix: For each entity, maintains a compliance matrix — applicable acts, filing calendar, and status. Distinguishes dormant companies (Sec 455 compliance) from active subsidiaries.

  Layer 3 — Dormant & Strike-Off Management: For dormant entities, manages Form MSC-1 (dormant status) and MSC-3 (annual returns). For entities being wound up, manages the voluntary strike-off (STK-2) process.

  Layer 4 — Cross-Entity Reporting: Consolidates compliance status across all entities for the GC dashboard — which entities are at risk, which have overdue filings, and which have governance gaps.

  Layer 5 — Foreign Subsidiary Coordination: For foreign subsidiaries, coordinates with local counsel on equivalent local filings. Tracks global filing calendar. Ensures Indian holding company disclosures reflect subsidiary events correctly.

LDM   Litigation & Dispute Management
Use Cases 12-16
Portfolio Management, Pre-Litigation Resolution, Arbitration, Labour Disputes, Regulatory Enforcement


12
Litigation Portfolio Management
LITIGATION & DISPUTE MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Maintains a complete, live litigation register across all pending cases — tracking court dates, filing deadlines, lawyer assignments, provisions, and strategic status — giving GC and CFO real-time visibility of the company's entire legal exposure.
  Litigation that is not tracked is litigation that will be lost by default.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Case Registry: Maintains a comprehensive register of all litigation — civil suits, criminal complaints, labour court matters, consumer forum cases, arbitrations, and regulatory proceedings. Each case has a unique ID, parties, forum, subject matter, and risk rating.

  Layer 2 — Deadline Tracking: Tracks all court dates, filing deadlines, limitation periods, and appeal windows. Sends multi-level reminders to the responsible lawyer and external counsel — 30, 14, 7, and 1 day before each date.

  Layer 3 — Financial Exposure Mapping: For each case, maintains the claimed amount, the company's assessed probable exposure, and the legal provision required under Ind AS 37 (Provisions, Contingent Liabilities). Aggregates to total litigation exposure.

  Layer 4 — Strategy Classification: Classifies each case by strategy — Defend (strong grounds), Settle (cost of defence exceeds likely outcome), Accelerate (company has counterclaim), or Monitor (low risk, minimal action needed).

  Layer 5 — External Counsel Management: Tracks external law firm assignments, fee arrangements, billing against budget, and performance (win rate, turnaround). Manages conflict checks for new external counsel appointments.

13
Pre-Litigation Dispute Resolution
LITIGATION & DISPUTE MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages pre-litigation dispute resolution — demand notices, negotiations, mediation, and conciliation — to resolve disputes before court filing, saving time, cost, and relationship capital.
  70% of disputes can be resolved pre-litigation with the right process — but only if managed proactively and systematically.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Dispute Identification: Reads all inbound legal notices, disputes, and escalated complaints from commercial teams. Classifies by dispute type — payment, performance, IP, employment, and breach of contract.

  Layer 2 — Merit Assessment: Analyses the merits of the dispute — company's legal position, counterparty's position, contractual provisions (dispute resolution clause, governing law), and commercial context.

  Layer 3 — Resolution Options Analysis: Evaluates the optimal resolution path — direct negotiation, mediation, conciliation, expert determination, or arbitration. Considers cost, timeline, relationship, and precedent risk.

  Layer 4 — Demand Notice Management: Drafts and tracks all outbound legal notices — demand notices, notice under Section 138 (cheque bouncing), SARFAESI notices, and pre-arbitration notices. Tracks response and limitation periods.

  Layer 5 — Negotiation & Settlement Tracking: Supports the negotiation process — preparing position papers, computing settlement value ranges, tracking negotiations, and documenting settlement agreements with proper discharge language.

14
Arbitration Management
LITIGATION & DISPUTE MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages arbitration proceedings — from notice of arbitration through pleadings, evidence, hearing, award, and enforcement — as both claimant and respondent, under domestic and international arbitration rules.
  Arbitration is increasingly the primary dispute resolution forum for commercial disputes — dedicated management improves outcomes and controls costs.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Arbitration Clause Analysis: Reviews the dispute resolution clause in the underlying contract — seat of arbitration, governing rules (SIAC, ICC, LCIA, DIAC, or domestic), number of arbitrators, and applicable law.

  Layer 2 — Arbitrator Selection: Analyses potential arbitrators — background, prior awards in similar matters, availability, and any conflicts of interest. Prepares a ranked shortlist for the parties or appointing authority.

  Layer 3 — Pleadings Management: Manages the pleadings timeline — Statement of Claim / Defence, Counterclaim, Reply, and Rejoinder. Tracks all filing deadlines under the applicable rules and arbitral directions.

  Layer 4 — Document Management: Organises the complete case document file — correspondence, evidence, expert reports, and legal authorities. Creates an indexed hearing bundle. Manages the discovery and disclosure process.

  Layer 5 — Award Analysis & Enforcement: Reads the award and analyses it — quantum, reasoning, and costs. Computes the net recovery / liability. Initiates enforcement (Section 36 of Arbitration Act) or challenge (Section 34) depending on outcome.

15
Labour & Employment Dispute Management
LITIGATION & DISPUTE MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages all employment-related disputes — labour court matters, Industrial Tribunal proceedings, High Court writ petitions by employees, and employment arbitrations — coordinating with HR and external labour counsel.
  Labour disputes carry reputational, financial, and operational risk — systematic management prevents escalation and precedent-setting adverse decisions.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Dispute Classification: Classifies employment disputes by type — individual disputes (wrongful termination, wage claims), collective disputes (union matters, Section 9A notices under Industrial Disputes Act), and statutory claims (PF, ESI, gratuity disputes).

  Layer 2 — Labour Court Calendar Management: Tracks all dates across labour courts, Industrial Tribunals, and appellate courts. Coordinates with specialised labour law external counsel.

  Layer 3 — HR-Legal Coordination: Coordinates with HR on factual evidence — employment records, disciplinary files, termination orders, and service records. Ensures HR provides complete and accurate inputs to legal team.

  Layer 4 — Conciliation Support: Supports conciliation proceedings before the Labour Commissioner — preparing the company's statement of facts, position on workman's claims, and conciliation settlement terms if settlement is preferred.

  Layer 5 — Compliance Risk Monitoring: Analyses all labour disputes for systemic HR compliance gaps — if multiple similar claims are being filed, identifies the root cause and recommends policy or process change to prevent further claims.

16
Regulatory Enforcement & Show Cause Management
LITIGATION & DISPUTE MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages all regulatory enforcement actions — show cause notices (SCNs), adjudication orders, compounding applications, and prosecution proceedings from SEBI, RBI, MCA, FSSAI, BIS, and other regulators.
  Regulatory enforcement carries financial penalties, licence suspension, and personal director liability — structured management dramatically improves outcomes.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — SCN Intake & Classification: Reads all incoming show cause notices. Classifies by regulator, nature of alleged violation, potential penalty range, and response deadline. Assigns severity — Critical (licence risk), High (significant penalty), Medium/Low.

  Layer 2 — Violation Analysis: Analyses the alleged violation — whether the facts are disputed, whether the regulatory interpretation is correct, what mitigating factors exist, and whether similar matters have been decided by the regulator or courts.

  Layer 3 — Response Drafting: Prepares the SCN response — statement of facts, legal grounds, supporting evidence, precedent analysis, and mitigating factors (past compliance record, voluntary disclosure, cooperation). Routes for expert review.

  Layer 4 — Compounding Application: Where compounding is available and strategic, prepares the compounding application — admission of default, computation of compounding fee, and undertaking for future compliance.

  Layer 5 — Adjudication & Appeal: Attends adjudication hearings. Analyses adjudication order. Initiates appeal (CCI, SEBI SAT, Securities Appellate Tribunal, or appropriate court) where the order is adverse and has strong appellate merit.

Analysis Agent
Violation merit assessed
Company's legal position and defences identified

Evidence Agent
Supporting evidence compiled
Facts and documents assembled for response
04
Response Agent
SCN reply drafted
Legal grounds and mitigating factors articulated
05
Compounding Agent
Compounding feasibility assessed
Compounding fee and application prepared if strategic
06

Use Cases 17-21
Compliance Universe & Calendar, EHS Legal, Consumer Protection, Competition Law & FEMA


17
Regulatory Universe & Compliance Calendar
REGULATORY COMPLIANCE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Builds and maintains the complete regulatory compliance universe — every applicable law, regulation, and guideline — and creates a master compliance calendar with all filing, reporting, and procedural obligations.
  The foundation of all compliance activity — without a complete, current regulatory universe, compliance will always have gaps.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Regulatory Universe Construction: Builds the regulatory universe from the company's operations — industry (FSSAI, BIS, Drugs, Environment, Labour), geography (state-specific laws), listing status (SEBI), and sector regulation (RBI, IRDAI, PFRDA).

  Layer 2 — Obligation Extraction: For each applicable regulation, extracts all compliance obligations — periodic filings, annual returns, event-based reporting, registration renewals, and procedural requirements.

  Layer 3 — Due Date Calendar: Builds a master compliance calendar — all obligations with due dates, responsible owner, and regulatory authority. Incorporates state-specific variations and leap year adjustments.

  Layer 4 — Legislative Change Monitoring: Continuously monitors the regulatory environment for changes — new regulations, amendment notifications, circulars, and compliance guidance. Updates the universe and calendar within 48 hours of any change.

  Layer 5 — Compliance Health Dashboard: Provides real-time compliance health — % of obligations complied with on time, overdue items, and upcoming high-risk deadlines. Alerts GC to Critical and High-risk gaps.

18
Environmental, Health & Safety (EHS) Legal Compliance
REGULATORY COMPLIANCE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages legal compliance with environmental laws (Environment Protection Act, Water Act, Air Act, Hazardous Waste Rules), occupational safety laws (Factories Act, BOCW, POSH), and EHS regulatory filings.
  EHS non-compliance carries criminal prosecution of directors, factory closure, and severe reputational damage.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — EHS Regulatory Mapping: Maps all applicable EHS regulations per establishment — Environment Act, Water Act, Air Act, Hazardous Waste Rules, Factories Act, Boilers Act, and state-specific pollution control board requirements.

  Layer 2 — Consent & NOC Management: Tracks all environmental consents — Consent to Establish (CTE), Consent to Operate (CTO), and Hazardous Waste Authorisation. Manages renewal 6 months before expiry.

  Layer 3 — CPCB/SPCB Reporting: Manages all periodic reports to the Central/State Pollution Control Board — annual environmental statement (Form V), half-yearly monitoring reports, and incident reporting.

  Layer 4 — Factories Act Compliance: Manages Factory Licence renewal, annual returns (Form No. 21), half-yearly returns (Form 22), and maintenance of statutory registers (Register of Accidents, Register of Dangerous Occurrences).

  Layer 5 — EHS Incident Legal Response: When an EHS incident occurs, manages the legal response — statutory notification requirements (CPCB, SPCB, factory inspector), preparation of incident report, and defence strategy if enforcement action follows.

Consumer Protection & Product Liability Compliance
REGULATORY COMPLIANCE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages compliance with consumer protection laws — Consumer Protection Act, Legal Metrology, BIS standards, and e-commerce rules — and handles consumer forum complaints and product liability claims.
  Consumer protection non-compliance attracts CCPA action, mandatory product recalls, and class-action risk.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Consumer Law Compliance Audit: Reviews product labelling (Legal Metrology), advertising claims (ASCI and CCPA guidelines), warranty terms, and return/refund policy for compliance with Consumer Protection Act and e-commerce rules.

  Layer 2 — BIS & Standards Compliance: Tracks BIS licence renewals, mandatory certification requirements (BIS hallmark, ISI mark), and statutory testing requirements. Manages FSSAI licence for food businesses.

  Layer 3 — Consumer Forum Complaint Management: Tracks all consumer forum complaints — District Forum, State Commission, NCDRC. Manages filing of written statements, evidence, and arguments within prescribed timelines.

  Layer 4 — Product Recall Management (Legal): When a product recall is required, manages the legal aspects — CCPA / FSSAI notification, recall execution documentation, consumer communication, and disposal records.

  Layer 5 — Advertising Compliance: Reviews advertising content for compliance — no misleading claims, substantiation for health/performance claims, mandatory disclosures (paid partnership, side effects), and ASCI code compliance.

Competition Law Compliance
REGULATORY COMPLIANCE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages Competition Act compliance — cartel and anti-competitive conduct risk monitoring, merger control filings, dominance abuse assessment, and CCI investigation management.
  CCI penalties can reach 10% of average turnover — proactive compliance is essential for market-leading businesses.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Competition Compliance Programme: Manages the competition compliance programme — risk assessment for the company's industry position, staff training on prohibited conduct, and competitor interaction protocols.

  Layer 2 — Pricing & Distribution Review: Reviews pricing decisions, distribution policies, and dealer agreements for competition law risk — resale price maintenance, exclusive dealing, tying, and predatory pricing signals.

  Layer 3 — Merger Control: Monitors all M&A transactions for CCI filing thresholds — asset and turnover-based triggers. Prepares Form I or Form II filings within the prescribed 30-day pre-merger notification window.

  Layer 4 — Industry Association Participation: Reviews all industry association interactions — agenda, discussions, and outcomes — for cartel risk. Provides real-time guidance to executives before industry meetings.

  Layer 5 — CCI Investigation Response: Manages CCI investigations — Director General (DG) investigation cooperation, dawn raid protocol, response to information requests, and defence strategy for penalty proceedings.

21
Foreign Exchange & FEMA Compliance
REGULATORY COMPLIANCE


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages FEMA compliance — FDI reporting (FCGPR/FCTRS), ECB filings (Form ECB), ODI compliance (Form ODI), and annual declarations (FLA return) — ensuring all cross-border transactions are compliant with RBI regulations.
  FEMA compounding penalties and RBI enforcement actions are increasingly common — systematic compliance management is essential.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — FEMA Transaction Classification: Reviews every cross-border transaction — equity investment, loan, trade credit, guarantee, and remittance — and classifies under FEMA as current account or capital account. Determines applicable route (automatic vs. approval).

  Layer 2 — FDI Reporting: For inbound FDI, manages all RBI reporting — FCGPR (within 30 days of share allotment), FCTRS (within 60 days of transfer), and Annual Return on Foreign Liabilities and Assets (FLA — July 15).

  Layer 3 — ECB Compliance: For External Commercial Borrowings, manages Form ECB filings, monthly Form ECB 2 returns, and compliance with permitted end-use restrictions.

  Layer 4 — ODI Compliance: For Overseas Direct Investment, manages Form ODI filings, annual performance reports (APR) for foreign subsidiaries, and monitoring of ODI conditions.

  Layer 5 — Compounding Management: When FEMA violations are identified (often through RBI or bank flagging), manages the voluntary compounding application — computing the compounding amount and preparing the application.

22
Trademark Portfolio Management
INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the company's trademark portfolio — applications, renewals, watch notices, enforcement, and international registrations — ensuring brand assets are protected across all key markets.
  Trademarks are core brand assets — unmanaged, they lapse or get attacked by competitors and squatters.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Trademark Register: Maintains the complete trademark register — all marks by class and jurisdiction, application date, registration number, registration date, renewal date, and owner.

  Layer 2 — Renewal Management: Tracks renewal dates for all registered trademarks (10-year renewal cycle in India). Sends alerts at 12, 6, and 3 months before expiry. Manages renewal filing and fee payment.

  Layer 3 — Trademark Watch: Runs ongoing trademark watch services — monitoring TM Registry journal for new applications that are confusingly similar to the company's registered marks. Files oppositions within the 4-month window.

  Layer 4 — Infringement Monitoring: Monitors the market for trademark infringement — e-commerce platforms, social media, and physical goods. Generates cease-and-desist notices and manages takedown requests.

  Layer 5 — International Portfolio: Manages international trademark registrations via Madrid Protocol. Tracks country-specific renewal requirements and handles office actions from foreign trademark offices.

Patent & Design Portfolio Management
INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the company's patent and design portfolio — prosecution, annuity payments, commercialisation analysis, and enforcement — maximising the value of the company's innovation investment.
  Patent lapsal from missed annuities is an irreversible loss — systematic management prevents it entirely.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Patent Register: Maintains the patent register — all applications and granted patents by jurisdiction, filing date, publication date, grant date, annuity due dates, and legal status.

  Layer 2 — Annuity Payment Management: Tracks annuity payment dates for all granted patents (annual in India, varies internationally). Manages payment through patent agents. Never lets a patent lapse from non-payment.

  Layer 3 — Prosecution Management: Tracks examination reports and deadlines. Prepares responses to patent office objections in coordination with patent agents and R&D team. Manages the grant process.

  Layer 4 — Prior Art Monitoring: Monitors patent databases for competitor filings in the company's technology space. Alerts R&D to freedom-to-operate risks and competitive intelligence.

  Layer 5 — Portfolio Valuation & Strategy: Periodically assesses the portfolio for commercial value — which patents are being practised, which have licensing potential, and which are candidates for abandonment (saving annuity costs).

Trade Secret & Confidentiality Management
INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the legal protection of the company's trade secrets and confidential information — NDAs, employee confidentiality obligations, trade secret classification, and enforcement against misappropriation.
  Trade secrets are often more valuable than registered IP — but unmanaged, they are legally unprotectable.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Trade Secret Identification: Works with business units to identify and document the company's trade secrets — formulae, processes, customer lists, pricing models, and strategic plans. Creates a trade secret register.

  Layer 2 — NDA Management: Manages all NDAs — ensuring appropriate NDAs are in place before any confidential disclosure, tracking expiry dates, maintaining a counterparty NDA register, and following up on violations.

  Layer 3 — Employee Confidentiality Obligations: Ensures all employment contracts have adequate confidentiality and IP assignment clauses. Manages exit obligations — remind departing employees of post-employment restrictions.

  Layer 4 — Misappropriation Detection: Monitors for potential trade secret misappropriation — ex-employees joining competitors, unusual data downloads before resignation, and competitors launching suspiciously similar products.

  Layer 5 — Enforcement: When misappropriation is suspected, manages the evidence preservation and legal response — injunction application in civil court, complaint under IPC Section 408/420, and criminal remedies.

IP Licensing & Commercialisation
INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages IP licensing — both licensing-out the company's IP to third parties and licensing-in third-party IP — negotiating terms, tracking royalty flows, and ensuring compliance with licence conditions.
  IP licensing is a significant revenue and cost item that is often poorly managed, leading to royalty leakage and licence term violations.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — IP Inventory Valuation: Assesses the commercial value of licensable IP — which patents, trademarks, or know-how have third-party licensing potential. Prepares licensing strategy for GC and business.

  Layer 2 — Outbound Licence Management: Drafts and manages licence agreements — field of use, territory, exclusivity, royalty rate, sublicensing rights, quality control (for trademarks), and audit rights. Tracks licence compliance.

  Layer 3 — Royalty Management: Tracks all royalty receivables and payables — due dates, computation bases, minimum royalty obligations, and milestone payments. Reconciles royalty statements.

  Layer 4 — Inbound Licence Compliance: Monitors compliance with inbound technology licences — permitted use restrictions, territory limitations, sublicensing restrictions, and maintenance fee obligations.

  Layer 5 — Royalty Audit Rights: Exercises audit rights in licence agreements where royalty understatement is suspected. Coordinates royalty audit through external auditors. Recovers underpaid royalties.

DP   Data Privacy & Cybersecurity Law
Use Cases 26-29
DPDP Act, GDPR & International Privacy, Cyber Incident Legal Response, Digital Business Compliance


26
DPDP Act Compliance Programme
DATA PRIVACY & CYBERSECURITY LAW


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Implements and manages compliance with India's Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act 2023 — data inventory, consent management, privacy notices, data principal rights fulfilment, and breach notification.
  The DPDP Act introduces significant penalties (up to ₹250 Cr per instance) — structured compliance is essential for every data-processing business.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Personal Data Inventory: Maps all personal data processed — what data, from whom (data principals), for what purpose, where stored, how protected, who has access, and how long retained. Creates the Record of Processing Activities (ROPA).

  Layer 2 — Consent Management: Implements a consent framework — clear, specific, and freely given consent notices, purpose limitation, granular consent options, and easy withdrawal mechanism. Manages consent records.

  Layer 3 — Data Principal Rights: Sets up the mechanism for data principal rights — access request, correction, erasure, and grievance redressal. Manages requests within the prescribed timelines.

  Layer 4 — Data Fiduciary Obligations: Manages all Data Fiduciary obligations — Data Protection Officer appointment, data processing agreements with data processors, cross-border transfer restrictions, and Significant Data Fiduciary obligations where applicable.

  Layer 5 — Breach Notification: Manages the personal data breach response — detection, containment, impact assessment, and mandatory notification to the Data Protection Board within the prescribed period.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

01
ROPA Agent
Personal data inventory compiled
All processing activities mapped and documented
02
Consent Agent
Consent framework implemented
Lawful basis for all processing confirmed
03
Rights Agent
Data principal requests managed
Access, correction, and erasure requests handled
04
DPO Agent
DPO functions managed
DPO independently functioning with board access

DPAs in place for all third-party processors

27
GDPR & International Privacy Compliance
DATA PRIVACY & CYBERSECURITY LAW


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages compliance with GDPR and other international privacy laws (CCPA, PDPA, PIPL) for the company's cross-border operations — ensuring adequate protections for personal data transferred to and from foreign jurisdictions.
  International privacy violations attract massive fines and market access restrictions.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Cross-Border Data Flow Mapping: Maps all cross-border personal data flows — India to EU (GDPR), India to US (CCPA for California residents), India to Singapore (PDPA), and China (PIPL). Identifies transfer mechanisms in place.

  Layer 2 — Transfer Mechanism Compliance: Ensures appropriate transfer mechanisms for each jurisdiction — Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) for EU, certification schemes, and adequacy decisions. Updates for regulatory changes.

  Layer 3 — GDPR Data Subject Rights: Manages GDPR data subject requests from EU residents — SAR (Subject Access Request) within 30 days, erasure rights, portability requests, and objection to processing.

  Layer 4 — Privacy by Design: Reviews new products, processes, and technology changes for privacy implications. Conducts DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment) for high-risk processing. Advises on privacy-by-design architecture.

  Layer 5 — Regulatory Response: Manages responses to data protection authority investigations — DPC (Ireland), ICO (UK), and CNIL (France) — in coordination with local privacy counsel.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

Data Flow Agent

Transfer mechanisms per jurisdiction confirmed

28
Cybersecurity Legal Response & Incident Management
DATA PRIVACY & CYBERSECURITY LAW


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the legal dimension of cybersecurity incidents — IT Act obligations, CERT-In reporting, RBI/SEBI cyber incident reporting, forensic investigation oversight, and stakeholder communication.
  Cybersecurity incidents have significant legal obligations — failure to manage them correctly compounds the harm.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — CERT-In Reporting Obligations: Reads CERT-In guidelines on mandatory incident reporting — which incident types must be reported (within 6 hours for critical incidents), to which authority, and in what format.

  Layer 2 — Sector-Specific Reporting: For regulated entities, manages sector-specific cyber incident reporting — RBI for banks/NBFCs, SEBI for listed companies, IRDAI for insurance, and TRAI for telecom.

  Layer 3 — Forensic Investigation Legal Oversight: Manages the legal aspects of forensic investigation — chain of custody, external forensic firm instructions, scope limitation advice, and preservation of privilege.

  Layer 4 — Stakeholder Communication: Drafts legally reviewed communications to impacted customers, employees, and the board — balancing transparency obligations, defamation risk, and litigation posture.

  Layer 5 — IT Act & Criminal Complaints: Advises on IT Act (Section 43A, 66, 66C) remedies — when to file a complaint with cybercrime police, what evidence to preserve, and how to pursue civil and criminal remedies against attackers.

29
Digital Business Legal Compliance
DATA PRIVACY & CYBERSECURITY LAW


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages legal compliance for digital business operations — IT Act, Intermediary Guidelines, e-commerce rules, digital payment regulations, and UCC (Unsolicited Commercial Communications) compliance.
  Digital businesses face a rapidly evolving compliance landscape — staying current is critical to avoiding enforcement action.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Intermediary Compliance: Manages compliance with the IT (Intermediary Guidelines) Rules 2021 — Grievance Officer appointment, monthly compliance reports for significant social media intermediaries, and content takedown processes.

  Layer 2 — E-Commerce Compliance: Ensures compliance with Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules — seller information disclosure, returns policy, grievance mechanism, and dark pattern prohibitions.

  Layer 3 — Digital Payment Compliance: For fintech/payment businesses, manages PPI licence compliance, RBI payment aggregator guidelines, and NPCI rule compliance.

  Layer 4 — UCC & Marketing Compliance: Manages compliance with TRAI's TCCCPR (Telemarketing regulations) — DND scrubbing, sender registration, and template registration. Manages email marketing CAN-SPAM and DPDP compliance.

  Layer 5 — Website & App Legal Requirements: Ensures website and app legal requirements — privacy policy, terms of service, cookie consent, accessibility (WCAG), and app store compliance (Google Play / App Store policies).

30
External Counsel Management
LEGAL OPERATIONS & KNOWLEDGE MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages all external law firm relationships — matter intake, counsel selection, fee arrangements, invoice review, performance measurement, and panel management — optimising legal spend and quality.
  External counsel costs are significant and poorly managed in most companies — structured management typically saves 20-30%.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Panel Management: Maintains the approved external counsel panel — firms empanelled by practice area and geography. Manages RFP process for panel additions and annual panel review. Conflict checks on new matters.

  Layer 2 — Matter Assignment: When a matter requires external counsel, matches the matter type and urgency to the appropriate panel firm — practice area expertise, conflict-free, and rate efficiency.

  Layer 3 — Engagement Letter & Budget: Ensures every matter has a signed engagement letter — scope, hourly rates or fixed fee, budget, billing guidelines, and reporting requirements. Tracks actuals against budget.

  Layer 4 — Invoice Review: Reviews all external counsel invoices — validates against billing guidelines (no block billing, no excessive paralegal work, no travel markup), checks against matter budget, and approves appropriate invoices.

  Layer 5 — Performance Management: Measures external counsel performance — matter outcomes (win/loss rate), adherence to timeline commitments, proactive advice quality, and client service.

31
Legal Budget Planning & Cost Management
LEGAL OPERATIONS & KNOWLEDGE MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Plans and manages the legal department budget — internal costs (headcount, technology), external counsel spend, regulatory filing fees, and litigation provisions — providing GC and CFO with real-time legal cost visibility.
  Legal departments are often viewed as cost centres without visibility — budget management improves both control and stakeholder trust.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Annual Budget Planning: Builds the annual legal budget — internal team costs, anticipated external counsel spend by practice area, regulatory fees, IP maintenance costs, and litigation provisions. Benchmarks against industry.

  Layer 2 — Matter-Level Cost Tracking: Tracks legal costs at matter level — each litigation, transaction, or compliance project has its own budget and actual cost tracking. Enables cost attribution to business units.

  Layer 3 — External Spend Analytics: Analyses external counsel spend — by firm, practice area, matter type, and business unit. Identifies spend concentration and opportunities for alternative fee arrangements.

  Layer 4 — Litigation Provision Management: Tracks Ind AS 37 provisions for litigation — coordinates with Finance for quarterly provision updates, manages provision releases on favourable outcomes, and provision additions on adverse developments.

  Layer 5 — Value Demonstration: Quantifies legal department value delivered — litigation savings vs. claimed amount, regulatory penalty avoidance, contract value protected, and IP licensing revenue. Prepares value report for CEO and board.

32
Legal Knowledge Management
LEGAL OPERATIONS & KNOWLEDGE MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the legal team's knowledge base — precedent contracts, research memoranda, opinions, playbooks, and regulatory guidance — ensuring institutional knowledge is captured, searchable, and accessible.
  Knowledge management prevents reinventing the wheel on every matter and captures value from every lawyer's work.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Precedent Library: Maintains an organised, searchable library of all precedent contracts — organised by type, counterparty type, jurisdiction, and key clauses. Tags negotiation history and clauses won/conceded.

  Layer 2 — Legal Research Repository: Archives all legal research — opinions, memos, judgement summaries, regulatory guidance. Makes them searchable by topic, jurisdiction, and applicable statute.

  Layer 3 — Playbook Maintenance: Maintains all legal playbooks — contract negotiation, litigation strategy, regulatory response, and M&A checklist. Updates playbooks after each matter with lessons learned.

  Layer 4 — AI-Powered Legal Search: Provides natural language search across the full knowledge base — lawyers can ask 'what is our standard position on indemnity for SaaS contracts?' and get the exact precedent and playbook guidance.

  Layer 5 — Knowledge Currency Monitoring: Monitors the knowledge base for stale content — opinions based on overruled judgements, contracts with outdated clauses, and playbooks not updated for regulatory changes.
