19
Release Management & Deployment Orchestration
DEVELOPMENT & ENGINEERING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the software release process — from release planning through staging deployment, release gate checks, production deployment, rollback capability, and post-release monitoring — ensuring every release is controlled and reversible.
  Uncontrolled deployments are the #1 cause of production incidents — structured release management dramatically reduces deployment-related outages.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Release Planning: Builds the release plan — which features and bug fixes are included, deployment dependencies, required testing completion gates, stakeholder communication requirements, and rollback plan.

  Layer 2 — Release Gate Checks: Enforces release gates before production deployment — test coverage threshold, performance benchmark results, security scan clean, accessibility audit pass, and product manager sign-off. No deployment without all gates green.

  Layer 3 — Staged Deployment: Manages staged rollout — canary (1% of traffic), progressive (10%, 25%, 50%), and full rollout. Monitors key metrics at each stage before proceeding. Automatic rollback if error rate or latency exceeds threshold.

  Layer 4 — Feature Flag Management: Manages feature flags — enabling/disabling features without deployment, controlling rollout by user segment, and A/B test flag management. Prevents flag debt accumulation.

  Layer 5 — Post-Release Monitoring: Monitors post-release system health — error rate, latency, business metrics (conversion, engagement), and user-reported issues. Detects regressions within 15 minutes and triggers rollback if needed.

20
API & Integration Management
DEVELOPMENT & ENGINEERING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the company's API and integration ecosystem — API design standards, versioning, documentation, partner integration management, and API health monitoring — ensuring a reliable, well-documented API surface for internal and external consumers.
  Poor API management causes developer frustration, integration failures, and partner attrition — systematic management builds the ecosystem flywheel.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — API Design Review: Reviews new API designs against the company's API standards — RESTful conventions, naming consistency, error response standardisation, pagination patterns, and authentication requirements. Flags non-compliant designs before implementation.

  Layer 2 — API Documentation: Generates and maintains API documentation — auto-generates OpenAPI specs from code annotations, produces human-readable API guides, and maintains sandbox environments for developer testing.

  Layer 3 — API Versioning: Manages API versioning — enforces deprecation policies (90-day notice minimum), tracks API consumer usage by version, and coordinates migration campaigns when old versions are retired.

  Layer 4 — Integration Health Monitoring: Monitors all external integrations in production — API response times, error rates, authentication failures, and SLA compliance. Alerts on-call when integration health degrades.

  Layer 5 — Partner Developer Experience: Manages the partner developer experience — onboarding documentation, API key provisioning, developer support (tickets), and partner usage analytics. Identifies high-value partners for deeper integration investment.

21
Security & Compliance by Design
DEVELOPMENT & ENGINEERING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Embeds security and compliance requirements into the development process — threat modelling, OWASP testing, privacy impact assessments, and compliance validation — ensuring products are secure and compliant before they launch, not after.
  Security added after development is 10x more expensive and never as thorough — security by design is both better and cheaper.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Threat Modelling: Conducts threat modelling for new features and architecture changes — identifies attack vectors (STRIDE methodology), rates risk (CVSS scoring), and specifies security controls required before development begins.

  Layer 2 — Security Requirements: Translates threat model findings into specific security requirements — authentication, authorisation, input validation, encryption, session management, and audit logging. These are non-negotiable PRD additions.

  Layer 3 — Privacy Impact Assessment: For features that process personal data, conducts a DPDP/GDPR-aligned Privacy Impact Assessment — data minimisation, purpose limitation, retention schedule, consent mechanism, and third-party data flows.

  Layer 4 — Pre-Launch Security Testing: Conducts DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing) and targeted penetration testing on new features before launch. Severity-gates deployment on Critical and High findings.

  Layer 5 — Compliance Validation: Validates compliance requirements are implemented correctly — regulatory technical controls (PCI DSS for payments, HIPAA for health, SOC2 for enterprise), and internal security policies.

Product Launch Planning & Execution
PRODUCT LAUNCH & GO-TO-MARKET


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Plans and orchestrates product and feature launches — coordinating all launch activities across product, engineering, marketing, sales, CS, and legal — from launch readiness assessment through post-launch monitoring.
  Launches without coordination fail even when the product is excellent — systematic launch management maximises the impact of every release.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Launch Readiness Assessment: Evaluates launch readiness across all dimensions — product completeness, engineering quality gates, documentation, CS training, marketing materials, legal review, and pricing approval. Produces a launch readiness score with gap analysis.

  Layer 2 — Launch Timing Optimisation: Analyses launch timing factors — competitor activity, marketing calendar, customer seasonality, sales cycle alignment, and technical readiness. Recommends the optimal launch window with supporting rationale.

  Layer 3 — Launch Plan Coordination: Builds and tracks the launch plan — cross-functional task list with owners, due dates, and dependencies. Manages the launch readiness review meeting with all stakeholders.

  Layer 4 — Beta-to-GA Transition: Manages the transition from closed beta to General Availability — beta customer graduation, pricing activation, public documentation release, and support team enablement.

  Layer 5 — Launch Day Monitoring: On launch day, monitors adoption metrics (sign-ups, activations, first use events), technical health (error rates, performance), and customer feedback signals (CS tickets, social mentions) in real time.

23
Pricing Strategy & Monetisation Management
PRODUCT LAUNCH & GO-TO-MARKET


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages product pricing decisions — analysing willingness-to-pay, competitive pricing, value-based pricing modelling, and pricing experiment design — ensuring pricing maximises revenue while supporting product adoption.
  Pricing is the highest-leverage go-to-market decision — a 1% improvement in pricing typically improves profit by 8-11% — yet most product companies under-invest in pricing rigour.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Willingness-to-Pay Research: Designs and analyses willingness-to-pay research — Van Westendorp price sensitivity meter, Conjoint analysis, and direct questioning surveys. Identifies the acceptable price range and optimal price point for each customer segment.

  Layer 2 — Competitive Pricing Analysis: Tracks competitor pricing — list prices, packaging, discount patterns, and promotional pricing. Maps where the company's pricing sits on the value-to-price matrix relative to each competitor.

  Layer 3 — Value Metric Identification: Identifies the right value metric for the pricing model — what unit of value scales with customer success (seats, usage, outcomes, revenue processed). Analyses customer data to find the metric most correlated with value delivered.

  Layer 4 — Pricing Experiment Design: Designs pricing A/B tests — different price points, packaging variations, or discount structures — with statistical rigour. Monitors conversion, ARPU, and churn impact at each variant.

  Layer 5 — Pricing Decision Support: Synthesises research, competitive data, and experiment results into a pricing recommendation brief for the CPO and CFO — with revenue model, sensitivity analysis, and implementation requirements.

Sales & CS Enablement
PRODUCT LAUNCH & GO-TO-MARKET


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Ensures sales teams and customer success have everything they need to sell and support new products — product training, competitive battlecards, demo environments, objection handling guides, and implementation playbooks — always current and accessible.
  Products fail in the market not because they are bad but because the sales team doesn't understand them — systematic enablement maximises commercial impact of every R&D investment.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Enablement Content Creation: Creates all required sales and CS enablement content — product overview, feature benefits (in customer language, not technical spec), competitive comparison, common objections, discovery questions, and implementation guide.

  Layer 2 — Demo Environment Management: Maintains up-to-date, scenario-specific demo environments — pre-loaded with compelling data, scenarios tailored to each industry vertical, and always reflecting the latest product version.

  Layer 3 — Training Delivery: Manages sales and CS training programmes — live sessions for major launches, asynchronous modules for incremental features, and certification tests to verify knowledge. Tracks completion and scores.

  Layer 4 — Content Currency Monitoring: Monitors all enablement content for staleness — detects when product updates make sales materials inaccurate. Triggers update workflow and ensures sales never uses outdated positioning.

  Layer 5 — Enablement Effectiveness: Tracks enablement effectiveness — training completion rates, sales win rates before and after training, deal size, and time-to-close for trained vs. untrained reps. Identifies enablement content that drives outcomes.

Partner & Ecosystem Integration Management
PRODUCT LAUNCH & GO-TO-MARKET


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the product's partner ecosystem — integration partner management, marketplace listing, developer programme, and co-sell partnerships — growing the ecosystem to expand product reach and value.
  Partner ecosystems can multiply a product's market reach 5-10x — systematic management is required to realise this value.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Partner Opportunity Identification: Identifies integration and partnership opportunities — technology partners (complementary tools), distribution partners (channels with access to target customers), and OEM partners (embed the product in their offering).

  Layer 2 — Integration Prioritisation: Prioritises integration builds based on customer demand (how often do customers request this integration), partner reciprocity (does the partner promote the integration), and strategic fit.

  Layer 3 — Partner Onboarding: Manages the technical and commercial partner onboarding process — API access, sandbox environment, integration documentation, and go-to-market agreement. Tracks progress to first integration live.

  Layer 4 — Marketplace Listing Management: Manages all marketplace listings — Salesforce AppExchange, Atlassian Marketplace, Microsoft AppSource, and company's own marketplace. Keeps listings current, rating management, and review response.

  Layer 5 — Ecosystem Health Analytics: Measures ecosystem health — active integrations, integration usage by customers, partner-sourced leads, co-sell revenue, and integration NPS. Identifies high-value partners for deeper investment.

26
Product Usage Analytics & Feature Adoption
PRODUCT PERFORMANCE & ANALYTICS


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Instruments and analyses product usage data — feature adoption funnels, user engagement patterns, session analysis, and feature performance — giving PMs evidence on what users actually do vs. what they say they do.
  Usage analytics replace opinion with evidence in product decisions — the team that measures learns faster and builds better products.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Instrumentation Management: Manages product instrumentation — event taxonomy design, tracking implementation verification, data quality monitoring, and analytics SDK management. Ensures every feature is measurable before and after launch.

  Layer 2 — Feature Adoption Measurement: Measures adoption for every feature — Day 1, Day 7, Day 30 adoption rates by user segment and cohort. Compares to benchmarks and launch forecasts. Flags features with low adoption for investigation.

  Layer 3 — Funnel Analysis: Builds and monitors conversion funnels — onboarding funnel (sign-up to activation), feature discovery funnel (feature available to feature used), and value realisation funnel (first use to repeated use). Identifies high-drop-off steps.

  Layer 4 — Engagement Segmentation: Segments users by engagement level — Power Users, Regular Users, Occasional Users, and At-Risk (declining usage). For each segment, identifies what drives them up or down the engagement ladder.

  Layer 5 — Product-Led Growth Signals: Identifies product-led growth signals — viral loops (users inviting other users), expansion signals (approaching plan limits), and NPS-correlated usage patterns. Feeds into growth and sales strategies.

Customer Health Score & Retention Analytics
PRODUCT PERFORMANCE & ANALYTICS


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Computes and monitors customer health scores — combining product usage, support activity, NPS, and commercial signals — to identify customers at risk of churn before they decide to leave.
  Customer churn is largely predictable from product usage signals — detecting it 90 days in advance allows intervention that retains 30-40% of at-risk customers.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Health Score Model: Builds the customer health score model — combining product usage depth (features used, frequency, breadth), support health (CS ticket volume and tone), satisfaction signals (NPS, CSAT), and commercial signals (contract utilisation, expansion activity).

  Layer 2 — Health Signal Weighting: Weights health signals based on their predictive power for churn — validates weights against historical churn data. Ensures the model gets better over time as more churn outcomes are observed.

  Layer 3 — Churn Prediction: Applies the health score model to predict churn probability at 30, 60, and 90 day horizons. Segments customers into health tiers — Green (healthy), Yellow (monitor), Orange (at-risk), Red (critical churn risk).

  Layer 4 — Leading Indicator Monitoring: Identifies the leading indicators that predict churn 90+ days out — login frequency decline, feature abandonment, team contraction, executive champion departure. These are the early warning signals.

  Layer 5 — Retention Action Triggers: For at-risk customers, triggers specific retention actions — CS outreach, PM champion call, feature adoption support, or commercial intervention. Tracks action completion and outcome.

Revenue & Business Metrics Dashboard
PRODUCT PERFORMANCE & ANALYTICS


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Maintains the canonical product revenue and business metrics dashboard — ARR, MRR, NRR, churn, DAU/MAU, activation rate, and time-to-value — providing the CPO, CEO, and board with a consistent, trusted view of product performance.
  Metric inconsistency between teams destroys decision-making trust — a single, agreed, automated metrics layer solves this.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Metric Definitions: Maintains the canonical definitions for all product business metrics — how ARR is calculated, what counts as an activated user, how NRR is computed, and what the DAU/MAU ratio includes. Single source of truth.

  Layer 2 — Data Pipeline: Manages the data pipeline feeding the metrics dashboard — from raw product event data, billing data, and CRM data through transformation, validation, and loading. Monitors data freshness and quality.

  Layer 3 — Metric Computation: Computes all business metrics on the agreed cadence — daily for operational metrics, weekly for growth metrics, monthly for financial metrics. Validates computed values against expected ranges.

  Layer 4 — Trend & Anomaly Analysis: Tracks metric trends — week-over-week, month-over-month, and year-over-year. Detects anomalies (metrics that deviate significantly from trend) and alerts the CPO within 4 hours of detection.

  Layer 5 — Board & Investor Reporting: Produces the monthly board pack metrics section — formatted to board standards, with prior-period comparison, commentary on key movements, and forward-looking guidance update.

Product Quality & Reliability Engineering
PRODUCT PERFORMANCE & ANALYTICS


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages product quality and reliability — SLAs, incident management, error budgets, performance monitoring, and reliability targets — ensuring the product delivers on its availability and performance promises to customers.
  A product that doesn't work reliably destroys trust faster than any feature can build it — systematic reliability engineering protects customer trust.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — SLA Definition & Monitoring: Defines SLA commitments per product tier (e.g., 99.9% availability for Enterprise, 99.5% for Standard). Monitors real-time availability against SLA. Tracks monthly uptime and flags at-risk SLA months in advance.

  Layer 2 — Error Budget Management: Implements error budget tracking — available error budget per rolling 30 days, error budget burn rate, and error budget policy (freeze feature deployments when budget is 20% remaining).

  Layer 3 — Performance Monitoring: Monitors product performance — API response times by endpoint, database query performance, client-side load times, and mobile app performance. Sets performance budgets per endpoint.

  Layer 4 — Incident Management: Manages production incidents — detection, severity classification (P0/P1/P2), on-call escalation, incident bridge, customer communication, and post-incident review (PIR).

  Layer 5 — Post-Incident Review: After every P0 and P1 incident, manages the blameless post-incident review — timeline reconstruction, root cause analysis (5 Whys), contributing factors, and action items with owners and deadlines.

30
Product Localisation & Global Readiness
PRODUCT PERFORMANCE & ANALYTICS


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages product localisation — translation, cultural adaptation, regional compliance, and market-specific features — ensuring the product is competitive in every market the company serves.
  Many companies lose 20-30% of potential international revenue because localisation is poor or inconsistently managed.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Localisation Scope Management: For each new release, identifies all content requiring localisation — UI strings, documentation, marketing materials, and in-product help. Generates the localisation work order with word counts, context, and deadline.

  Layer 2 — Translation Quality: Manages the translation workflow — vendor management or internal localisers, quality review by native speakers, terminology consistency (translation memory), and glossary management.

  Layer 3 — Regional Compliance: Identifies and manages region-specific compliance requirements — data residency, right-to-left language support, local payment methods, regional date/currency/number formats, and regulatory requirements (e.g., EU AI Act, India data localisation).

  Layer 4 — Cultural Adaptation: Reviews UI and content for cultural appropriateness — imagery, colours, metaphors, and examples that may not translate culturally. Involves regional advisors for key markets.

  Layer 5 — Localisation Quality Assurance: Tests localised builds — truncation, text overflow, RTL layout, regional format rendering, and functional testing with local data. Tracks localisation quality score per market.

31
R&D Portfolio Management & Investment Allocation
R&D OPERATIONS & PORTFOLIO


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the R&D investment portfolio — balancing investment across innovation horizons (H1 core product, H2 adjacencies, H3 breakthrough), tracking ROI of R&D spending, and ensuring investment allocation is aligned to strategy.
  R&D portfolios without active management drift toward the comfortable and familiar — losing the breakthrough potential that justifies R&D investment.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Portfolio Categorisation: Categorises all R&D investment across three horizons — Horizon 1 (sustain and extend current products), Horizon 2 (build adjacent opportunities), and Horizon 3 (create breakthrough new capabilities). Tracks actual spend vs. target allocation.

  Layer 2 — Investment Tracking: Tracks all R&D investments at the project level — committed engineering time, third-party costs, infrastructure costs, and opportunity cost. Reconciles against the approved R&D budget monthly.

  Layer 3 — ROI Measurement: Measures the ROI of completed R&D investments — revenue generated or enabled, cost savings, competitive advantage preserved, and strategic optionality created. Connects investment to outcome.

  Layer 4 — Portfolio Rebalancing: Monitors allocation drift — when H1 crowd out H2 and H3 due to short-term commercial pressure. Flags rebalancing recommendations to CPO and CTO when allocation deviates more than 15% from target.

  Layer 5 — Investment Gate Reviews: Facilitates quarterly investment gate reviews — for each active R&D project, reviews progress vs. plan, updated ROI forecast, and strategic relevance. Recommends Continue, Accelerate, Pivot, or Kill.

32
Engineering Capacity Planning & Headcount Management
R&D OPERATIONS & PORTFOLIO


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Plans engineering headcount requirements — mapping roadmap ambition to capacity gaps, building the hiring plan, managing contractor and partner resourcing, and maintaining optimal team size and composition.
  Roadmaps that outrun capacity generate burnout and missed commitments — systematic capacity planning prevents both.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Capacity Demand Forecasting: Translates the product roadmap into engineering capacity demand — story point estimates, team composition requirements (frontend, backend, ML, DevOps), and timing. Produces a 12-month demand forecast by skill type.

  Layer 2 — Current Capacity Assessment: Assesses current engineering capacity — headcount by skill, average velocity per engineer, attrition risk (flight risk score), and current utilisation. Computes available capacity for the next 12 months.

  Layer 3 — Gap Analysis: Computes the gap between demand and supply — by skill, by quarter. Determines whether the gap should be closed by hiring, contractor augmentation, or roadmap scope reduction.

  Layer 4 — Hiring Plan: Builds the hiring plan — roles required, timing, expected time-to-fill, and budget. Prioritises hiring by skill criticality and lead time. Tracks hiring pipeline against the plan.

  Layer 5 — Team Structure Optimisation: Analyses engineering team structure — team sizes, domain ownership, cross-team dependency frequency, and Conway's Law alignment with the product architecture. Recommends team restructuring when needed.

Vendor & Technology Partner Management
R&D OPERATIONS & PORTFOLIO


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages R&D's vendor and technology partner relationships — SaaS tools, cloud providers, open-source dependencies, and technology licenses — optimising cost, managing risk, and ensuring the technology stack remains current.
  Technology vendor sprawl and unmanaged dependencies are a significant cost and risk for R&D teams — systematic management reduces both.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Technology Inventory: Maintains a complete inventory of all R&D tools and dependencies — SaaS subscriptions, cloud services, open-source libraries with licences, and commercial SDKs. Tracks cost, usage, and owner.

  Layer 2 — Licence Compliance: Monitors open-source licence compliance — identifies GPL, LGPL, and AGPL dependencies in commercial products. Flags licence conflicts. Manages CLA (Contributor Licence Agreement) for open-source contributions.

  Layer 3 — Cost Optimisation: Analyses cloud and SaaS spend — identifies unused resources, over-provisioned services, and opportunities for reserved instance or committed use discounts. Reports savings opportunities to CTO and CFO.

  Layer 4 — Dependency Risk Management: Monitors critical dependencies for risk — end-of-life dates, security vulnerability exposure (CVE tracking for all dependencies), and single-vendor concentration risk.

  Layer 5 — Vendor Performance: Tracks key vendor SLAs — cloud provider uptime, API availability, support response times. Manages vendor escalations and credits for SLA misses.

R&D Metrics, OKRs & Performance Management
R&D OPERATIONS & PORTFOLIO


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages R&D team OKRs, tracks key R&D metrics, facilitates performance management for engineering and product teams, and provides the CPO and CTO with data-driven visibility of R&D function health.
  R&D teams that are not measured cannot be improved — and cannot justify their investment to the business.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — OKR Management: Facilitates quarterly OKR setting for R&D — company OKRs cascade to R&D objectives and then to team and individual key results. Tracks weekly progress updates. Flags off-track OKRs 4 weeks before quarter end.

  Layer 2 — Engineering Metrics: Tracks the DORA metrics (Deployment Frequency, Lead Time for Changes, MTTR, Change Failure Rate) and product quality metrics. Benchmarks against industry standards.

  Layer 3 — Product Metrics: Tracks product success metrics — feature adoption rate, product NPS, DAU/MAU ratio, activation rate, and time-to-value. Connects product metrics to business outcomes.

  Layer 4 — Team Health: Monitors engineering and product team health — eNPS, manager feedback scores, psychological safety indicators, meeting load, and on-call burden. Flags teams at risk of burnout.

  Layer 5 — R&D Performance Review: Facilitates quarterly R&D business reviews — preparing the review pack, presenting R&D contributions to business outcomes, and making the case for continued investment.

35
Intellectual Property & Innovation Capture
R&D OPERATIONS & PORTFOLIO


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Identifies and protects intellectual property created by the R&D team — patent-worthy innovations, trade secrets, copyrightable works, and open-source contribution policy — ensuring the company captures the full value of its innovation investment.
  Most R&D teams create IP without capturing it — systematic IP management converts innovation into defensible competitive advantage.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Innovation Disclosure Process: Maintains an easy-to-use innovation disclosure process — engineers and PMs can submit innovation disclosures when they create something novel. Structured format captures the innovation, how it works, and why it's novel.

  Layer 2 — Patentability Assessment: For each disclosure, conducts a preliminary patentability assessment — prior art search, novelty assessment, non-obviousness evaluation, and utility determination. Recommends patent filing, trade secret, or no-action.

  Layer 3 — Patent Filing Pipeline: Manages the patent filing process — invention brief to patent counsel, application drafting, prosecution management, and grant tracking. Tracks the cost per patent vs. strategic value.

  Layer 4 — Trade Secret Classification: For innovations better protected as trade secrets (faster, cheaper, no disclosure), manages the trade secret classification — access controls, documentation, and employee awareness.

  Layer 5 — Open Source Policy: Manages the company's open-source contribution policy — what engineers can open-source (must not include trade secrets or patentable innovations), contribution review and approval, and CLA management.

R&D Knowledge Management & Documentation
R&D OPERATIONS & PORTFOLIO


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the R&D team's knowledge infrastructure — engineering documentation, architecture decision records, runbooks, onboarding materials, and institutional knowledge — preventing knowledge loss and accelerating team productivity.
  Poor documentation is the root cause of slow onboarding, repeated mistakes, and the 'key person dependency' problem — systematic knowledge management solves all three.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Documentation Quality Monitoring: Monitors the health of engineering and product documentation — identifies undocumented systems, outdated runbooks, missing architecture records, and knowledge silos. Prioritises gaps by risk (production systems without runbooks are critical).

  Layer 2 — Architecture Decision Records (ADRs): Manages the ADR process — every significant architectural decision is documented with context, decision, and consequences. Prevents revisiting decided questions and provides new engineers with decision rationale.

  Layer 3 — Runbook Management: Maintains operational runbooks — step-by-step procedures for all production operations, incident response, and disaster recovery. Tests runbooks regularly. Flags runbooks not updated in >90 days.

  Layer 4 — Onboarding Knowledge Base: Maintains a comprehensive engineering onboarding programme — environment setup, codebase orientation, architecture overview, key contacts, and first-sprint guidance. Updates with every new-hire feedback cycle.

  Layer 5 — Knowledge Search & Discovery: Provides AI-powered search across all R&D documentation — engineers can ask 'how does our payment processing work?' and get the relevant ADR, runbook, and code reference in one place.

ICP Qualification, Outbound Prospecting, Lead Scoring, Nurture Automation & ABM Signal Detection


01
Inbound Lead Capture & Qualification
LEAD MANAGEMENT & PROSPECTING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Captures leads from all inbound channels — web forms, email, chat, social, events — and instantly qualifies them against ICP (Ideal Customer Profile) criteria before any human touches the record.
  Assigns a lead score, routes to the right sales rep or nurture track, and logs the complete interaction history in CRM.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Multi-Channel Ingestion: Captures leads from website forms, chat transcripts, LinkedIn DMs, email enquiries, event registrations, and inbound calls. Normalises all into a standard lead record.

  Layer 2 — ICP Matching: Compares lead attributes (company size, industry, geography, role/title, technology stack) against the configured Ideal Customer Profile. Scores fit on each dimension.

  Layer 3 — Behavioural Scoring: Analyses engagement signals — pages visited, content downloaded, email opens, webinar attendance — and adds intent score to the fit score.

  Layer 4 — Duplicate & Enrichment Check: Cross-checks against existing CRM records for duplicates. Enriches lead with firmographic data (LinkedIn, Clearbit, ZoomInfo) to fill gaps.

  Layer 5 — Routing Decision: Combines fit score + intent score + lead source to determine routing — immediate sales assignment (hot), nurture sequence (warm), disqualify (poor fit), or partner referral.

02
Outbound Prospect Identification
LEAD MANAGEMENT & PROSPECTING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Autonomously identifies high-fit prospects matching the ICP from external databases, news signals, and intent data — building targeted outbound lists without manual research.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — ICP Translation: Converts the ICP definition into machine-readable filters — NAICS codes, employee range, revenue band, technology stack, geography, and funding stage.

  Layer 2 — Database Prospecting: Queries ZoomInfo, LinkedIn Sales Navigator, Lusha, or Apollo against ICP filters. Returns candidate company and contact lists with confidence scores.

  Layer 3 — Intent Signal Overlay: Enriches candidates with third-party intent data — companies researching relevant topics, attending industry events, hiring for roles indicating budget and need.

  Layer 4 — Trigger Event Detection: Scans news, LinkedIn, and funding databases for trigger events — new funding round, leadership change, M&A, product launch, geographic expansion, regulatory change.

  Layer 5 — List Scoring & Prioritisation: Ranks all prospects by combined ICP fit + intent + trigger event recency. Outputs a prioritised, de-duplicated outbound list with contact details.

03
Lead Scoring & Prioritisation
LEAD MANAGEMENT & PROSPECTING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Continuously scores and re-scores every lead in the CRM based on fit, behaviour, and engagement — ensuring sales reps always work the highest-probability leads first.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Fit Scoring (Static): Evaluates firmographic and demographic fit against ICP — industry, company size, revenue, geography, technology stack, decision-maker seniority.

  Layer 2 — Behavioural Scoring (Dynamic): Tracks every engagement action — email open (+5), link click (+10), page visit (+3), demo request (+40), pricing page view (+20), content download (+15).

  Layer 3 — Score Decay: Reduces score over time for inactive leads — if no engagement in 30 days, score decays 10% per week. Prevents stale leads appearing high-priority.

  Layer 4 — Negative Scoring: Subtracts points for disqualifying signals — wrong industry (-30), student email domain (-50), competitor domain (-40), unsubscribe (-30).

  Layer 5 — MQL Threshold & Alert: When score crosses the MQL threshold (configurable, e.g., 70), triggers immediate Sales alert and CRM status change from Lead to MQL.

Score, status, and reason visible to Sales rep

04
Lead Nurture Sequence Automation
LEAD MANAGEMENT & PROSPECTING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Enrols leads in personalised, multi-touch nurture sequences based on persona, industry, and stage — adapting content and cadence based on engagement responses in real time.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Persona Classification: Classifies lead by persona (CTO, CFO, COO, Procurement Head, etc.) and industry vertical. Selects the appropriate nurture track with relevant content.

  Layer 2 — Content Selection: Matches persona + stage + industry to the best available content assets — case studies, whitepapers, ROI calculators, webinars, product demos.

  Layer 3 — Cadence Optimisation: Determines send timing using AI — optimal days/times based on historical engagement data for this persona/industry. Avoids send fatigue.

  Layer 4 — Engagement Adaptation: If lead engages with email 1, next email goes deeper on the same topic. If lead ignores 3 emails, cadence slows and topic changes. If lead unsubscribes, sequence stops.

  Layer 5 — Sequence Exit Triggers: Exits lead from sequence on MQL conversion, direct sales contact, meeting booked, or account-level activity suggesting the deal is active.

05
Account-Based Marketing (ABM) Signal Detection
LEAD MANAGEMENT & PROSPECTING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Monitors target accounts for buying signals — digital behaviour, news events, personnel changes, technology changes — and alerts the account team when a target is showing in-market intent.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Target Account Monitoring: Watches a defined list of high-priority target accounts across web, news, social, and job boards. Monitors employee activity, company news, and technology signals.

  Layer 2 — Intent Signal Classification: Classifies detected signals by type — Research Intent (visiting competitor/category pages), Hiring Intent (job postings for relevant roles), Funding Intent (new investment), Event Intent (conference registration).

  Layer 3 — Signal Scoring: Weights signals by intent strength — Demo request (100), Pricing research (80), Competitor comparison (70), Relevant job posting (40), Funding news (30).

  Layer 4 — Account Spike Detection: Identifies when signal volume for an account spikes above its rolling baseline — suggesting an active buying cycle has started.

  Layer 5 — Account Team Alert: Generates a personalised alert for the AE and CSM — what signals were detected, which contacts showed activity, recommended next action.

06
Personalised Outreach Generation
SALES DEVELOPMENT (SDR)


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Generates highly personalised outreach messages for SDRs — email, LinkedIn, call scripts — using prospect research, company news, and pain-point mapping, eliminating generic templates.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Prospect Research: Reads the prospect's LinkedIn profile, company website, recent news, job postings, and CRM history. Builds a prospect intelligence brief.

  Layer 2 — Pain Point Mapping: Maps the prospect's role, industry, and company signals to known pain points in SequelString's ICP database — what problems this persona typically faces.

  Layer 3 — Trigger Hook Identification: Identifies the most relevant outreach hook — recent funding, new product launch, leadership change, hiring surge, a relevant news story.

  Layer 4 — Message Drafting: Generates a personalised first-line that references the specific hook, connects it to a relevant pain point, and delivers a crisp value proposition — not a generic pitch.

  Layer 5 — Multi-Channel Variants: Creates variants for email (subject line + body), LinkedIn connection request, LinkedIn InMail, and call opener — all from the same research base.

07
Outreach Sequence & Cadence Management
SALES DEVELOPMENT (SDR)


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages multi-touch SDR outreach sequences — tracking touchpoint delivery, monitoring responses, adapting cadence based on engagement, and flagging hot prospects for immediate follow-up.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Sequence Enrolment: Enrols prospects in the appropriate outreach sequence based on persona, industry, and lead source. Sequences have 6-12 touchpoints across email, LinkedIn, and phone.

  Layer 2 — Touchpoint Scheduling: Calculates optimal timing for each touchpoint — day of week, time of day — based on historical reply rates for this persona/industry/geography.

  Layer 3 — Response Detection: Monitors all outbound channels for prospect responses — positive, neutral, objection, unsubscribe, or out-of-office. Classifies and routes each response type.

  Layer 4 — Meeting Detection: Detects when a prospect has booked a meeting (calendar link click, reply confirming meeting). Immediately removes from sequence; updates CRM; notifies AE.

  Layer 5 — Sequence Analytics: Tracks performance of each step — open rate, reply rate, meeting booked rate. Flags underperforming steps for SDR manager review.

Meeting Scheduling & Confirmation
SALES DEVELOPMENT (SDR)


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Autonomously manages the back-and-forth of meeting scheduling — offering availability, confirming times, sending calendar invites, reminders, and pre-meeting prep packs to all parties.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Availability Reading: Reads AE/SDR calendar availability in real time. Identifies 3-5 optimal meeting slots that avoid conflicts, back-to-backs, and focus time blocks.

  Layer 2 — Slot Presentation: Presents availability to the prospect via a personalised scheduling link or embedded email options — single-click booking without email back-and-forth.

  Layer 3 — Confirmation & Calendar: On slot selection, automatically creates the meeting in all parties' calendars. Sends confirmation emails with agenda, dial-in/video link, and location.

  Layer 4 — Reminder Sequence: Sends automated reminders — 24 hours before (email), 1 hour before (email + SMS/WhatsApp). Detects if prospect opens reminder — flags if not, enabling human follow-up.

  Layer 5 — Pre-Meeting Brief: Assembles a pre-meeting intelligence brief for the AE — prospect background, company news, LinkedIn profile, previous interactions, and suggested talking points.

CRM Data Hygiene & Update Automation
SALES DEVELOPMENT (SDR)


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Keeps CRM data accurate and current by automatically updating contact details, enriching records, detecting stale data, and capturing activity from email and calendar — without SDR manual entry.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Email & Calendar Sync: Reads all sales emails and calendar events. Automatically logs calls, emails, and meetings in CRM against the correct contact and deal record.

  Layer 2 — Contact Enrichment: Periodically re-enriches all CRM contacts against LinkedIn, Clearbit, and ZoomInfo. Updates job titles, phone numbers, email addresses, and company details automatically.

  Layer 3 — Stale Data Detection: Identifies contacts with no recent activity, bounced emails, departed employees, or mismatched job titles. Flags or auto-updates where confidence is high.

  Layer 4 — Duplicate Detection: Continuously scans for duplicate records — same person/company under different entries. Merges duplicates using confidence-scored matching.

  Layer 5 — Deal Stage Consistency: Validates that deal stages in CRM match the actual activity evidence — a deal cannot be in 'Proposal Sent' if no proposal document is attached.

OPP   Opportunity Management
Use Cases 10-14
Win Probability, Next Best Action, Proposal Generation, Deal Risk & Competitive Intelligence


10
Opportunity Scoring & Win Probability
OPPORTUNITY MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Assigns a real-time win probability to every open opportunity using deal characteristics, engagement patterns, competitive signals, and historical win/loss data — replacing gut-feel forecasting.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Deal Attribute Analysis: Evaluates static deal factors — deal size, product fit, decision-maker seniority, procurement process complexity, competitive landscape, geographic fit.

  Layer 2 — Engagement Velocity: Measures how fast the deal is moving — frequency of meetings, email response speed, stakeholder expansion, document sharing, legal/procurement involvement.

  Layer 3 — CRM Activity Analysis: Reads all logged activities — notes, emails, calls — and uses NLP to detect positive signals (budget confirmed, timeline shared) and negative signals (ghosting, scope reduction).

  Layer 4 — Historical Pattern Matching: Matches current deal profile to historical won and lost deals with similar characteristics. Applies win rate from comparable deals.

  Layer 5 — Risk Factor Detection: Identifies specific risk flags — no executive sponsor, single-threaded relationship, procurement involved but no budget confirmed, competitor shortlisted.

Next Best Action Recommendation
OPPORTUNITY MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  For every open opportunity, recommends the highest-impact next action — who to contact, what to say, what content to share, what obstacle to address — based on deal stage and signals.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Stage Assessment: Reads current deal stage and maps it to the ideal sales motion at this stage — discovery, qualification, technical validation, commercial negotiation, procurement.

  Layer 2 — Gap Analysis: Identifies what is missing to progress to the next stage — economic buyer not engaged, security review not started, ROI business case not shared, legal terms not reviewed.

  Layer 3 — Stakeholder Mapping: Analyses who has been engaged and who has not. Identifies key stakeholders the AE has not yet met — champions, blockers, economic buyers.

  Layer 4 — Content Matching: Matches the deal stage and stakeholder to the most relevant content asset — customer case study (same industry), ROI calculator, technical documentation, security questionnaire.

  Layer 5 — Action Prioritisation: Ranks all possible next actions by expected impact on win probability. Presents top 3 recommended actions with rationale.

Deal Risk & Stall Detection
OPPORTUNITY MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Continuously monitors all open deals for stall signals and risk factors — alerting AEs and managers before a deal goes cold, and suggesting specific interventions.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Activity Monitoring: Tracks the time since last activity on every deal — email, call, meeting, document share. Flags deals with no activity beyond configurable thresholds.

  Layer 2 — Stakeholder Engagement Decay: Monitors whether key contacts are becoming less responsive — reply time increasing, opening emails but not responding, meeting attendance declining.

  Layer 3 — Stage Duration Analysis: Compares how long the deal has been in the current stage vs. the average time to progress. Flags deals stuck longer than 1.5x average stage duration.

  Layer 4 — Competitor Risk Detection: Scans meeting notes and emails for competitor mentions, RFP language suggesting competitive bids, or customer requests for feature comparison.

  Layer 5 — Escalation Recommendation: Based on risk profile, recommends the appropriate intervention — executive sponsor outreach, pricing review, technical proof of concept, competitive response.

Competitive Intelligence Battlecard Delivery

WHAT THE AGENT DOES

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Competitor Detection: Scans CRM notes, emails, and call transcripts for competitor mentions — direct names, euphemisms ('the other vendor'), RFP comparison requests.

  Layer 2 — Competitor Identification: Identifies which competitor(s) are present. Checks the competitive intelligence database for the latest battlecard and win/loss data.

  Layer 3 — Positioning Selection: Selects the relevant competitive positioning based on the customer's primary evaluation criteria — if they're focused on price, surfaces the value/TCO argument; if on features, surfaces the differentiation points.

  Layer 4 — Objection Handling: Retrieves the top anticipated objections this customer is likely to raise based on deal stage and competitor, along with proven responses from won deals.

  Layer 5 — Reference Customer Matching: Identifies a customer reference in the same industry who chose SequelString over this specific competitor — most powerful competitive proof point.

FCST   Sales Forecasting
Use Cases 15-17
Pipeline Health, Quota Attainment & Revenue Recognition


15
Pipeline Health & Forecast Generation
SALES FORECASTING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Generates a data-driven revenue forecast by analysing every deal in the pipeline — applying win probabilities, stage weights, and expected close dates to produce a bottom-up forecast with confidence intervals.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Pipeline Aggregation: Consolidates all open opportunities across all reps, territories, and segments. Validates deal stage, amount, and close date currency.

  Layer 2 — Win Probability Application: Applies AI-computed win probabilities (from UC10) to each deal — rather than using stage-based flat percentages, uses deal-specific, evidence-based probabilities.

  Layer 3 — Close Date Reliability: Assesses close date credibility — has the close date slipped before? Is there procurement/legal activity confirming the timeline? Adjusts expected timing accordingly.

  Layer 4 — Forecast Category Override: Compares AI forecast vs. rep-submitted forecast (Commit/Best Case/Pipeline). Flags significant discrepancies — rep over-committing or under-reporting.

  Layer 5 — Confidence Interval: Generates a forecast range — base case, upside, and downside scenarios — based on deal probability distribution and historical forecast accuracy.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

01
Data Aggregation Agent

Current amounts, stages, and close dates verified
02

AI win probability applied per deal
Evidence-based probability replaces stage percentage
03
Close Date Agent
Close date reliability scored
Timeline adjustments applied for slipped or unconfirmed dates
04
Forecast Reconciliation Agent
AI forecast vs. rep commit compared
Discrepancies flagged for manager review
05
Scenario Agent
Base/upside/downside scenarios computed
Forecast range with confidence intervals generated
06
Trend Agent
Historical forecast accuracy applied
Calibration factor applied based on past accuracy
07
Report Agent

CRO/Sales VP dashboard updated; board report prepared

Quota Attainment & Performance Tracking
SALES FORECASTING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Tracks every rep's real-time quota attainment, pipeline coverage, and activity metrics — identifying who is on track, who is at risk, and what specific coaching actions will help.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Quota Loading: Loads each rep's quota by month, quarter, and year. Applies ramp factors for new hires. Handles territory and product-line quota splits.

  Layer 2 — Attainment Calculation: Computes real-time attainment — closed won deals / quota. Tracks against monthly, quarterly, and annual targets simultaneously.

  Layer 3 — Pipeline Coverage Analysis: Computes pipeline coverage ratio (total pipeline / remaining quota). Flags reps with < 3x coverage as at risk of missing target.

  Layer 4 — Activity Benchmarking: Compares each rep's activity metrics (calls, emails, meetings, demos) against team benchmarks and against their own historical best performance.

  Layer 5 — Coaching Alert: For reps below target with low pipeline coverage and below-benchmark activity, generates specific, data-backed coaching recommendations for the manager.

SALES FORECASTING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Monitors signed contracts and delivery milestones to identify when revenue recognition criteria are met — bridging the gap between Sales close and Finance revenue booking.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Contract Analysis: Reads signed contracts to extract revenue recognition terms — delivery milestones, acceptance criteria, subscription start dates, and multi-element arrangement allocation.

  Layer 2 — Delivery Milestone Tracking: Monitors project/delivery system for milestone completion — Go-Live, customer acceptance sign-off, module delivery, SLA commencement.

  Layer 3 — Ind AS 115 / IFRS 15 Check: Validates recognition against accounting standard criteria — performance obligation identified, transaction price allocated, control transferred.

  Layer 4 — Finance Alert: When recognition criteria are met, alerts Finance team to book revenue. Provides all supporting documentation — contract, delivery confirmation, customer acceptance.

  Layer 5 — Deferred Revenue Monitoring: Tracks unrecognised contract value as deferred revenue. Updates deferred revenue schedule on each recognition event.

CSR   Customer Success & Retention
Use Cases 18-22
Health Scoring, Renewal/Upsell, Churn Prediction, QBR Prep & Sentiment Analysis


18
Customer Health Scoring
CUSTOMER SUCCESS & RETENTION


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Continuously computes a health score for every customer account — using product usage, support activity, engagement, sentiment, and commercial signals — identifying at-risk accounts before they churn.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Product Usage Analysis: Reads product usage data — login frequency, feature adoption, active users vs. licensed users, last login date. Low usage is the strongest early churn predictor.

  Layer 2 — Support Ticket Analysis: Monitors open tickets, resolution times, CSAT scores, and ticket volume trends. Spike in tickets or declining CSAT = health risk.

  Layer 3 — Engagement Monitoring: Tracks customer engagement with CSM — meeting frequency, QBR attendance, response rates, event participation, training completion.

  Layer 4 — Commercial Signal Analysis: Monitors renewal proximity, unpaid invoices, scope reduction requests, reduction in POs, and stakeholder changes (champion departure).

  Layer 5 — NPS/CSAT Trend: Analyses survey responses and sentiment from emails/calls using NLP — declining sentiment even before formal survey scores signal emerging risk.

19
Renewal Risk & Upsell Opportunity Detection
CUSTOMER SUCCESS & RETENTION


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Identifies which accounts are at risk of non-renewal AND which are ready for upsell/cross-sell — prioritising CS team effort on the highest-impact accounts in both directions.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Renewal Calendar: Tracks all subscription and contract renewal dates. Alerts CSM team at 180, 90, 60, and 30 days before renewal with account health context.

  Layer 2 — Churn Risk Assessment: Applies health score, usage trends, and sentiment to assess churn probability. Distinguishes between at-risk-of-churn and likely-to-renew accounts.

  Layer 3 — Upsell Signal Detection: Identifies upsell readiness signals — high usage of current modules, requests for features in higher tiers, growth in headcount, new business units, geographic expansion.

  Layer 4 — Revenue Impact Calculation: Computes the ARR at risk (churn) and ARR opportunity (upsell) for each account — enabling CS team to prioritise effort by revenue impact.

  Layer 5 — Action Plan Generation: For each account, generates a recommended action plan — for at-risk accounts: retention actions; for upsell-ready: expansion conversation starter.

20
Churn Prediction & Intervention
CUSTOMER SUCCESS & RETENTION


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Predicts which customers are likely to churn in the next 60-90 days — with enough lead time to intervene — and generates a specific, personalised retention playbook for each at-risk account.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Churn Model: Applies a machine learning churn prediction model trained on historical churned and retained accounts. Features include usage trends, ticket patterns, payment behaviour, and engagement decay.

  Layer 2 — Churn Driver Identification: For each predicted churn account, identifies the primary churn driver — product gaps, pricing, poor adoption, competitor, champion departure, business change.

  Layer 3 — Intervention Playbook: Matches churn driver to proven intervention playbooks — if product gap, trigger roadmap conversation; if adoption, trigger training programme; if pricing, trigger commercial review.

  Layer 4 — Executive Escalation Threshold: For accounts above ARR threshold or strategic importance, automatically escalates to VP CS or CEO for personal outreach.

  Layer 5 — Intervention Tracking: Tracks whether the recommended intervention was executed and whether health score improved. Closes the loop on retention effectiveness.

QBR (Quarterly Business Review) Preparation
CUSTOMER SUCCESS & RETENTION


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Account Data Aggregation: Pulls all account data for the quarter — usage statistics, feature adoption, user counts, support tickets, and uptime/SLA performance.

  Layer 2 — ROI & Value Realisation: Computes the customer's ROI from SequelString — time saved, process efficiency gains, cost reduction — using agreed KPI metrics from the success plan.

  Layer 3 — Goal Progress Review: Checks progress against the goals set at the start of the engagement or last QBR. Summarises hits, misses, and adjusted targets.

  Layer 4 — Product Roadmap Alignment: Matches the customer's feature requests and pain points against the upcoming product roadmap. Surfaces upcoming features relevant to this customer.

  Layer 5 — Next Quarter Planning: Generates recommended next quarter goals, expansion opportunities, and success metrics — ready for the customer conversation.

Customer Sentiment Analysis
CUSTOMER SUCCESS & RETENTION


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Continuously analyses all customer communications — emails, support tickets, call transcripts, survey responses — to detect emerging sentiment changes before they manifest as formal complaints or churn.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Communication Ingestion: Reads all customer-facing communications — support tickets, email threads, chat transcripts, NPS survey verbatims, LinkedIn mentions, review platforms.

  Layer 2 — Sentiment Classification: Applies NLP sentiment analysis to each communication — positive, neutral, frustrated, urgent, or disappointed. Tracks trend over time per account.

  Layer 3 — Topic Extraction: Identifies the specific topics driving sentiment — product reliability, support quality, pricing, missing features, onboarding experience. Enables targeted response.

  Layer 4 — Trend Detection: Monitors for sentiment trend changes — account that was positive for 6 months going neutral is an early warning even before it goes negative.

  Layer 5 — Alert & Escalation: Negative sentiment from a key contact triggers CSM alert. Escalating frustration on multiple channels triggers management escalation.

Sales Territory & Account Assignment
SALES OPERATIONS


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Optimises territory design and account assignment — ensuring equitable quota distribution, minimising travel overlap, and assigning accounts to the best-fit rep based on industry expertise and relationships.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Account Profiling: Reads all accounts — ARR potential, industry, geography, relationship history, and existing rep relationships. Profiles each account for assignment optimisation.

  Layer 2 — Rep Capability Matching: Profiles each rep — industry experience, win rates by segment, existing relationships, language capabilities, and current capacity.

  Layer 3 — Territory Balancing: Runs optimisation algorithm to balance territories by total addressable revenue, account count, and geographic proximity — ensuring fair and achievable quota distribution.

  Layer 4 — Conflict Detection: Identifies territory conflicts — accounts that span multiple regions, existing relationships that cross territory boundaries, strategic accounts that need special handling.

  Layer 5 — Change Impact Analysis: When territory changes are proposed, models the revenue impact — accounts moving between reps, relationship disruption risk, and transition plan needs.

Sales Commission Calculation & Validation
SALES OPERATIONS


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Automatically calculates sales commissions for every rep based on closed deals, commission plan rules, accelerators, clawback conditions, and special SPIFs — with full audit trail.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Deal Closure Confirmation: Reads closed-won deals from CRM. Validates against Finance system — contract signed, invoice raised, payment terms confirmed.

  Layer 2 — Commission Plan Application: Applies each rep's commission plan — base rate, product mix rates, new logo vs. expansion rates, multi-year deal credits, and partner/co-sell splits.

  Layer 3 — Accelerator Computation: Calculates accelerators triggered by quota attainment levels — at 100% attainment, 1.5x multiplier; at 125%, 2x multiplier. Applied correctly per plan rules.

  Layer 4 — Clawback Monitoring: Tracks deals subject to clawback conditions — customer cancellation within warranty period, payment default. Auto-triggers clawback deduction from future commission.

  Layer 5 — Dispute Resolution: When reps dispute commission, agent retrieves full calculation evidence — deal record, plan document, approval history — for transparent resolution.

Sales Methodology Adherence Monitoring
SALES OPERATIONS


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Monitors whether sales reps are following the defined sales methodology (MEDDIC, SPIN, Challenger, etc.) — coaching in real time where adherence is low and correlation with win rate is strong.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Methodology Framework Loading: Loads the sales methodology criteria — for MEDDIC: Metrics, Economic Buyer, Decision Criteria, Decision Process, Identify Pain, Champion. Maps to CRM fields.

  Layer 2 — CRM Completeness Check: For each active deal, checks whether all methodology fields are populated with substantive data — not just placeholders or generic text.

  Layer 3 — Quality Scoring: Reads the content of completed fields using NLP. Scores quality — a MEDDIC 'Pain' field that says 'needs automation' scores lower than a specific, quantified pain statement.

  Layer 4 — Win Rate Correlation: Computes the correlation between methodology adherence scores and win rates across historical deals. Identifies which criteria most predict winning.

  Layer 5 — Coaching Nudge: For deals with low adherence on high-correlation criteria, surfaces a specific coaching question or task — 'Have you confirmed the economic buyer? Add to CRM.'

Contract Lifecycle Management (CLM) Automation
SALES OPERATIONS


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Manages the full contract lifecycle from initial draft through redline, legal review, approval, execution, and renewal — reducing contract cycle time and legal bottlenecks.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Contract Generation: Pulls deal parameters from CRM and auto-generates the contract from approved templates — MSA, SOW, SaaS subscription agreement — with correct terms for deal type and geography.

  Layer 2 — Redline Analysis: When the customer returns a redlined contract, the agent analyses changes — categorises each redline as Standard, Non-standard, or Rejected clause — and highlights for legal review.

  Layer 3 — Pre-approved Clause Library: Matches customer redlines against the pre-approved clause library. Auto-accepts standard alternative language; routes non-standard clauses to legal.

  Layer 4 — Approval Routing: Routes contract for internal approval — based on deal value, non-standard clauses, geography, and customer type — to the right approval authority.

  Layer 5 — eSignature & Execution: Sends for eSignature via DocuSign/Adobe Sign. Monitors signing status. On execution, extracts key terms and posts to CLM system and CRM.

Executive Relationship Mapping

WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Maps the stakeholder landscape in every key account — identifying champions, blockers, economic buyers, and white-space contacts — and tracks relationship health at each level.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 4 — White Space Detection: Identifies important stakeholders the AE has not yet engaged — heads of departments that use or could use the product, executives above the current point of contact.

  Layer 5 — Relationship Risk Alert: Flags relationship risks — champion departure, key contact going silent, economic buyer change, new executive joining who is unknown to SequelString.

ACCOUNT MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Aggregates all customer feedback signals — NPS, CSAT, support tickets, call transcripts, QBR notes, and email sentiment — and synthesises actionable insights for product, sales, and leadership.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Feedback Ingestion: Collects all structured and unstructured customer feedback from NPS surveys, CSAT ratings, support transcripts, QBR notes, sales call recordings, and email threads.

  Layer 2 — Theme Extraction: Uses NLP topic modelling to extract recurring themes across all feedback — what customers love, what frustrates them, what they want that they don't have.

  Layer 3 — Segmentation: Breaks themes down by customer segment — industry, company size, product tier, geography — to identify whether issues are universal or segment-specific.

  Layer 4 — Trend Analysis: Tracks theme frequency over time — is a complaint getting more common? Is a positive theme growing? Distinguishes structural issues from one-off feedback.

  Layer 5 — Action Routing: Routes insights to the right team — product feedback to Product Management, pricing feedback to Sales Ops, support quality to CS Ops, feature requests to Product Roadmap.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

Structured and unstructured feedback collected
02

Top themes by frequency and sentiment identified
03
Segmentation Agent

Segment-specific vs. universal issues identified
04

05
Severity Agent

Outcome:  Product roadmap driven by data, not loudest voice. Customer-reported issues addressed 40% faster. NPS improved as customers see feedback acted on.


31
Account Plan Generation
ACCOUNT MANAGEMENT


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Generates a data-driven, personalised account plan for each strategic account — covering growth strategy, relationship map, competitive threats, and a 12-month action plan.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Account Intelligence Assembly: Pulls together all account data — ARR, product usage, health score, relationship map, support history, decision-maker profiles, and strategic context.

  Layer 2 — Growth Opportunity Mapping: Identifies all expansion opportunities — white space products, new business units, geographic expansion — and quantifies the addressable ARR.

  Layer 3 — Competitive Threat Assessment: Scans for competitive threats in the account — competitor trials, alternative solutions in place, dissatisfaction signals that competitors could exploit.

  Layer 4 — Relationship Gap Analysis: Maps current relationship vs. ideal relationship — who should be engaged that isn't, which stakeholders need stronger connections.

  Layer 5 — 12-Month Action Plan: Generates a specific, time-bound action plan — milestones, owner, expected outcomes — covering relationship development, expansion plays, and retention activities.

Use Cases 32-36

32
Win/Loss Analysis
SALES ANALYTICS & REPORTING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Automatically analyses every closed deal — won and lost — to identify the patterns, differentiators, and risk factors that determine outcomes, feeding insights back to sales, product, and marketing.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Deal Data Assembly: Pulls all data from closed deals — deal attributes, activity history, competitive landscape, methodology adherence score, rep, segment, and close date.

  Layer 2 — Win/Loss Survey Integration: Reads post-sale customer surveys and automated win/loss interview data. NLP extracts key reasons stated by buyer.

  Layer 3 — Pattern Recognition: Runs statistical analysis to identify which factors most correlate with winning vs. losing — deal size, sales cycle length, number of stakeholders, demo to proposal ratio.

  Layer 4 — Competitor Analysis: Breaks down win/loss by competitor — win rate against each competitor, common loss reasons per competitor, most effective counter-strategies.

  Layer 5 — Insight Distribution: Routes insights to the right teams — pricing feedback to Finance, product gaps to Product, competitive analysis to Marketing, sales process issues to Sales Ops.

33
Sales Pipeline Analytics
SALES ANALYTICS & REPORTING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Provides real-time, multi-dimensional pipeline analytics — by stage, rep, segment, product, and region — with predictive insights on pipeline sufficiency, velocity, and conversion rates.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Pipeline Snapshot: Captures the full pipeline state — all open deals by stage, amount, expected close date, rep, and segment. Updated in real time from CRM.

  Layer 2 — Velocity Metrics: Computes pipeline velocity — average deal size x win rate x number of opportunities / average sales cycle length. Tracks trend over time.

  Layer 3 — Stage Conversion Analysis: Measures conversion rates between every pipeline stage — and identifies where deals are leaking most. High drop-off between stages indicates process or skill gap.

  Layer 4 — Coverage Analysis: Computes pipeline coverage ratio by rep, team, and company. Flags where coverage is insufficient to hit target — at the right specificity to take action.

  Layer 5 — Comparative Benchmarking: Benchmarks current pipeline metrics against same period last year, company target, and top-performer benchmarks — contextualising the data.

Improving or declining trends identified

Gap to benchmark quantified per metric
07

VP Sales and CRO real-time view updated

34
Sales Activity & Productivity Tracking
SALES ANALYTICS & REPORTING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Tracks sales rep activity against benchmarks — calls made, emails sent, meetings held, demos delivered, proposals submitted — and correlates activity level with pipeline and quota outcomes.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Activity Capture: Reads all sales activities from email, calendar, CRM, and dialler — logs every outbound email, call, meeting, demo, and proposal automatically, without rep manual entry.

  Layer 2 — Benchmark Setting: Computes the team's top-quartile activity benchmarks for each metric — calls/day, emails/day, meetings/week, demos/week. Updates benchmarks quarterly.

  Layer 3 — Activity-Outcome Correlation: Computes the correlation between activity types and revenue outcomes. Identifies which activities most drive pipeline creation and deal closing.

  Layer 4 — Leading Indicator Monitoring: Monitors leading indicators that predict quota attainment 4-6 weeks ahead — pipeline adds, demo completion rate, proposal submission rate.

  Layer 5 — Productivity Insights: Identifies time wasters — reps spending excessive time on administrative tasks, excessive back-and-forth on scheduling, time spent on low-score leads.

Outcome:  Rep manual CRM entry eliminated. Coaching conversations backed by data, not intuition. Leading indicator tracking prevents quota miss surprises.


35
Revenue Attribution & Campaign ROI
SALES ANALYTICS & REPORTING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Tracks the contribution of every marketing campaign, channel, and content asset to pipeline and revenue — enabling data-driven marketing spend decisions and sales-marketing alignment.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Touch Point Capture: Logs every marketing and sales touch point on every deal — first touch (how the lead was generated), last touch (what drove the final conversion), and all intermediate touches.

  Layer 2 — Attribution Modelling: Applies multiple attribution models — first touch, last touch, linear, time-decay, and data-driven — to distribute revenue credit across touch points.

  Layer 3 — Campaign Performance: Computes pipeline generated, pipeline converted to revenue, and ROI for every marketing campaign, channel, and content asset.

  Layer 4 — Channel Comparison: Ranks all channels by cost-per-lead, cost-per-opportunity, and cost-per-closed-deal — enabling budget reallocation to highest-performing channels.

  Layer 5 — Budget Recommendation: Based on attribution data, recommends marketing budget reallocation — increase spend on high-ROI channels, reduce on low-ROI channels.

Content Agent

36
Sales Compensation Plan Optimisation
SALES ANALYTICS & REPORTING


WHAT THE AGENT DOES

  Analyses the effectiveness of the current sales compensation plan — identifying whether it is driving desired behaviours, rewarding the right deals, and motivating the right reps — and recommends plan adjustments.

REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS

  Layer 1 — Plan Performance Analysis: Maps current commission plan rules against actual rep behaviour — are reps chasing the deals the plan incentivises? Are there unintended gaming behaviours?

  Layer 2 — Behaviour Correlation: Correlates commission plan changes (new rates, new SPIFs) with changes in rep behaviour — did a multi-year deal SPIF actually drive more multi-year deals?

  Layer 3 — Equity Analysis: Analyses whether the plan is equitable across territories — are reps in high-potential territories benefiting disproportionately? Are there windfall earners vs. under-earners for equal effort?

  Layer 4 — Competitive Benchmarking: Benchmarks compensation plan against market data for similar roles in similar companies — assessing whether the plan is competitive for retention and hiring.

  Layer 5 — Plan Design Recommendations: Generates specific, data-backed recommendations for the next plan period — rate adjustments, new accelerators, SPIF design, and cap removal suggestions.

AGENT FLOW — STEP BY STEP

01
Plan Audit Agent
Current plan rules mapped to rep behaviour
Behaviour-plan alignment analysis performed
02
Correlation Agent
Plan changes vs. behaviour changes measured
Plan change effectiveness quantified
03
Equity Agent
Territory fairness analysis run
Windfall earners and under-earners identified
04
Retention Agent
Comp competitiveness assessed
At-risk of departure reps identified (under-market comp)
05
Benchmark Agent
Market comp data applied
Plan vs. market percentile computed
06
Recommendation Agent

Specific changes with expected impact quantified
07
Simulation Agent
Proposed plan modelled on current deals
Financial impact of proposed changes estimated

Outcome:  Sales comp plan designed for outcomes, not activity. Gaming behaviours eliminated. Rep retention improved. Revenue per comp dollar improved 18%.

01
Market Strategy & Intelligence Agent
SALES EXCELLENCE
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Provides comprehensive market intelligence including market sizing, growth trends, competitive landscape, regulatory changes, and strategic opportunities using AI-powered analysis of market data, industry reports, and competitive intelligence.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — Market Data Aggregation: Collects market data from multiple sources including industry reports (Gartner, Forrester, IDC), financial databases, government statistics, trade associations, and news sources. Aggregates data on market size, growth rates, segmentation, and trends.
Layer 2 — Competitive Landscape Mapping: Identifies key competitors, market share distribution, competitive positioning, and market dynamics. Analyzes competitor strategies, product offerings, pricing models, and go-to-market approaches. Creates competitive landscape visualization.
Layer 3 — Market Opportunity Analysis: Identifies high-growth segments, underserved markets, emerging opportunities, and white space. Quantifies market opportunity size, accessibility, and strategic fit. Prioritizes opportunities by potential revenue and strategic importance.
Layer 4 — Regulatory & Compliance Intelligence: Monitors regulatory changes, compliance requirements, industry standards, and policy developments impacting target markets. Assesses regulatory barriers, compliance costs, and policy risks.
Layer 5 — Strategic Insights Generation: Synthesizes market intelligence into actionable strategic insights including market entry strategies, positioning recommendations, pricing strategies, and partnership opportunities. Generates executive briefings and market entry plans.

WHAT THE AGENT DOES

Layer 1 — Competitor Intelligence Gathering: Monitors competitor websites, product announcements, pricing changes, customer reviews, social media, job postings, and financial reports. Tracks competitor product features, roadmaps, partnerships, and strategic moves.
Layer 2 — Product Feature Comparison: Compares product features, capabilities, technical specifications, and performance metrics across competitors. Identifies feature gaps, unique differentiators, and areas of parity. Creates detailed feature comparison matrices.
Layer 3 — Pricing & Packaging Intelligence: Analyzes competitor pricing models, packaging options, discount structures, and total cost of ownership. Identifies pricing sweet spots, common objections, and value-based differentiation opportunities.
Layer 4 — Win/Loss Theme Analysis: Analyzes historical win/loss data to identify patterns of why deals are won or lost against specific competitors. Identifies successful positioning strategies, common competitor weaknesses, and effective rebuttals.
Layer 5 — Battle Card Creation & Maintenance: Generates dynamic battle cards with competitor overview, strengths/weaknesses, product comparison, pricing intelligence, common objections, and proven responses. Updates battle cards based on new intelligence and win/loss learnings.

Layer 2 — Stakeholder & Decision Maker Identification: Identifies key decision makers, influencers, champions, and economic buyers. Maps organizational structure, reporting relationships, and decision-making processes. Profiles stakeholder backgrounds, career history, and interests.
Layer 3 — Technology Stack & Digital Footprint: Identifies technology stack using BuiltWith, Datanyze, and similar tools. Analyzes digital footprint including website technologies, cloud platforms, marketing tools, and IT infrastructure. Assesses technology maturity and modernization initiatives.
Layer 4 — Business Priority & Initiative Analysis: Extracts business priorities from earnings calls, press releases, job postings, and strategic announcements. Identifies key initiatives, transformation programs, pain points, and investment areas. Aligns solution value to business priorities.
Layer 5 — Relationship Mapping & Engagement History: Maps existing relationships, past interactions, deal history, and engagement patterns. Identifies warm introductions, mutual connections, and relationship leverage opportunities. Tracks account health and satisfaction indicators.

21
RFP Response Automation Agent
PRESALES & SOLUTION ENGINEERING
WHAT THE AGENT DOES
Automates RFP/RFI response process including requirement extraction, content matching, response generation, compliance checking, and assembly of complete RFP responses using AI and content libraries reducing response time by 70%.
REASONING LOGIC — LAYERS
Layer 1 — RFP Intake & Parsing: Automatically extracts RFP requirements from various formats (PDF, Word, Excel) using AI-powered document parsing. Identifies all questions, requirements, evaluation criteria, submission guidelines, and deadlines. Structures requirements into categorized, actionable items.
Layer 2 — Content Library & Knowledge Base: Maintains comprehensive content library including standard responses, technical descriptions, company information, certifications, case studies, and past proposals. Uses semantic search and AI matching to find relevant content for each requirement.
Layer 3 — Automated Response Generation: Generates draft responses by matching requirements to content library using NLP and semantic similarity. Customizes responses based on customer industry, size, and specific requirements. Ensures response consistency and message alignment across sections.
Layer 4 — Compliance & Completeness Checking: Validates response completeness ensuring all mandatory requirements addressed, page limits respected, and formatting guidelines followed. Checks for compliance with submission requirements, file formats, and naming conventions.
Layer 5 — Collaboration & Review Workflow: Orchestrates collaboration across stakeholders (sales, technical, legal, finance) with task assignment, progress tracking, and automated reminders. Maintains version control and consolidates inputs into final response.
